title: fix(workflows): Make action created_at/updated_at optional in Zod schema
body: ## Problem A [customer reported](https://posthoghelp.zendesk.com/agent/tickets/53868) that their workflow's Trigger and Exit blocks show yellow exclamation marks indicating they need further configuration, but there is no indication in the configuration panel of what's wrong. The issue affects any workflow where the trigger or exit actions were stored without `created_at`/`updated_at` fields in the actions JSON. The backend DRF serializer treats these fields as optional (`required=False`), but the frontend Zod schema required them, causing a silent validation failure with no user-facing error message. ## Changes - Made `created_at` and `updated_at` optional in the `HogFlowActionSchema` Zod schema (`z.number().optional()`), aligning with the backend serializer's `required=False` contract - Added regression tests that verify trigger, exit, and function actions validate successfully without these fields ### Screenshots **Before:** **After:** ## How did you test this code? - Reproduced the issue locally by inserting a workflow with missing timestamps on trigger/exit actions directly into the DB, confirmed yellow badges appeared - Verified the fix removes the false validation errors - Unit tests: parameterized tests covering trigger, exit, and function actions without `created_at`/`updated_at` ## Publish to changelog? No
author: dmarchuk

commits.message: fix(workflows): Make action created_at/updated_at optional in Zod schema

title: ci(storybook): fetch update-snapshots label live so re-runs respect it
body:
<!-- mendral:session:slack:TSS5W8YQZ:C09G8QA6740:1774375634.277809 -->
## Summary

- Replaces the static `github.event.pull_request.labels` check (baked into the event payload at trigger time) with a live `gh api` call to fetch current labels at runtime
- This means adding the `update-snapshots` label and hitting **Re-run** now works as expected — no need to push an empty commit

## Before

```yaml
HAS_UPDATE_LABEL: ${{ contains(github.event.pull_request.labels.*.name, 'update-snapshots') }}
```
GitHub evaluates this once when the workflow is first triggered. Re-runs reuse the original event payload, so labels added after the initial trigger are never seen.

## After

```bash
gh api repos/${{ github.repository }}/issues/${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}/labels \
  --jq '.[].name' | grep -q '^update-snapshots$'
```
Fetches the current label state from the API at the time the `changes` job runs, so re-runs always see the latest labels.

## Security

No new attack surface — fork PRs are still blocked unconditionally before this check, and the label only controls whether PNG snapshots get committed to the PR branch.


---

> [!NOTE]
> Created by [Mendral](https://mendral.com). Tag @mendral-app with feedback or questions.
>
> - [View implementation session](https://app.mendral.com/sessions/slack:TSS5W8YQZ:C09G8QA6740:1774375634.277809)
> - Triggered by [this Slack message](https://posthog.slack.com/archives/C09G8QA6740/p1774391093750299?thread_ts=1774375634.277809&cid=C09G8QA6740)
author: mendral-app

reviews.body: ## Pull request overview Updates the Storybook CI workflow so “update-snapshots” label detection is evaluated at runtime (via GitHub API) rather than relying on the original pull_request event payload, allowing workflow re-runs to respect labels added after the initial trigger. **Changes:** - Replace `${{ contains(github.event.pull_request.labels.*.name, ...) }}` with a live `gh api` label lookup at job runtime. - Authenticate the GH CLI call using `GH_TOKEN: ${{ github.token }}`. --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.

title: feat(flags): verify evaluation_metadata presence in flags cache
body:
## Problem

The flags HyperCache recently gained `evaluation_metadata` (dependency stages, missing deps, transitive deps), but the verification task (`verify_team_flags`) doesn't check for its presence. Cache entries written before this field was added silently lack it, leaving the Rust flags service without dependency information.

I recently ran `python manage.py warm_flags_cache` in prod-us and prod-eu, so I'm pretty confident every cache entry is good. But this would give me 100% confidence.

## Changes

- Add an `evaluation_metadata` presence check in `verify_team_flags` that returns a `MISSING_EVALUATION_METADATA` mismatch, triggering the existing auto-fix path to rebuild the entry
- Add a test that simulates a pre-`evaluation_metadata` cache entry and verifies detection

## How did you test this code?

- New test `test_verify_detects_missing_evaluation_metadata` covers the detection path
- All 21 existing verify-related tests pass without regressions

## Publish to changelog?

no
author: haacked

reviews.body: ## Pull request overview Adds a verification safeguard to the feature flags HyperCache so legacy cache entries (written before `evaluation_metadata` existed) are detected and rebuilt, ensuring the Rust flags service always receives dependency metadata. **Changes:** - Add a `MISSING_EVALUATION_METADATA` mismatch path in `verify_team_flags` when cached entries lack `evaluation_metadata`. - Add a regression test that simulates a legacy cache payload missing `evaluation_metadata` and asserts the verifier detects it. ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 2 out of 2 changed files in this pull request and generated 1 comment. | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | `posthog/models/feature_flag/flags_cache.py` | Adds verifier mismatch detection for missing `evaluation_metadata` in cached flag payloads. | | `posthog/models/feature_flag/test/test_flags_cache.py` | Adds a test that removes `evaluation_metadata` from a warmed cache entry and verifies mismatch detection. | --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.

## Pull request overview Copilot reviewed 2 out of 2 changed files in this pull request and generated no new comments. --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.

feat(flags): verify evaluation_metadata presence in flags cache

Add a check to verify_team_flags to ensure cached entries contain evaluation_metadata.
Entries written before this field was added to the HyperCache will now be detected
as mismatches and rebuilt with the required dependency information for the Rust service.
fix(flags): use assert for mypy type narrowing in test

fix(flags): handle None cached_data in verification check

Fixes potential TypeError when cache returns the empty value marker.
When get_from_cache_with_source returns (None, "redis"), we now
report a mismatch instead of crashing on the "in" operator.

title: test(storybook): update Search snapshot
body:
<!-- mendral:session:action:01KMDZFHVKFFG4XC64D0A4ACXP -->
## Summary

- Removes stale `components-search--default--light.png` and `components-search--default--dark.png` snapshots so CI regenerates them with the correct dimensions (632×994 instead of 632×1024)

## Background

PR #51959 (`feat(visual-review): Gate visual review behind feature flag`) gated the Visual Review product behind `FEATURE_FLAGS.VISUAL_REVIEW`. In Storybook (where no feature flags are active), this removes Visual Review from `getTreeItemsProducts()` output, causing the Search Default story to render one fewer item — 30px shorter.

The stored snapshots still reflected the old 632×1024 layout, causing `Components/Search › Default › smoke-test` to fail on master (shard 11/16) with:
> `Expected image to be the same size as the snapshot (632x1024), but was different (632x994)`

Deleting the stale snapshots triggers CI to write fresh ones at the correct size in UPDATE mode.

Closes insight: Storybook snapshot drift: Search Default height mismatch after Visual Review feature flag

title: fix(insights): map HogQL left()/right() to leftUTF8()/rightUTF8()
body:
## Problem

Error tracking issue: https://us.posthog.com/project/2/error_tracking/019ceb15-7b38-7263-9683-4722c1cf0d59

Users with non-ASCII breakdown values (e.g. Cyrillic, CJK) hit a `TypeError: sequence item 0: expected str instance, bytes found` in trends queries when the breakdown value exceeds `BREAKDOWN_VALUE_MAX_LENGTH` (400).

**Root cause:** ClickHouse's `left()` function operates on **bytes**, not UTF-8 characters. When the 400-byte truncation lands in the middle of a multi-byte character (e.g. Cyrillic chars are 2 bytes each), the result is invalid UTF-8. The `clickhouse-driver` can't decode invalid UTF-8 and returns Python `bytes` instead of `str`, which then causes a `TypeError` when `_format_breakdown_label` tries to `"::".join(labels)`.

63 occurrences today across 9 users, active since 2026-03-14.

## Changes

FYI - I made a potentially controversial change to the hogql function mapping here, I remapped left to leftUTF8 rather than adding leftUTF8 as its own thing.

I did this because I'm worried about leaving this footgun lying around, but it's different behaviour to what we've done for leftPad. Ultimately I'm easy either way as long as we can fix this query.

AI PR summary continues:

- Map HogQL's `left()` → ClickHouse's `leftUTF8()` and `right()` → `rightUTF8()` in the function registry. This is correct since HogQL should always handle strings as UTF-8 text, not raw bytes.
- Add regression test that creates a 401-char Cyrillic string (802 bytes) to trigger truncation at the character boundary.
- Update all affected snapshots.

## How did you test this code?

I'm an agent — no manual testing was performed. Automated testing:

- Verified `left('text', N)` returns `bytes` when truncation splits a multi-byte UTF-8 character, while `leftUTF8()` correctly returns `str`
- Regression test `test_trends_breakdown_with_multibyte_value_at_truncation_boundary` fails without the fix, passes with it
- All existing snapshot tests pass with updated snapshots

## Publish to changelog?

No

## Docs update

N/A

## 🤖 LLM context

Authored by Claude Code (Opus 4.6). The key debugging insight was that ClickHouse's `left()` is byte-based — confirmed by running `SELECT left('Нам', 3)` which returns `b'\xd0\x9d\xd0'` (3 bytes, invalid UTF-8) vs `leftUTF8('Нам', 3)` which returns `'Нам'` (3 characters).
author: robbie-c

reviews.body: This is probably fine... though I'm curious about what might happen to existing queries. Hopefully nothing substantial.

commits.message:
fix(insights): map HogQL left()/right() to leftUTF8()/rightUTF8() to prevent invalid UTF-8 truncation

ClickHouse's `left()` operates on bytes, not UTF-8 characters. When
BREAKDOWN_VALUE_MAX_LENGTH (400) truncation landed in the middle of a
multi-byte character (e.g. Cyrillic = 2 bytes each), the result was
invalid UTF-8. The clickhouse-driver can't decode invalid UTF-8 and
returns `bytes` instead of `str`, causing TypeError in
`_format_breakdown_label` when joining breakdown labels.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: fix(flags): set FLAGS_REDIS_URL in feature-flags service startup
body:
## Problem

The `feature-flags` case in `bin/start-rust-service` explicitly sets every env var the
service needs — except `FLAGS_REDIS_URL`. It works under flox (which sets the var in
`manifest.toml`), but fails when the script is run standalone without flox. Without this
var, local dev doesn't match our prod setup, where a dedicated Redis instance is used for
flags.

## Changes

Add `FLAGS_REDIS_URL` with a default of `redis://localhost:6379/1` to the `feature-flags`
block, matching the value already in the flox manifest and `docker-compose.dev.yml`. Uses
`${FLAGS_REDIS_URL:-…}` so an existing value (e.g., from flox) is preserved.

This better simulates our production setup, where the flags service uses a dedicated Redis
instance separate from the shared one.

title: feat(llma): auto-load more generations to fill sentiment tab
body:
## Problem

The sentiment tab fetches 200 generations and classifies them, but after applying polarity filters and intensity thresholds, users often see only 5-10 cards — a sparse, underwhelming page despite burning 200 sentiment API calls.

## Changes

Auto-fill logic that progressively loads more generations until the tab has at least 50 visible cards (grouped/deduped), capped at 3 extra rounds (800 generations max total).

- After each batch of sentiment classifications finishes (`stillAnalyzing` goes false), checks `groupedSentimentCards.length`
- If < 50 visible cards and more data exists, automatically calls `loadMoreGenerations()` to fetch the next page
- Caps at `MAX_AUTO_LOAD_ROUNDS = 3` to prevent runaway API calls
- Resets the round counter on fresh loads (filter/date changes)
- Defers the "empty state" analytics event until auto-loading is exhausted, avoiding noisy intermediate captures

## How did you test this code?

Code review only — the change is small and self-contained within the existing `stillAnalyzing` subscription flow. The auto-load loop naturally terminates via `hasMore` or the round cap.

## Publish to changelog?

No
author: andrewm4894

commits.message: feat(llma): auto-load more generations to fill sentiment tab with 50 cards
fix(llma): move autoLoadRounds to kea reducer for instance isolation

title: fix(storybook): don't remove data-stable in debounce effect to prevent waitForSelector timeout
body:
<!-- mendral:session:action:01KM4KKCBMQ02GNZVC8PY9NDPG -->
## Summary

Fixes the `[data-attr=paths-viz][data-stable]` timeout in UserPaths Storybook stories (shard 14/16).

### Root cause

PR #51347 introduced a `data-stable` attribute on the paths canvas element to signal when the SVG has finished rendering. The debounce effect in `Paths.tsx` was removing `data-stable` **on every resize event**, even before the SVG was actually being recreated:

```typescript
// OLD: removed data-stable immediately on every resize
useEffect(() => {
    if (canvasRef.current) {
        canvasRef.current.removeAttribute('data-stable')  // ← problem
    }
    const timer = setTimeout(() => {
        setCanvasWidth(...)
        setCanvasHeight(...)
    }, CANVAS_RESIZE_DEBOUNCE_MS)
    return () => clearTimeout(timer)
}, [rawCanvasWidth, rawCanvasHeight])
```

The Storybook test runner (`test-runner.ts`) dispatches resize events during `postVisit` (via `resizeViewportAndWait` and the explicit `window.dispatchEvent(new Event('resize'))` call). Each resize event triggered the debounce effect, which removed `data-stable` for the entire 50ms debounce window. Playwright's `waitForSelector('[data-attr=paths-viz][data-stable]')` would then time out because `data-stable` was never stably present.

For `UserPathsEditViewports` (3 viewport widths), the context timeout is `10000ms × 3 = 30000ms`, matching the exact "Timeout 30000ms exceeded" error seen in the CI logs.

### Fix

Remove the premature `removeAttribute('data-stable')` from the debounce effect. The render effect already correctly removes `data-stable` only when the SVG is actually being recreated (synchronously, taking milliseconds), then re-adds it immediately after. The debounce effect's only job is to debounce dimension updates — it doesn't need to touch `data-stable`.

With this fix, `data-stable` remains present during resize events (when no SVG recreation is happening), and is only briefly absent during the actual SVG recreation.

### Why intermittent?

The failure was intermittent because it depended on the timing of resize events relative to the debounce timer. In fast CI environments, the debounce could settle before `waitForSelector` ran. In slower environments, continuous resize events kept resetting the debounce timer, preventing `data-stable` from ever appearing.

Relates to insight: Storybook UserPaths stories still failing on master — timeout waiting for `[data-attr=paths-viz][data-stable]` after stabilization fix merged


---

> [!NOTE]
> Created by [Mendral](https://mendral.com). Tag @mendral-app with feedback or questions.
>
> - [View implementation session](https://app.mendral.com/sessions/action:01KM4KKCBMQ02GNZVC8PY9NDPG)
> - Addresses [this insight](https://app.mendral.com/insights/01KKVY921NQT5RT61MX2XTSRBM)
author: mendral-app

reviews.body: ## Pull request overview Fixes intermittent Storybook/Playwright timeouts in the UserPaths stories by ensuring the `data-stable` attribute on the paths canvas isn’t cleared during resize debouncing, so `[data-attr=paths-viz][data-stable]` remains reliably present except during actual SVG recreation. **Changes:** - Stop clearing `data-stable` in the resize debounce effect. - Add an explanatory comment clarifying that `data-stable` is only toggled during real SVG recreation (render effect), avoiding `waitForSelector` flakiness. --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.

commits.message:
fix(storybook): don't remove data-stable in debounce effect to prevent waitForSelector timeout

The debounce effect was removing data-stable on every resize event, causing
Playwright's waitForSelector('[data-attr=paths-viz][data-stable]') to time out
in UserPaths stories. The render effect already removes data-stable only when
the SVG is actually being recreated, so the premature removal in the debounce
effect was unnecessary and harmful.

title: feat: Expand OAuth COOP middleware to support additional OAuth flows
body:
## Problem

OAuth popup flows were failing because Django's SecurityMiddleware sets Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy to "same-origin" by default, which breaks the `window.opener` reference that popup-based authentication flows rely on to communicate completion status back to the parent window.

## Changes

- Renamed `ToolbarOAuthCoopMiddleware` to `OAuthCoopMiddleware` to reflect its broader scope
- Expanded the middleware to handle all OAuth-related paths instead of just toolbar and Vercel flows
- Added support for additional OAuth endpoints: `/oauth/`, `/login/vercel/`, `/api/agentic/authorize`, and `/api/agentic/oauth/`
- Refactored path checking logic to use a centralized list of OAuth path prefixes for better maintainability

## How did you test this code?

As an agent, I have not manually tested this code. The changes maintain the existing middleware pattern and expand coverage to additional OAuth endpoints. Manual testing should verify that popup-based OAuth flows work correctly on all the specified paths and that the Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy header is properly set to "unsafe-none" for these endpoints.

## Publish to changelog?

No

## Docs update

No documentation update needed for this internal middleware change.
author: rafaeelaudibert

title: ci: skip migration SQL comment for import-only changes
body:
## Problem

The CI migration SQL checker posts SQL output for every changed migration file, including modifications to existing migrations (e.g., import path updates, formatting fixes). This creates noise on PRs that don't add new database migrations.

This change was inspired by [this migration change comment on this PR](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/pull/51513#issuecomment-4084919765)

## Changes

- Compare each changed migration file against the PR's base branch using `git cat-file -e` to determine if it's new or a modification to an existing file
- Only run `sqlmigrate` and post SQL output for genuinely new migrations
- Clean up stale SQL comments when all changed migration files already exist on the base branch (e.g., a previous push added a migration but a force-push removed it)
- Use GitHub Actions `::warning::` annotations instead of plain `echo` for fetch failures, making them visible in the PR checks UI
- Add a defensive guard for empty `BASE_SHA`

## How did you test this code?

Simulated the key logic paths locally:
- `git cat-file -e` correctly identifies existing migrations vs non-existent ones
- Empty `BASE_SHA` guard triggers the warning
- `git fetch` failure fallback works (all migrations treated as new, preserving previous behavior)
- `--no-tags --prune` fetch flags work correctly

No automated tests (inline CI bash script). Will verify end-to-end by observing CI on a PR that touches migration files.

## Publish to changelog?

No
author: haacked

reviews.body: ## Pull request overview Reduces noise from the CI “migration SQL checker” by only posting `sqlmigrate` output for migrations that are newly added in the PR (vs edits to already-existing migration files), and cleans up previously-posted SQL comments when appropriate. **Changes:** - Adds base-SHA comparison (`git cat-file -e`) to classify changed migration files as new vs existing. - Runs `sqlmigrate` / posts SQL output only for new migration files; deletes stale SQL comments when no new migrations remain. - Adds `::warning::` annotations for base-SHA/fetch issues to improve visibility in GitHub Actions UI. --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.

commits.message:
ci: skip migration SQL comment for import-only changes

Only post a migration SQL comment for new migrations that don't exist on
the base branch. This prevents false positives when an existing migration
file is modified to update an import path or similar non-operational
changes. When all changed migrations already exist on the base branch,
clean up any stale SQL comment from a previous push.

ci: improve migration checker observability and robustness

Add empty BASE_SHA guard and use GitHub Actions annotations (::warning::) for fetch failures instead of plain echo. Update git fetch flags to align with codebase convention (--no-tags --prune). Remove stderr suppression to preserve helpful diagnostic info in CI logs.
ci: fail hard when BASE_SHA is empty in migration checker
ci: warn instead of hard-fail when BASE_SHA is empty in migration checker

body: ## Problem Prompt names are immutable after creation, so there's no way to rename a prompt or fork an existing one without manually recreating it. Users and MCP agents need a simple way to duplicate a prompt under a new name. ## Changes **Backend:** - Add `POST /api/environments/{team_id}/llm_prompts/name/{prompt_name}/duplicate/` endpoint that copies the latest version's content to a new prompt at version 1 - Add `LLMPromptDuplicateSerializer` with `new_name` field validation (same rules as create: alphanumeric, hyphens, underscores) - Add `duplicate_prompt` service function with conflict detection **MCP:** - Add `prompt-duplicate` MCP tool definition and generated handler - Regenerate OpenAPI types and MCP tool schemas **Frontend:** - Add "Duplicate" button to the prompt list dropdown menu (between View and Archive) - Opens a form dialog with the new name pre-filled as `{name}-copy` - Client-side validation for name format - Navigates to the new prompt on success - Add `api.llmPrompts.duplicateByName()` API method - Add `duplicatePrompt` action in `llmPromptsLogic` ## How did you test this code? - 7 new backend tests covering: happy path, 404 for missing source, 400 for name conflict, name format validation, reserved name rejection, source unaffected after duplicate, and archived name reuse - 2 new MCP tool tests covering schema validation and handler wiring - All 50 backend tests pass, all 247 MCP tests pass ## Publish to changelog? title: fix(personhog-replica): rm test before acquire
body:
## Problem

The `personhog_replica_db_pool_acquire_duration_ms` p99 metric shows a ~5ms baseline with frequent spikes to 30ms+. Every `pool.acquire()` call sends a health-check ping through PgBouncer to Postgres before returning the connection, adding a full network roundtrip to every single query.

With `test_before_acquire: true`, sqlx pings every connection before returning it from the pool. The ~5ms baseline *is* that ping roundtrip through PgBouncer. The 30ms+ spikes are variance in that roundtrip (PgBouncer server-connection reassignment, replica replay pressure, etc.).

Since PgBouncer runs in transaction mode, it reassigns backend Postgres connections between transactions anyway — the client connection (sqlx → PgBouncer) stays alive regardless. The ping isn't protecting against stale connections, it's just adding latency. This is confirmed by pool saturation sitting near 0% and connection churn being flat (connections aren't going stale).

## Changes

Disable `test_before_acquire` on the personhog-replica database pool. This is safe because:

- PgBouncer in transaction mode means the sqlx → PgBouncer connection doesn't silently close
- The personhog-router already has retry logic (max 3 retries with backoff) that handles transient connection errors
- `is_transient_error` in `common/database` classifies IO and connection errors as retryable

Pool acquisition time should drop from ~5ms baseline to near-zero, and the 30ms+ p99 spikes should disappear.

## How did you test this code?

Will monitor personhog-replica post deploy to see if we get the perf gains

title: chore(capture): measure throughput production to kafka
body:
## Problem

We recently had an incident where we exceeded the throughput of disk writes to Kafka. We currently have metrics for events/s being produced, but no visibility into bytes/s. These are meaningfully different — a customer with disproportionately large events can spike byte throughput without a corresponding spike in event count, making it hard to diagnose or anticipate capacity issues.

## Changes


Added a `capture_kafka_produce_bytes_total` counter in the Kafka sink, incremented by the serialized payload size (in bytes) on every event produced. The counter is labeled by `topic`, so throughput can be broken down per Kafka topic (e.g., `events_plugin_ingestion`, `session_recording_snapshot_item_overflow`).

In Grafana, `rate(capture_kafka_produce_bytes_total[5m])` gives bytes/s produced, complementing the existing `capture_events_ingested_total` (events/s).

## How did you test this code?


- Verified the crate compiles cleanly with `cargo check -p capture`
- The metric is a straightforward counter increment on an already-serialized payload — the `payload.len()` call is the exact byte count sent to Kafka
- Existing tests continue to pass; no behavioral changes to event routing or production logic

title: fix(llma): improve sentiment card text display and dedup grouping
body:
## Problem

Sentiment cards were showing only the last 200 characters of message text, making many distinct messages appear identical. This caused confusion about why "duplicate" cards weren't being grouped, when they were actually different messages that shared the same tail.

## Changes

- **Display**: Replaced tail-only truncation (last 200 chars) with start...end truncation (first 250 + last 250 chars) so users can identify messages at a glance
- **Expand**: Clicking a card now shows the full 2000-char classifier window (the actual text the sentiment model evaluated)
- **Dedup grouping**: Changed the grouping key from full raw text to the 2000-char classifier window, so messages that differ only in a prefix (e.g. varying system prompt headers) are correctly deduplicated

## How did you test this code?

Verified the truncation logic produces correct start...end output for texts of varying lengths. Confirmed the grouping key change aligns with the backend classifier window.

## Publish to changelog?

No

title: chore(personhog_client): more gRPC client metrics
body:
## Problem

The Django gRPC personhog client had limited observability — only request duration and a status-labeled request counter. When latency spikes occurred, we couldn't easily distinguish between:

- Timeout/deadline exceeded errors vs other failures
- Connection establishment problems (slow DNS, network issues between Django and the router)
- Channel instability (frequent reconnects, prolonged TRANSIENT_FAILURE states)

The existing `grpc.enable_retries` channel option was also a no-op (requires a service config to activate), so it was removed to avoid confusion.

## Changes

**Timeout tracking** (`interceptor.py`):
- Added `personhog_django_grpc_timeouts_total` counter that fires specifically on `DEADLINE_EXCEEDED`, in both the response-code and exception paths of `MetricsInterceptor`. This is a subset of the existing `requests_total{status="DEADLINE_EXCEEDED"}` but makes alerting and dashboard queries simpler.

**Channel connectivity monitoring** (`client.py`):
- Added `_ChannelStateMonitor` that subscribes to the raw gRPC channel's connectivity callbacks and emits:
  - `personhog_django_grpc_channel_state` — Enum gauge showing current state (IDLE, CONNECTING, READY, TRANSIENT_FAILURE, SHUTDOWN)
  - `personhog_django_grpc_channel_state_transitions_total` — Counter with `from_state`/`to_state` labels for every transition
  - `personhog_django_grpc_connection_establishment_seconds` — Histogram measuring CONNECTING→READY latency (TCP + HTTP/2 handshake time)
- The monitor subscribes to the raw channel (not the intercepted one) since interceptors don't affect connectivity state.
- Removed the no-op `grpc.enable_retries` channel option (retries are handled by the personhog-router, not this client).


## How did you test this code?

- Added `test_metrics_interceptor.py` (10 tests): verifies `MetricsInterceptor` returns/raises correctly for success, non-OK, and exception paths; verifies the timeout counter fires only on `DEADLINE_EXCEEDED` and not on other statuses or success.
- Added `test_channel_state_monitor.py` (6 tests): verifies state transitions are tracked, connection establishment latency is measured for CONNECTING→READY, latency is not recorded for TRANSIENT_FAILURE or READY-without-CONNECTING, reconnection cycles produce separate latency observations, and `close()` unsubscribes.
- All 16 new tests pass (`uv run pytest posthog/personhog_client/test_metrics_interceptor.py posthog/personhog_client/test_channel_state_monitor.py`).
- Existing interceptor tests (`test_interceptor.py`) are unaffected — they test `_with_metadata` and `ClientNameInterceptor`, neither of which changed.
author: nickbest-ph

title: chore(personhog): instrument connection level behavior
body:
## Problem

The personhog services (router and replica) only had request-level metrics (`grpc_server_requests_total`, `grpc_server_request_duration_ms`). We had no visibility into connection-level behavior or request concurrency, making it hard to diagnose issues like connection storms, pool backpressure, or in-flight request buildup.

## Changes

- **Connection tracking:** Added `TrackedTcpStream` wrapper and `tracked_tcp_incoming` helper that tracks active gRPC server connections via a `grpc_server_connections` gauge. Both services now bind their own `TcpListener` and use `serve_with_incoming_shutdown` instead of `serve_with_shutdown`.
- **In-flight request gauges:** `GrpcMetricsLayer` now emits `grpc_server_requests_in_flight` (per method) using a drop guard, so cancellations are always counted. The router additionally tracks `personhog_router_client_requests_in_flight` for outbound backend calls.
- **Moved gRPC metrics to `personhog-common`:** Relocated the `GrpcMetricsLayer` implementation from `personhog-common/src/middleware/grpc_metrics.rs` to `personhog-common/src/grpc.rs`, consolidating all personhog gRPC instrumentation in one module. Removed the now-empty `middleware` submodule.
- **Cleaned up `common-metrics`:** Removed gRPC-specific dependencies (`futures`, `http`, `pin-project`, `tonic`, `tower`) that were briefly added to `common-metrics` — those deps now live in `personhog-common` where the code moved.
- **Updated metrics reference:** Added the three new metrics and dashboard panels to `PERSONHOG_METRICS_REFERENCE.md`.

### New metrics

| Metric | Type | Labels | Description |
|--------|------|--------|-------------|
| `grpc_server_connections` | Gauge | `service` | Active TCP connections to the gRPC server |
| `grpc_server_requests_in_flight` | Gauge | `service`, `method` | Concurrent requests being processed |
| `personhog_router_client_requests_in_flight` | Gauge | `backend` | Concurrent outbound requests from router to backends |


## How did you test this code?

- `cargo check -p personhog-common -p personhog-replica -p personhog-router -p common-metrics` passes cleanly

## Publish to changelog?

<!-- For features only -->

<!-- If publishing, you must provide changelog details in the #changelog Slack channel. You will receive a follow-up PR comment or notification. -->

<!-- If not, write "no" or "do not publish to changelog" to explicitly opt-out of posting to #changelog. Removing this entire section will not prevent posting. -->

title: chore(ci): add timeout and partial clone to bundle size job
body:
## Problem

The frontend bundle size CI job can hang indefinitely (observed 35+ min) when `compressed-size-action` runs `git fetch -n origin master:master` on a degraded runner. The job has no timeout, so it burns runner minutes for up to 6 hours (GitHub Actions default).

## Changes

- Add `timeout-minutes: 10` to the `frontend-bundle-size` job (normal runtime is ~4 min)
- Add `filter: blob:none` to the checkout step so the action's internal `git fetch` only pulls commit/tree objects, not blobs — blobs are lazy-fetched on demand during `git reset --hard`, and most are already local from the PR checkout

## Problem

The feature flags system needs to support per-condition-set aggregation types to enable more flexible targeting. Currently, `aggregation_group_type_index` only exists at the flag level, but we need it at the condition set level to eventually support mixed person/group targeting within a single flag.

This PR adds the field to the Rust models as the first step in a stack of changes.

## Changes

- Add `aggregation_group_type_index: Option<i32>` field to `FlagPropertyGroup` struct with `#[serde(default)]` for backward-compatible deserialization

  - Fixes compile errors from the new required field
  - Future-proofs against additional field additions

title: fix(workflows): Don't send empty HTML body for plaintext emails
body:
## Problem

Customers sending plaintext-only emails via workflows receive blank emails. When the email editor's "Plain text" tab is used (setting `html: ""`), SES still receives an empty `Body.Html` field. SES wraps this in a `multipart/alternative` MIME message and injects its own tracking pixel into the HTML part. Email clients like Gmail prefer the HTML part per RFC 2046, rendering just a hidden 1x1 pixel - a blank email.

Confirmed via Gmail "Show original" on a production email - the `text/html` MIME part contains only an AWS tracking pixel.

## Changes

- Skip `Body.Html` in the SES request when `params.html` is empty, so SES sends a single-part `text/plain` email
- Same treatment for the maildev (local dev) path
- Added test asserting `Body.Html` is undefined for plaintext-only emails

## How did you test this code?

- Verified the root cause by inspecting raw MIME source of a production email in Gmail
- Sent a plaintext-only email via SES (dev account) with Body.Html omitted — Gmail source confirms single-part Content-Type: text/plain with no HTML part
- Added a unit test (should send plaintext-only email when html is empty) that mocks the SES client and asserts Body.Html is undefined

## Publish to changelog?

No

title: chore(personhog): better observability
body:
## Problem

The personhog-router → personhog-replica service is experiencing latency spikes, but current metrics are too coarse to isolate the root cause. The existing instrumentation measures end-to-end request time and total DB query duration, but can't distinguish between:

- Time waiting for a DB connection from the pool vs actual query execution
- Primary pool latency vs replica pool latency
- gRPC transport/serialization overhead between router and replica
- Connection pool pressure and saturation
- Error categories (connection failures vs pool exhaustion vs query errors)
- Query result cardinality issues

This makes it impossible to build a Grafana dashboard that pinpoints where latency is being introduced in the request path.


## Changes

**Pool identification** — Set `pool_name` on both `PoolConfig` instances (`"primary"` / `"replica"`) so the existing `db_connection_created_total` counter from common-database can distinguish connection churn per pool.

**Error type counters** — Added `personhog_replica_storage_errors_total` counter with `error_type` (connection/pool_exhausted/query) and `operation` labels in `log_and_convert_error`. Previously errors were only logged.

**Pool health gauges** — New `pool_monitor.rs` spawns a background task polling pool stats every 10s (configurable via `POOL_MONITOR_INTERVAL_SECS`), reporting `personhog_replica_db_pool_size`, `personhog_replica_db_pool_idle`, and `personhog_replica_db_pool_max` per pool.

**Pool acquisition timing** — Added `personhog_replica_db_pool_acquire_duration_ms` histogram. All storage methods now use an `acquire_timed()` helper that times just the `pool.acquire()` step, then execute queries on the acquired connection. This separates "waiting for a connection" from "executing the query." Also added a `pool` label (`"primary"` / `"replica"`) to the existing `personhog_replica_db_query_duration_ms` histogram.

**Rows returned tracking** — Added `personhog_replica_db_rows_returned` histogram with `operation` label on all batch/list queries. Detects cardinality issues (e.g., persons with thousands of distinct IDs).

**gRPC metrics layer on replica** — Added a `GrpcMetricsLayer` (Tower middleware, same pattern as personhog-router) recording `grpc_server_requests_total` and `grpc_server_request_duration_ms` on the replica service. This enables computing `(router backend duration) - (replica gRPC duration) = network/serialization overhead`.

### Resulting dashboard capabilities

| Panel | What it tells you |
|-------|-------------------|
| `router grpc_duration - router backend_duration` | Routing/proto marshal overhead |
| `router backend_duration - replica grpc_duration` | gRPC network/serialization overhead |
| `replica grpc_duration - db_query_duration` | Service-layer processing overhead |
| `db_pool_acquire_duration` | Time waiting for a DB connection |
| `db_query_duration - db_pool_acquire_duration` | Pure SQL execution time |
| `db_query_duration{pool=primary\|replica}` | Primary vs replica comparison |
| `(pool_size - pool_idle) / pool_max` | Pool saturation |
| `rate(db_connection_created_total)` | Connection churn |
| `storage_errors_total{error_type=...}` | Error breakdown |
| `db_rows_returned` | Large result set detection |


## How did you test this code?

<!-- Briefly describe the steps you took. -->
<!-- Include automated tests if possible, otherwise describe the manual testing routine. -->
<!-- If you are an agent writing this, do NOT include manual tasks you have NOT completed. You can clearly outline you're simply an agent and you haven't tested this manually except for code-based unit/integration tests -->

title: chore(devex): require `update-snapshots` label for snapshot auto-updates
body:
**Problem**
Snapshot auto-updates run on every human PR by default. When flaky snapshots update on unrelated changes, devs get confused by unexpected commits and noise in their PRs.

**Change**
Storybook and Playwright CI now default to check mode — snapshots must match or the test fails. To opt in to auto-updates, add the `update-snapshots` label and re-run CI (or just push a new commit with the label applied).

When tests fail in check mode, a PR comment explains the two options:
- Add label if the snapshot changes are intentional
- Ignore if flaky — wait for fixes on master

Only mendral-app[bot] still gets update mode automatically (fully automated, no human in the loop).

**Note:** the `update-snapshots` label needs to be created in the repo settings.

title: fix(frontend): forward HTML attributes in SceneContent to fix workflows E2E tests
body:
## Summary

- `SceneContent` only accepted `children` and `className`, silently dropping any other HTML attributes (including `data-attr`) passed to it
- PR #50123 added `data-attr="workflows-scene"` to `<SceneContent>` but the attribute was never forwarded to the underlying `<div>`, causing Playwright E2E tests to fail deterministically on master
- Fix: extend `SceneContentProps` to `extends React.HTMLAttributes<HTMLDivElement>` and spread `...rest` onto the inner `<div>`

Replaces #51256 (closed due to merge conflicts from stale snapshot commits — CI will regenerate snapshots).

title: fix(session-recordings): add tier to snapshot throttle scope for prom metrics
body:
## Changes

The `rate_limit_exceeded_total` Prometheus counter's `scope` label was always `snapshots_burst` or `snapshots_sustained` regardless of org tier, making it impossible to tell from Grafana/metrics which tier was hitting rate limits.

After `_apply_tier_rates` resolves the tier, `self.scope` is updated to include it — e.g. `snapshots_burst_free`, `snapshots_sustained_paid`, `snapshots_burst_enterprise`. This flows into both the Prometheus `scope` label and the DRF throttle cache key.

The cache key becoming tier-specific is intentional: different tiers have different rates and should have separate rate limit buckets.

For non-personal-API-key requests (session auth) where tier lookup is skipped, the scope is unchanged.


🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

title: chore: replace axum::async_trait re-export with direct async_trait import
body:
## Problem

Axum 0.8 removes the `async_trait` re-export. Several Rust crates in the workspace (`feature-flags`, `cymbal`, `batch-import-worker`, `kafka-deduplicator`) import `async_trait` via `axum::async_trait`, which will break on upgrade.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This PR doesn't upgrade us to Axum 0.8. It's just preparing us for such an upgrade in the future.

## Changes

- Replace all `use axum::async_trait` imports with `use async_trait::async_trait` across 31 `.rs` files
- Add `async-trait = { workspace = true }` to `cymbal` and `feature-flags` `Cargo.toml` (the other two crates already had it)
- Remove one redundant function-local import in `feature-flags/src/utils/test_utils.rs`

This is a no-op — axum 0.7 simply re-exports the same crate.

## How did you test this code?

`cargo check -p feature-flags` passes. The change is a mechanical import path substitution with no behavioral difference.

No publish to changelog.

title: fix(llma): upgrade labeling agent to gpt-5.4
body:
## Summary

- The previous PR (#50776) changed the labeling agent model from `gpt-5.2` to `gpt-5.3`, but `gpt-5.3` doesn't exist as an OpenAI model (they went from 5.2 → 5.4)
- This caused all labeling agent calls to silently fail, resulting in every cluster getting fallback "Label not generated by agent" labels since March 12
- Upgrades to `gpt-5.4` (OpenAI's latest frontier model) — modest cost increase ($1.75 → $2.50/1M input tokens, $14 → $15/1M output tokens) but 2.5x context window (400K → 1.05M)

## Changes

- Update `LABELING_AGENT_MODEL` from `gpt-5.3` to `gpt-5.4` in clustering constants
- Add `gpt-5.4` to `OpenAIConfig.SUPPORTED_MODELS` and `SUPPORTED_MODELS_WITH_THINKING`
- Add `llma_labeling` product to LLM gateway config with `gpt-5.4` allowlisted
- Update READMEs

## Test plan

- [ ] Verify cluster labeling works by triggering a manual clustering workflow for team 2
- [ ] Check Temporal worker logs for `cluster_labeling_agent_completed` (not `cluster_labeling_agent_error`)
- [ ] Confirm clusters in the UI show generated labels instead of "Label not generated by agent"

title: feat: track personal API key auth source on local evaluation endpoint
body:
## Problem

We have no visibility into how personal API keys are being passed to the local evaluation endpoint — whether via Authorization header, request body, or query string. This makes it hard to understand SDK usage patterns and plan deprecation of less secure methods.

## Changes

- Normalize source identifiers as class constants on `PersonalAPIKeyAuthentication` (`SOURCE_HEADER`, `SOURCE_BODY`, `SOURCE_QUERY_STRING`) instead of using ad-hoc strings
- Store `personal_api_key_source` on the authenticator instance after successful auth
- Add a `posthog_local_evaluation_personal_api_key_source_total` Prometheus counter in the local evaluation endpoint, labeled by `source` (`header`, `body`, `query_string`), incremented only when the request authenticated via personal API key
- Add parameterized tests for the new counter (header and body sources, plus a negative test for secret key auth)

title: chore(flags): remove unused String payload from PersonalApiKeyInvalid
body:
## Problem

`FlagError::PersonalApiKeyInvalid(String)` carried a `String` payload, but every call site passed the identical value `"Authorization header"`. The payload added no information and required unnecessary heap allocations on error paths.

The Rust port does not support auth tokens from anywhere except the Authorization header.

## Changes

- Convert `PersonalApiKeyInvalid(String)` to a unit variant `PersonalApiKeyInvalid`
- Hardcode the error detail message, aligned with the `#[error]` attribute
- Update all 4 call sites in `auth.rs`, match arms in `errors.rs`, and test cases in `errors.rs` and `canonical_log.rs`

Now consistent with sibling auth error variants (`SecretApiTokenInvalid`, `NoAuthenticationProvided`, etc.) which are already unit variants.

## How did you test this code?

- `cargo check -p feature-flags` passes
- All existing tests in the affected files remain valid (test instances updated from `PersonalApiKeyInvalid("test".to_string())` to `PersonalApiKeyInvalid`)

I'm an agent and have not tested this manually beyond code-based checks.

## Publish to changelog?

No

## Docs update

No docs changes needed.
author: haacked

title: chore(flags): remove PBKDF2 personal API key support from Rust service
body:
## Problem

The Rust feature-flags service supports three personal API key hashing modes (`SHA256`, `PBKDF2` with 260k iterations, `PBKDF2` with 390k iterations), trying each sequentially on every auth request. This adds unnecessary complexity and a slow-path dependency (`pbkdf2` crate) to a service that hasn't shipped yet.

Since Feb 2024, Django auto-upgrades any `PBKDF2` key to `SHA256` on use. Production data confirms zero `PBKDF2` keys have been used in 2+ years across US (~6,750 keys) and EU (~1,750 keys). Every actively-used key has already been upgraded.

## Changes

- Remove `hash_legacy_pbkdf2_key()` and multi-mode hashing logic from `auth.rs`
- Simplify `hash_personal_api_key()` to SHA256-only (now `pub(crate)` for reuse)
- Flatten `validate_personal_api_key_with_scopes_for_team()` from a 3-iteration loop to a single hash + query
- Remove `pbkdf2` crate dependency from `Cargo.toml`
- Remove `PERSONAL_API_KEY_HASH_MODE_COUNTER` metric (meaningless with one mode)
- Reuse `hash_personal_api_key()` in `test_utils.rs` instead of duplicating inline SHA256 hashing

## How did you test this code?

- `cargo check -p feature-flags` — compiles clean
- `cargo test -p feature-flags -- api::auth` — all 5 auth tests pass
- `cargo clippy -p feature-flags -- -D warnings` — zero warnings

## Publish to changelog?

No

## Docs update

N/A
author: haacked

title: fix(flags): check Redis before negative cache in ReadThroughCache
body:
## Problem

The `ReadThroughCache::get_or_load` method checks the negative cache before Redis. The negative cache exists to protect against repeated calls to the expensive loader function for keys known not to exist — not to protect Redis. This means a valid Redis entry could be blocked by a stale negative cache entry.

The scenario we want to avoid is a signal creates a cache entry, but a request comes in before the cache entry is ready. We don't want to block the next 5 minutes (or whatever) of requests because of the negative cache entry. The negative cache entry is more difficult to invalidate dynamically, so we use a TTL. Eventually, we may want to implement a proper approach to negative cache invalidation. But this should work for now.

## Changes

- Reorder cache lookups to Redis → negative cache → loader so a valid Redis entry is returned immediately
- Move negative cache check from `get_or_load` into `handle_cache_miss`, where it gates the loader across all error paths (miss, corruption, Redis-unavailable)
- Deduplicate `handle_true_cache_miss` and `handle_corrupted_cache` into a single `load_and_cache` method parameterized by `CacheSource` variants
- Include `CacheSource` in cache write failure warning messages for better diagnostics
- Fix `_key` parameter naming in `handle_redis_unavailable` (parameter was actually used)
- Add test verifying Redis hit takes priority over negative cache

## How did you test this code?

All 24 existing + new unit tests pass (`cargo test` in `rust/common/cache`). The new `test_redis_hit_takes_priority_over_negative_cache` test validates that when a key exists in both Redis and the negative cache, Redis wins.

## Publish to changelog?

no

## Docs update

N/A
author: haacked

title: fix(replay): mobile recordings show black screen
body:
## Problem

Mobile session recordings show a black screen during playback. This is a regression from removing the `REPLAY_SNAPSHOT_STORE` feature flag (#50211).

`SnapshotStore.canPlayAt()` requires a full snapshot before allowing events to be fed to rrweb, but mobile recordings don't have full snapshots in the raw data — they're synthesized later by `processAllSnapshots`. This causes `isWaitingForPlayableFullSnapshot` to stay true and the player to never receive events.

The `LoadingScheduler` is also affected — `findNearestFullSnapshot()` returns null, so the scheduler searches backward through every source trying to find a full snapshot that will never exist.

## Changes

- Add `syncFullSnapshotTimestamps()` to `SnapshotStore` — after `processAllSnapshots` runs, scans the processed result for full snapshot events and updates entries whose timestamps changed
- Call it from the coordinator's `processSnapshotsAsync` after processing completes, triggering `storeUpdated()` so `isWaitingForPlayableFullSnapshot` re-evaluates
- This fixes both `canPlayAt()` and the `LoadingScheduler`

## How did you test this code?

- Unit tests for `syncFullSnapshotTimestamps`: syncs synthetic timestamps, makes `canPlayAt` return true, no-op when unchanged, skips unloaded entries
- All existing SnapshotStore, coordinator, and snapshotDataLogic tests pass (55 tests)

Supersedes #50991

title: feat: Add host message logging and tool call testing to MCP debug app
body:
## Problem

The MCP debug app needs better debugging capabilities to help developers troubleshoot issues with host-app communication and tool calls. Currently, it's difficult to track message flow and test tool functionality in real-time.

## Changes

Enhanced the MCP debug app with comprehensive debugging features:

- **Host message logging**: Automatically captures and displays all messages received from the host with timestamps
- **Tool call testing**: Added a button to trigger the `debug-mcp-ui-apps` tool with unique timestamped messages
- **Tool call result tracking**: Shows the status, timing, and results of all tool calls with success/error indicators
- **Real-time notifications**: Visual indicators for new messages with auto-scroll functionality
- **Enhanced error logging**: Added console logging for errors in both AppWrapper and AppErrorState components
- **Improved UI organization**: Better structured debug information with collapsible sections and scrollable containers

The debug tab now provides a complete view of the communication flow between the app and host, making it much easier to diagnose issues during development.

## How did you test this code?

As an AI agent, I have not manually tested this code. The changes include:
- TypeScript type safety for all new interfaces and functions
- React hooks following proper patterns with useCallback and useEffect
- Event listener cleanup in useEffect return functions
- Proper state management for message tracking and tool call status
- Error handling for async tool calls with try/catch blocks

Manual testing should verify:
- Host messages are captured and displayed correctly
- Tool call button triggers the debug tool and shows results
- New message notifications work as expected
- Error states are properly logged and displayed

## Publish to changelog?

No - this is a development/debugging tool enhancement, not a user-facing feature.

## Docs update

No docs update needed for internal debugging tools.

title: fix(devex): pr-approval-agent tuning and label retention
body:
**CI no longer blocks.** Failing checks are not a hard gate anymore — CI is its own gate and the agent is often triggered before checks finish.

**Zero reviews → escalate.** If there are no review comments at all, the agent escalates. Authors should request reviews (Codex, Claude, Copilot, Greptile, or human) before stamphog.

**Faster on obvious cases.** Gate denials and T1a-trivial PRs use low effort + 3 max turns instead of the full 20-turn deep exploration.

**Label stays on approval.** Only removed on failure. Branch protection's "dismiss stale reviews on push" handles revocation for free.

**Misc.** Disable session persistence, plain-language denial reasons (no internal gate codes), CODEOWNERS-soft for team-devex.

title: chore(flags): read new holdout field in Rust
body:
## Summary

Second task of the `holdout_groups` → `holdout` migration #47931 . The Rust feature flag service now reads the new `holdout` field with a fallback to the legacy `holdout_groups` format.

Django (first task) already writes both formats when saving experiment holdouts. This PR makes Rust prefer the new format when present.

### How it works

- If `holdout` is present, then use `exclusion_percentage` and construct variant as `holdout-{id}`
- Else if `holdout_groups` is present → use the legacy path (unchanged)
- Else → no holdout

### Logic changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `rust/feature-flags/src/flags/flag_models.rs` | Added `Holdout` struct (`id`, `exclusion_percentage`) and `holdout: Option<Holdout>` field to `FlagFilters` |
| `rust/feature-flags/src/flags/flag_matching.rs` | Gate checks both fields; `is_holdout_condition_match` prefers new format with `else if` fallback to legacy (legacy path unchanged) |
| `rust/feature-flags/src/flags/test_flag_matching.rs` | Two new tests: new format only, and both formats present (new takes precedence) |

### Struct literal updates (it only added `holdout: None`)

- `rust/feature-flags/src/utils/test_utils.rs`
- `rust/feature-flags/src/utils/test_graph_utils.rs`
- `rust/feature-flags/src/flags/flag_matching_utils.rs`
- `rust/feature-flags/src/flags/flag_operations.rs`
- `rust/feature-flags/src/flags/flag_service.rs`
- `rust/feature-flags/src/flags/test_helpers.rs`
- `rust/feature-flags/src/handler/billing.rs`
- `rust/feature-flags/src/handler/tests.rs`

### Remaining phases

- **Task 3:** Django data migration to backfill all existing flags with the new `holdout` field (~18 rows)
- **Task 4:** Remove `holdout_groups` fallback from Rust and Django reading code
- **Task 5:** Stop writing `holdout_groups` in Django and clean up from existing flags

## Test plan

- [x] Existing `test_feature_flag_with_holdout_filter` passes (legacy format)
- [x] New `test_feature_flag_with_new_holdout_format` passes (new format only)
- [x] New `test_feature_flag_holdout_new_format_takes_precedence` passes (both present)
- [x] All 7 holdout-related tests pass

title: feat: add Expire command to Redis pipeline framework
body:
## Problem

The auth cache needs to set TTLs on reverse index sets to prevent unbounded memory growth. Currently there is no way to issue an `EXPIRE` command within a batched Redis pipeline round-trip — callers would need a separate network call, defeating the purpose of pipelining.

## Changes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These are all new addition to the library. No existing callers. No risk to existing services.

- Add `PipelineCommand::Expire { key, seconds }` variant and `Pipeline::expire()` builder method (`pipeline.rs`)
- Handle `EXPIRE` in real pipeline execution and result processing (`client.rs`)
- Handle `EXPIRE` in mock pipeline with configurable `expire_ret` map for per-key error injection (`mock.rs`)
- Update `test_pipeline_all_commands` to cover the new command

title: chore(flags): update local-only dependencies in feature-flags crate
body:
## Problem

The `feature-flags` Rust crate has several local-only dependencies that are multiple major versions behind. Keeping dependencies current reduces future upgrade burden and picks up bug fixes, performance improvements, and security patches.

## Changes

Updates five dependencies that are **scoped entirely to the `feature-flags` crate** — no other workspace crates are affected:

| Dependency | Old | New | Notable improvements |
|---|---|---|---|
| `json5` | 0.4 | **1.3** | Complete rewrite with UTF-16 surrogate pair fix, u128/i128 support |
| `petgraph` | 0.6.5 | **0.8** | `no_std` support, new algorithms (Dinic's max flow, bidirectional Dijkstra, Bron-Kerbosch cliques), dot file parsing |
| `strum` | 0.26 | **0.28** | `no_std` compatibility, automatic `#[default]` propagation for `EnumDiscriminants`, `#[automatically_derived]` on generated impls |
| `md5` | 0.7 | **0.8** | Patch-level update, same `compute()` API |
| `rstest` | 0.25 | **0.26** | Windows `#[files]` reliability, new `#[test_attr]` and `#[dirs]` attributes (dev-dependency only) |

These are all **local dependencies** declared directly in `rust/feature-flags/Cargo.toml`, not workspace-level deps. No other crates in the Rust workspace reference these versions, so there is no risk to other services.

## How did you test this code?

- `cargo check -p feature-flags` — compiles cleanly with zero warnings
- `cargo test -p feature-flags` — 911 tests pass. 8 pre-existing failures unrelated to this change (missing GeoIP database in worktree environment + flaky timing test)

## Publish to changelog?

No

title: chore(flags): refactor FlagsCanonicalLogLine and split evaluation counters
body:
## Problem

`FlagsCanonicalLogLine` mixes request-level metadata with per-flag evaluation counters in a single flat struct. `merge_rayon_delta` must explicitly list every evaluation counter field to merge rayon thread-local deltas back into the request log. If someone adds a new eval counter and writes it via `with_canonical_log` inside `evaluate_single_flag` but forgets to add it to `merge_rayon_delta`, the counter silently stays at zero, re-introducing a silent data loss bug for that field.

Closes https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/issues/49255

## Solution

Extract the 6 evaluation counter fields into an `EvalCounters` sub-struct embedded in `FlagsCanonicalLogLine` as `pub eval: EvalCounters`. The `merge` method on `EvalCounters` uses an exhaustive destructuring pattern (no `..`), so adding a new field to `EvalCounters` without a corresponding merge rule is a **compile error**.

`merge_rayon_delta` becomes a one-liner that delegates to `self.eval.merge(&delta.eval)`.

## Changes

**`handler/canonical_log.rs`**
- New `EvalCounters` struct with `flags_device_id_bucketing`, `cohorts_evaluated`, `property_cache_hits`, `property_cache_misses`, `person_properties_not_cached`, `group_properties_not_cached`.
- `EvalCounters::merge()` with exhaustive destructuring for compile-time safety.
- `FlagsCanonicalLogLine` embeds `pub eval: EvalCounters` replacing the 6 flat fields.
- `merge_rayon_delta` delegates to `self.eval.merge(&delta.eval)`.
- `emit()` references `self.eval.*` fields (log output field names unchanged).
- All tests updated to use `log.eval.*` paths and construct `EvalCounters` directly.

**`handler/mod.rs`**
- Re-export `EvalCounters` alongside `FlagsCanonicalLogLine`.

**`flags/flag_matching.rs`**
- All 6 `with_canonical_log` call sites updated from `log.field` to `log.eval.field`.

## How did you test this code?

- All 80 `canonical_log` unit tests pass (merge, isolation, rayon fallback, emit, error mapping, truncation).
- `cargo check` and `cargo clippy` clean.
- `cargo fmt` clean.
- No behavioral changes, log output field names are decoupled from struct field paths.

## Publish to changelog?

No
author: matheus-vb

title: fix(llma): add schedule_to_start_timeout for sentiment activity
body:
## Summary
- Adds a 30s `schedule_to_start_timeout` to the sentiment classification activity
- When all workers are busy with CPU-intensive ONNX inference, new activities queue indefinitely — the API hangs until the 120s workflow timeout
- With this change, if no worker picks up the activity within 30s, it fails fast so the user gets an error promptly instead of waiting minutes

## Test plan
- [ ] Run sentiment workflow when cluster is idle — should work as before
- [ ] During high load, verify activities fail with schedule-to-start timeout instead of hanging

title: fix(llm-gateway): always throttle against auth user
body:
## Problem

The LLM gateway was vulnerable to rate limit poisoning attacks where malicious users could inject victim user IDs through client-provided fields (`user` param, `standard_logging_object.end_user`, or `litellm_params.metadata.user_id`) to bill costs against other users' quotas or bypass rate limiting entirely.

## Changes

- Modified `_extract_end_user_id()` to always return the authenticated user's ID instead of trusting client-provided user identification
- Updated rate limiting logic to consistently use the authenticated user's ID for quota enforcement across both OAuth and API key authentication methods
- Added safeguard in `record_cost()` to prevent overriding existing end_user_id with client-supplied values
- Removed fallback logic that previously checked multiple client-controlled sources for user identification

## How did you test this code?

Added test coverage including:
- Unit tests verifying that all client-provided user fields are ignored in favor of authenticated user ID
- Poisoning prevention tests confirming attackers cannot bill costs to victim accounts
- Rate limiting tests ensuring users are properly limited by their own authenticated identity
- Context tests verifying end_user_id cannot be overridden once set from authentication

## Publish to changelog?
No

title: refactor: use parent + component pricing for services catalog
body:
## Problem

The services catalog listed each PostHog product as a separate top-level service with `paid` pricing. But PostHog projects include **all** products — you provision "PostHog" not "session_replay" individually. Rafa suggested using the APP spec's `component` pricing with `parent_service_ids` to model this hierarchy.

## Changes

- Added a parent `posthog` service with `pricing.type = "free"` — this is what users provision
- Changed each product service from `pricing.type = "paid"` to `pricing.type = "component"` with `parent_service_ids: ["posthog"]`
- Each component still carries its `stripe_price` ID so the orchestrator can display pricing info
- On billing fetch failure, returns just the parent service instead of empty list
- Updated `VALID_SERVICE_IDS` to include `"posthog"`

Context: [APP spec (v0.1d)](https://github.com/agentic-provisioning/posthog-spec), Slack: #project-posthog-stripe-apps

## How did you test this code?

- `pytest ee/api/agentic_provisioning/test/test_services.py -x -q` — 7 tests pass
- Tests cover: parent service present, component pricing structure, cache, billing failure fallback, exclusion of inclusion-only products

title: fix(replay): remove duplicate metric labels from Temporal interceptor
body:
## Summary

- The Temporal SDK's `workflow.metric_meter()` and `activity.metric_meter()` already include `workflow_type`, `activity_type`, `namespace`, and `task_queue` as default attributes (see [`_workflow_instance.py:1218`](https://github.com/temporalio/sdk-python/blob/main/temporalio/worker/_workflow_instance.py#L1218) and [`activity.py:234`](https://github.com/temporalio/sdk-python/blob/main/temporalio/activity.py#L234))
- Our `DeleteRecordingsMetricsInterceptor` was passing these same attributes again via `get_metric_meter({"workflow_type": ...})`, causing Prometheus to concatenate duplicate label values with semicolons (e.g. `delete-recordings-with-person;delete-recordings-with-person`)
- Removed the redundant `workflow_type` and `activity_type` attributes; only truly additional attributes like `status` are still passed

Note: the same bug exists in the llm_analytics interceptors (`metrics.py`, `sentiment/metrics.py`, `trace_clustering/metrics.py`, `trace_summarization/metrics.py`) — will fix in a follow-up.

## Test plan

- [ ] Deploy and verify the Grafana "Workflow throughput by type" panel on the session-replay-recording-api dashboard no longer shows duplicated names like `delete-recordings-with-person;delete-recordings-with-person`
- [ ] Verify existing metric queries in Grafana still work (the `workflow_type` label is still present, just no longer duplicated)

title: feat(onboarding): add adblocker detection to SDK onboarding verification
body: ## Problem Users setting up PostHog SDKs during onboarding may have adblockers enabled that silently block PostHog requests. This results in a confusing experience where the "Verify Installation" check appears to hang indefinitely, with no indication that requests are being blocked rather than the SDK failing to initialize. ## Changes Added adblocker detection to the SDK onboarding flow: 1. **New hook: `useAdblockDetection`** – Detects whether PostHog requests are being blocked by checking: - Whether `posthog-js` successfully initialized (`window.posthog.__loaded`) - Whether a test fetch to the PostHog `/decide/` endpoint succeeds - Results are delayed by 20 seconds to avoid false positives during normal initialization 2. **Updated `RealtimeCheckIndicator`** – Now displays a warning banner when: - Installation verification is still pending AND - Adblocker detection indicates requests are being blocked - The banner includes helpful guidance on disabling adblockers and setting up a reverse proxy ## How did you test this code? Code review of the implementation: - `useAdblockDetection` properly handles async detection with cleanup on unmount - Detection is delayed to avoid premature warnings - `RealtimeCheckIndicator` correctly integrates the hook and conditionally renders the warning - Warning only shows when both conditions are met (pending verification + blocked detection) ## Publish to changelog? Yes – This improves the onboarding experience by helping users diagnose adblocker-related issues. https://claude.ai/code/session_01V2YpYguNpwtSfwUXeYVw9J
author: fercgomes

title: perf(cohorts): optimize realtime cohort query building by passing team objects
body:
## Problem

Realtime cohort calculations were experiencing slow query building times (~800ms for p0-50 percentile). Additionally, overlapping temporal workflows were being queued instead of skipped, and there was no visibility into which specific cohorts were causing performance issues.

## Changes

- **Added optional `team` and `cohort` parameters** to `FOSSCohortQuery.unwrap_cohort()` to eliminate redundant database queries
- **Updated all callers** to pass preloaded team and cohort objects from `select_related("team")` queries
- **Maintained cross-team cohort access** by keeping `team__project_id` filtering for project-level cohort sharing
- **Changed temporal schedule overlap policy** from `BUFFER_ONE` to `SKIP` to prevent workflow queuing when one is already running
- **Added slow cohort logging** with warnings for cohorts taking longer than 10 seconds, including cohort ID, duration, team ID, and cohort name for identification

title: fix(test): dismiss Monaco autocomplete in HogQL E2E test
body:
## Summary
- Fixes the flaky E2E test "SQL / HogQL: write query, execute and verify results" by dismissing Monaco autocomplete after typing the query

## Problem
The test types a multi-line HogQL query character by character using `keyboard.type()`. Monaco's HogQL autocomplete provider has **space as a trigger character** (`triggerCharacters: [' ', ',', '.', '{']` in `hogQLAutocompleteProvider.ts:89`) with a 300ms delay (`quickSuggestionsDelay: 300` in `QueryPane.tsx:67`).

When `keyboard.type()` sends a space, Monaco starts a timer to show autocomplete suggestions. If the suggestion widget appears while subsequent characters are being typed, it can **intercept keystrokes** — corrupting the query. The corrupted query either fails to parse or returns wrong results, so `"Showing 10 rows"` never appears and the test times out.

The test has been flaky since it was first added (~Feb 23), appearing as a failure in 6+ distinct runs in the last 7 days on master.

## Fix
1. **Press `Escape` after typing** to dismiss any lingering autocomplete popup before the run button is clicked
2. **Replace `Meta+A` with `Control+A`** — on Linux CI, `Meta` maps to the Super/Windows key (not Ctrl), so `Meta+A` is a no-op. Not currently causing failures since the editor starts empty, but fixes a latent bug.

## Related
- Insight: [`01KK9XNDVZ445T05Y2BX8YM584`](https://app.mendral.com/insights/01KK9XNDVZ445T05Y2BX8YM584)

title: fix(insights): stop double timezone conversion on x-axis labels
body:
Datetime strings in the API response are already in the project timezone (ClickHouse applies toTimeZone before truncation), but the x-axis formatter was treating them as UTC and converting again. This caused hour labels to be offset by the project's UTC difference (e.g. 05:00 displayed as 13:00 for UTC+8).

Switch from dayjsUtcToTimezone() to dayjs.tz() to match the tooltip formatter, which already handles this correctly.

## Problem

Users in non-UTC timezones see inconsistent times between the x-axis labels and tooltips on hourly trend charts. For example, a UTC+8 user hovering over a data point sees "05:00 (UTC+8)" in the tooltip but the x-axis label at that position reads "13:00", an 8-hour offset matching their UTC difference.

## Changes

The x-axis formatter in `formatXAxisTick.ts` was treating datetime strings from the API as UTC and converting them to the project timezone using `dayjsUtcToTimezone()`. However, ClickHouse already applies `toTimeZone(e.timestamp, '<project_tz>')` before truncation, so the strings arrive **already in the project timezone**. This caused a double conversion (e.g. 05:00 + 8h = 13:00).

The fix replaces `dayjsUtcToTimezone(s, timezone, false)` with `dayjs.tz(s, timezone)`, which correctly interprets the string as already being in the target timezone — matching the tooltip formatter's existing behavior.

## How did you test this code?

I followed these testing steps:
1. Set your project timezone to something non-UTC (e.g. UTC+8 or America/New_York)
2. Create a trends insight with hourly interval (e.g. "Last 24 hours" grouped by hour)
3. Hover over data points and verify the x-axis label matches the time shown in the tooltip

## Publish to changelog?

No

## 🤖 LLM context

Co-authored by Claude Opus 4.6 via Claude Code.

title: fix(test): scope insight delete button locator to visible side panel
body:
## Summary
- Fixes the consistently flaky E2E test "Deleting an insight from dashboard redirects back" by scoping the delete button locator to `#side-panel`

## Problem
The delete button is rendered via `createPortal` into `scenePanelElement`. There are **two portal targets**:

1. **Inline panel** (`Navigation.tsx:199`) — always has `display: none` for insights because `scenePanelOpenManual` defaults to `false`
2. **Side panel** (`SidePanelInfo.tsx:29`) — visible after opening the Info tab, registered via `useEffect`

The unscoped `page.getByTestId('insight-delete')` matches the button in the **hidden inline panel** and times out (90s) waiting for it to become visible. The button never becomes visible there because the inline panel is permanently hidden for insights.

PR #50057 attempted to fix this with `scrollIntoViewIfNeeded()` and waiting for `.scene-panel-actions-section` to be attached, but neither helps — the button *is* attached, just in a `display: none` container.

## Fix
Scope the locator to `#side-panel` so Playwright only finds the button once the portal content has moved to the visible side panel container:

```typescript
const deleteButton = this.page.locator('#side-panel').getByTestId('insight-delete')
```

This is a test-side fix. The product behavior is correct — the portal switch happens quickly and users never see the intermediate state.

## Related
- Insight: `01KJZDHSWQ6Q11TGBNVZMAVDWG`
- Previous fix attempt: PR #50057

title: fix(personhog): wrong types on group type mapping
body:
## Problem

After enabling `get_group_type_mappings_by_project_id` calls to the personhog-replica service,
we hit two runtime type decode errors:

1. `detail_dashboard_id`: Rust struct used `Option<i64>` (`INT8`) but the Postgres column is `INT4`
   (Django `ForeignKey` maps to a 32-bit integer).
2. `default_columns`: Rust struct used `Option<serde_json::Value>` (`JSONB`) but the Postgres column
   is `TEXT[]` (Django `ArrayField(TextField())`).

These errors were returned as `Code::Internal` by the replica, and the personhog-router incorrectly
retried them up to 3 times — wasting resources on a permanent error that could never succeed.

Our integration tests didn't catch this because the test helper only inserted `NULL` values for
these columns. sqlx happily decodes `NULL` into any `Option<T>` without validating the inner type.

## Changes

**personhog-replica: fix type mismatches**
- `src/storage/postgres/group.rs`: Changed `GroupTypeMappingRow.detail_dashboard_id` from
  `Option<i64>` to `Option<i32>`, with `.map(|id| id.into())` to widen to `i64` in the
  service/proto layer.
- `src/storage/postgres/group.rs` and `src/storage/types/group.rs`: Changed `default_columns`
  from `Option<serde_json::Value>` to `Option<Vec<String>>` to match the actual `TEXT[]` column.

**personhog-router: fix retry policy**
- `src/backend/retry.rs`: Removed `Code::Internal` from the retryable status code set.
  Only `Unavailable` and `DeadlineExceeded` are retried now, per gRPC conventions.
  `Internal` errors (type mismatches, bugs) are permanent and should fail fast.

**personhog-replica: improve test coverage**
- `tests/common/mod.rs`: Updated `insert_group_type_mapping` to populate all columns
  (`name_singular`, `name_plural`, `default_columns`, `detail_dashboard_id`) with non-null
  values. This ensures sqlx validates the full type mapping against the real schema, so any
  future mismatches are caught immediately in CI instead of in production.


## How did you test this code?

- `cargo check` passes for personhog-replica
- `cargo test --lib backend::retry` passes for personhog-router (6/6 tests)
- `cargo check --tests` passes for personhog-replica (confirms test helper compiles with correct types)
 	- addedtests to the personhog-replica service to cover the missed cases
- Verified Rust types against the Django model definitions in `posthog/models/group_type_mapping.py`

title: fix(test): make assertConversationEqual order-agnostic for tool call messages
body:
## Summary

- Fix flaky `test_root_node_can_execute_multiple_tool_calls` test (~2% failure rate on master) by making `assertConversationEqual` order-agnostic for parallel tool call responses
- Consecutive `AssistantToolCallMessage` entries are now sorted by `tool_call_id` before comparison, so the assertion passes regardless of async execution order

## Problem

The test mocks two concurrent tool calls (`search` and `read_taxonomy`) dispatched in parallel. Their responses arrive in non-deterministic order, but `assertConversationEqual` compared messages positionally — when `read_taxonomy` resolved before `search`, the assertion failed.

## Changes

**`ee/hogai/test/base.py`**:
- Extract `_assert_message_equal` helper to reduce duplication
- Add `_sort_tool_call_runs` inside `assertConversationEqual` that sorts consecutive runs of `AssistantToolCallMessage` by `tool_call_id` before comparison
- Both `output` and `expected_output` are sorted the same way, so the test expectations remain readable and unchanged

No changes to the test itself — the fix is in the shared assertion helper, benefiting any future tests with parallel tool calls.

title: fix(llma): handle malformed $ai_input in sentiment data fetching
body:
## Summary
- Skip `$ai_generation` events with malformed (non-JSON) `$ai_input` instead of crashing the entire sentiment workflow
- Fixes both `fetch_generations` (trace-level) and `fetch_generations_by_uuid` (generation-level)

**Example failure:** https://cloud.temporal.io/namespaces/posthog-prod-us.usz2o/workflows/llma-sentiment-297697-1772665233932-83439089/72b9546e-fbad-4256-b4ce-6fc9a81346d9/timeline

- [ ] Confirm malformed generations are skipped without crashing

title: fix(oauth-proxy): rewrite client_secret for confidential EU clients
body:
## Problem

Confidential OAuth clients (e.g. Claude Desktop) authenticate via `client_secret_post`, sending `client_secret` in the token exchange body. The oauth-proxy already rewrites `client_id` and `redirect_uri` when forwarding to the regional server, but was **not** rewriting `client_secret`.

When an EU user connects, the proxy forwards the EU `client_id` paired with the US `client_secret` → EU server returns `invalid_client`.

## Changes

- `proxyPostWithClientId` in `proxy.ts` now accepts an optional `clientSecretRewrite` parameter and swaps `client_secret` in both JSON and form-encoded bodies
- `proxyWithMapping` in `token.ts` computes the secret rewrite from the KV client mapping (US secret → regional secret) and passes it through
- Added test for confidential client secret rewrite

## How did you test this code?

- Added failing test first, confirmed it fails with `expected 'us_secret_abc' to be 'eu_secret_xyz'`
- Applied fix, all 24 tests pass
- Reproduced locally end-to-end: registered confidential client through proxy, authorized with EU region, token exchange with US secret returns `invalid_grant` (correct — fake auth code) instead of `invalid_client` (the bug)

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
