title: fix(replay): proxy recording-api requests through node to bypass browser security
body:
## Problem

The in-browser data-loader was failing with `[INIT_FAILED] Failed to fetch` because Chromium blocks:
- **Mixed content**: HTTPS page (`https://us.posthog.com/player`) cannot fetch HTTP resources (`http://recording-api...svc.cluster.local:6738`)
- **Private Network Access**: public origin cannot reach cluster-internal IPs

## Changes

Proxy all recording-api requests through Puppeteer request interception in Node.js, so the browser never contacts recording-api directly.

- Add `BLOCK_REQUEST_PREFIX` (`/__blocks/`) to shared protocol, add `blockCount` to `PlayerConfig`
- Simplify data-loader to fetch blocks by index via synthetic `/__blocks/:index` URLs — no listing call, no auth/S3 knowledge needed in browser
- Add `fetchBlockList()` + `handleBlockRequest()` proxy in `PlayerController` — Node.js prefetches the block listing then proxies individual block requests with auth headers, byte ranges, and decompress flags
- Remove `recordingApiBaseUrl`/`recordingApiSecret` from `PlayerConfig` — secrets stay entirely on the Node.js side
- Remove CORS from recording-api (browser never contacts it directly)
- Use global `fetch` (not `internalFetch`) — the rasterizer runs in a standalone Node.js worker without Postgres, and `internalFetch` transitively imports config that requires `DATABASE_URL`
- Fix `INTERNAL_API_SECRET` env var in rasterizer startup script to match what `config.ts` reads
- Delete unused `test-puppeteer.mjs`

## Test plan

- [x] All rasterizer + replay-headless + recording-api tests pass (135 + 30 + 141)
- [x] Unit tests for `fetchBlockList` (4 tests) and `handleBlockRequest` (5 tests)
- [x] End-to-end test via `RasterizeRecordingWorkflow` locally — video renders correctly
- [ ] Re-run rasterization workflow in production after deploy
author: TueHaulund

reviews.body: Only had couple nits and a question, LGTM 🥇

commits.message:
fix(replay): proxy recording-api requests through node to bypass browser security

Move recording-api fetches from in-browser to Node.js Puppeteer request
interception, eliminating mixed-content and Private Network Access issues.

The browser-side data-loader now fetches synthetic /__blocks/:index URLs
which the PlayerController intercepts and proxies to recording-api with
auth headers. This keeps the API secret entirely on the Node.js side.

title: fix(data-warehouse): restore Z suffix for Klaviyo datetime
body:
## Problem

Klaviyo data warehouse syncs configured as "append by date" re-import the entire dataset on every run instead of just new records, creating exact duplicates and inflating data warehouse usage.

The root cause is a regression introduced in #50629 (make Klaviyo source resumable, Mar 11). That PR branched from before #49890 (Z suffix fix, Mar 5) and rewrote `klaviyo.py` without carrying over the fix. This reverted `_format_incremental_value` to using `datetime.isoformat()`, which produces the `+00:00` UTC offset format that Klaviyo's API rejects.

As a result, the incremental datetime filter (`greater-than(datetime,...)`) is malformed on every subsequent sync, causing either a 400 Bad Request or the filter being silently ignored. Since append mode does a pure append with no deduplication on primary keys, any re-fetched data creates exact duplicates.

## Changes

- Restores `_format_datetime_z()` helper that formats datetimes with the `Z` suffix (e.g. `2026-03-04T02:58:14.000Z` instead of `2026-03-04T02:58:14+00:00`)
- Updates `_format_incremental_value` to use it for both `datetime` and `date` inputs
- Adds regression tests for `_format_incremental_value`, `_build_filter`, and `_build_initial_params` to prevent this from being lost again

## How did you test this code?

I haven't manually tested this, however, I did the following:

- Verified the fix logic directly by running the formatting functions against all input types (UTC datetime, datetime with microseconds, naive datetime, date, string passthrough)
- All produce Z suffix format, none contain `+00:00`
- Added parameterized test suite covering the formatting, filter building, and initial params construction

## Publish to changelog?

No

## Docs update

No

## 🤖 LLM context

Co-authored by Claude Code.
author: christiaan-ph

commits.message:
fix(data-warehouse): restore Z suffix for Klaviyo datetime filters

The resumable source rewrite (66447ed5cfd, Mar 11) branched from before
the Z suffix fix (1a8932d6a7c, Mar 5), regressing _format_incremental_value
back to using datetime.isoformat() which produces the +00:00 format that
Klaviyo's API rejects.

This caused incremental/append syncs to either fail with 400 Bad Request
or silently ignore the datetime filter, re-importing the entire dataset
on every run and creating exact duplicates in append mode.

Restores _format_datetime_z() and adds regression tests.

fix(data-warehouse): handle naive datetimes as UTC and parameterize tests

Address review feedback:
- Treat naive datetimes as UTC via replace() instead of astimezone()
  which would use the local system timezone
- Parameterize TestBuildFilter per project conventions

title: fix: Prevent 'slow' tasks from increasing queue depth in temporal
body:
## Problem

The subscription scheduling workflow can encounter issues when a previous run is still executing and a new scheduled run attempts to start. This can lead to scheduling conflicts or missed executions as by default, temporal will "skip" the new workflow if something is already in flight.

[Docs](https://docs.temporal.io/schedule#overlap-policy)

## Changes

Added `ScheduleOverlapPolicy.ALLOW_ALL` to the subscription schedule configuration, allowing new scheduled runs to start even if previous runs are still executing. This is safe because child workflows use idempotent IDs (`process-subscription-{id}`), ensuring Temporal automatically rejects duplicate starts for the same subscription ([docs](https://docs.temporal.io/workflow-execution/workflowid-runid#workflow-id-reuse-policy)).

- This seemed the safest approach to avoid delaying the deliveries for users who are waiting on their subscription "in the new batch" (which is a drawback of the buffered options), whilst giving a lot of additional time to the activities in flight. Additionally cancelling whatever was in flight does not seem right as it means that the subscription won't potentially be picked up until the next delivery date (depending on how we cancel/terminate it), and otherwise it might cause us to do additional work / create infinite loops if a subscription consequently takes >1hr to process, as it would get terminated and then restart from 0 again with the 1hr processing, ad infinitum. 

## How did you test this code?

Will closely monitor post release, and tested ni the UI over here:

[In flight run](https://cloud.temporal.io/namespaces/posthog-prod-us.usz2o/workflows/schedule-all-subscriptions-schedule-2026-03-25T07%3A55%3A00Zv2/019d24c1-3393-784a-a7b0-39779c7e5b5a/timeline)

[New run, ran without issues](https://cloud.temporal.io/namespaces/posthog-prod-us.usz2o/workflows/schedule-all-subscriptions-schedule-2026-03-25T11%3A55%3A00Z/019d24d9-1742-753a-9904-b1c2fb30b7a1/timeline)

## Publish to changelog?

No

## Docs update

No docs update needed for this internal scheduling configuration change.
author: vdekrijger

commits.message: fix: Prevent 'slow' tasks from increasing queue depth
fix: Gracefully deal with already started workflow errors

title: fix: defer marketing analytics column config changes until Apply

The "Configure columns" modal in marketing analytics was recalculating the table query on every individual toggle (show/hide column, pin, sort). This caused unnecessary re-renders and data fetches while the user was still configuring their preferences.

## Changes

- Verified "Cancel" discards all draft changes

title: feat(flags): parallelize property fetch
body:
## Problem

`fetch_and_locally_cache_all_relevant_properties` is a 260+ line function that ran three database queries **sequentially on a single connection** from the `persons_reader` pool:

1. Person query (person_id, UUID, properties)
2. Cohort query (static cohort membership) — depends on query 1
3. Group query (group properties) — **independent** of queries 1 and 2

The group query has no dependency on person data, yet it waited for person+cohort to complete before it could start because all three shared the same connection.

## Changes

Decompose the monolithic function into focused helpers and parallelize the independent branches using `tokio::try_join!`.

Single file changed: `rust/feature-flags/src/flags/flag_matching_utils.rs`

### Intermediate result types

Two private structs carry results from the async helpers back to the orchestrator:

- **`PersonCohortResult`**: Holds `person: Option<Person>` and `cohort_matches: Option<HashMap<CohortId, bool>>`. The `Option` on `cohort_matches` encodes a three-state semantic: `None` = person not found (cohort evaluation never attempted), `Some(empty)` = person found but no static cohorts to check, `Some(results)` = person found and cohorts checked.
- **`GroupResult`**: Holds `HashMap<GroupTypeIndex, HashMap<String, Value>>`.

### `fetch_person_and_cohorts`

Extracted async helper that acquires its own `persons_reader` connection and runs the person query followed by the cohort query sequentially (cohort lookup depends on `person.id` from the person query). All metrics, canonical log writes, and slow-query warnings are preserved with the same thresholds (500ms person, 200ms cohort).

### `fetch_group_properties`

Extracted async helper for group property queries. Acquires its own connection from the same `persons_reader` pool — `posthog_group` lives in the persons database alongside person/cohort tables.

### `apply_person_cohort_to_state`

Synchronous helper that applies `PersonCohortResult` to `FlagEvaluationState`. Sets person_id, person_uuid, cohort_matches, and person_properties (always including `distinct_id`).

### Rewritten orchestrator

The public function signature is unchanged. The body is now a thin orchestrator:

- When groups are needed (~5.7% of requests): `tokio::try_join!` runs person+cohort and group queries concurrently, each acquiring its own connection from the same pool.
- When no groups are needed (~94.3%): only the person+cohort path runs.

`tokio::try_join!` runs both futures cooperatively on the **same task**, so `with_canonical_log`'s task-local `RefCell` borrow is safe. A `SAFETY` comment documents this invariant, refactoring to `tokio::spawn` would break task-local access. On error, `try_join!` short-circuits and cancels the other future cleanly.

## How did you test this code?

- All existing tests pass (1021 passed, 0 failed after fix)
- Group-related tests (`test_group_property_overrides`, `test_numeric_group_keys`, `test_paired_group_identifiers_avoid_cartesian_product`, `test_partial_group_property_overrides_fallback_behavior`) exercise the parallel path with dual-database routing enabled in CI
author: matheus-vb

reviews.body: ## Pull request overview Refactors the Rust feature-flag property fetch path to reduce end-to-end latency by running the independent group-property query concurrently with the person+cohort query (when groups are needed), while keeping the public API unchanged. **Changes:** - Decomposes the monolithic DB property fetch function into focused helpers (`fetch_person_and_cohorts`, `fetch_group_properties`, `apply_person_cohort_to_state`). - Parallelizes the person+cohort and group branches using `tokio::try_join!` when group properties are required. - Introduces intermediate result structs to pass data from async helpers back to the orchestrator. --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.
Nice refactor! A few non-blocking suggestions inline.

commits.message: parallelize queries with different connection pools

title: fix(toolbar): echo custom headers in CORS preflight for toolbar OAuth endpoints
body:
## Problem

Customers whose apps monkey-patch `window.fetch` to inject custom headers (e.g. `x-app-version`, `x-correlation-id`) cannot authenticate the toolbar. The toolbar runs inside the customer's page and uses `window.fetch` for the OAuth token exchange. When the injected headers aren't in PostHog's CORS `Access-Control-Allow-Headers`, the browser blocks the preflight and the token exchange fails with a network error.

This has been reported by multiple customers, particularly those using CDNs like CloudFront where fetch interceptors are common.

https://posthoghelp.zendesk.com/agent/tickets/54184

## Changes

Added `ToolbarOAuthCorsMiddleware` that intercepts CORS preflight (`OPTIONS`) requests to `/oauth/token/` and `/toolbar_oauth/check` and echoes back whatever `Access-Control-Request-Headers` the browser requests. This runs before `django-cors-headers` so it can handle the preflight before the standard middleware rejects unknown headers.

Key design decisions:
- **No `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials`**: the toolbar uses Bearer tokens, not cookies, so credentials aren't needed. This avoids the security concern of open origin reflection + credentials.
- **Narrowly scoped**: only intercepts OPTIONS on two specific paths, all other requests pass through to the normal middleware stack.
- **`frozenset` for path matching**: O(1) lookup, includes both trailing-slash variants.

## How did you test this code?

Automated tests covering:
- Preflight echoes back custom headers and reflects origin
- Non-preflight requests (GET/POST) pass through unaffected
- Preflight without `Origin` header falls back to `*`
- Preflight to unrelated paths is not intercepted by this middleware
- `Access-Control-Max-Age` is set correctly
- No `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials` header is present

All 92 tests in `test_toolbar_oauth_endpoint_auth.py` pass.

I am an agent — I have not tested this manually. Manual testing would involve loading the toolbar on a site with a fetch interceptor that injects custom headers and verifying the OAuth flow completes.

## Publish to changelog?

no

## Docs update

Toolbar troubleshooting docs for CDN/CSP issues should be added to posthog.com (separate repo).

## 🤖 LLM context

Co-authored with Claude Code. The fix was identified by tracing a customer report where `VersionHeaderInit.tsx` in their app patched `window.fetch` to add `x-app-version` to every request, breaking the toolbar's OAuth token exchange CORS preflight.
author: fercgomes

reviews.body: Added a few lowish pri comments

commits.message: chore: allow custom headers for toolbar

title: fix(phrocs): reset stdin in pipe fallback to prevent fd 0 closure
body:
## Problem

`celery-worker`  exited for me with `exit 0`. No errors, just a clean exit. Had Claude churn on the problem for a bit and found this issue. It's likely related to my terminal setup (kitty + zellij). Anyways, Claude think there still should be a fallback path that works here, and that the fix is legit.

When `pty.Start()` fails, phrocs falls back to `startWithPipe`. The fallback resets `SysProcAttr` and reassigns `Stdout`/`Stderr` to the pipe, but never resets `Stdin` — which still references the now-closed PTY slave file. The closed file's `Fd()` returns `-1`, which Go's `forkExec` interprets as "close fd 0 in the child".

This causes Python child processes to start with `sys.stdin = None`, which silently breaks celery's click-based CLI: `worker_main()` returns `None` instead of blocking, and the process exits with code 0 without ever starting the worker.

## Changes

Reset `cmd.Stdin`, `cmd.Stdout`, and `cmd.Stderr` to `nil` at the top of `startWithPipe`, before assigning the pipe. This ensures the child gets `/dev/null` for stdin and the pipe for output.

## Testing

1. `hogli start --phrocs`
2. Verify celery-worker reaches `ready` state and shows the celery banner + "Connected to redis" in logs
author: andehen

reviews.body: makes sense 👍🏼

commits.message:
fix(phrocs): reset stdin in pipe fallback to prevent fd 0 closure

When pty.Start() fails and phrocs falls back to startWithPipe, the
cmd.Stdin/Stdout/Stderr fields still reference the now-closed PTY slave
file. The fallback resets SysProcAttr and reassigns Stdout/Stderr to the
pipe, but never resets Stdin. The closed file's Fd() returns -1, which
Go's forkExec interprets as "close fd 0 in the child". This causes
Python to start with sys.stdin=None, which silently breaks celery's
click-based CLI (exits with code 0 without starting the worker).

title: feat(logs): add logs-saved-views feature flag and integrate into API and frontend
body:

title: fix(workflows): Remove direct pixel/redirect metric recording to prevent double counting with SES webhooks
body:
## Problem

After merging [#52210](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/pull/52210), email open and click metrics are being double counted. Both the SES webhook handler and our custom tracking pixel/redirect handlers now record the same events, inflating the numbers (e.g. 42 opens shown for 24 emails sent).

This happens because SES fires its own Open/Click webhook events AND the email client loads our tracking pixel/follows our redirect - each path independently records a metric. On localhost this wasn't happening as maildev doesn't send any webhooks like SES does.

## Changes

- Removed metric recording from `handleEmailTrackingPixel` and `handleEmailTrackingRedirect` - these handlers now only serve the gif and redirect respectively, without calling `trackMetric`. SES webhooks remain the sole source of open/click metrics.
- Updated `cdp-api.ts` callers to match the now-synchronous handler signatures.
- Updated tests to verify handlers return correct responses without producing Kafka messages.

## How did you test this code?

- All email tracking, SES webhook, and email service tests pass (45 total)
- Observed the double counting in production after the previous PR merged, confirmed the inflated numbers match the expected 2x pattern

## Publish to changelog?

No

title: feat(flags): optimize regex matching by pre-compiling builder
body:
## Problem

`match_property()` recompiles a `fancy_regex::Regex` from scratch on every evaluation of a `Regex`/`NotRegex` property filter. Regex compilation is orders of magnitude more expensive than matching.

This was identified in #52022 as the next optimization target: regex operators (~70us each) dominate total evaluation time at higher flag counts.

## Changes

Relocate regex compilation from the per-match hot path (`match_property()`) to a single upfront pass (`prepare_regexes()`) called once per request after flag deserialization.

Today, flags are deserialized from HyperCache on every request, so the compiled regexes are **not cached across requests** — total per-request compilation cost is the same. The wins are:

1. **Eliminates redundant re-compilation within a request** — a regex filter that is evaluated more than once (experience continuity re-evaluation, flag dependency chains, super_groups) now compiles once instead of N times.
2. **Short-circuits known-invalid patterns** — previously, a broken pattern would attempt (and fail) compilation on every evaluation; now it fails once and subsequent evaluations return `Ok(false)` immediately.
3. **Stepping stone for cross-request caching** — the `CompiledRegex` field is the hook for a future change that caches the `FeatureFlagList` with pre-compiled regexes in the HyperCache layer, eliminating per-request compilation entirely.

### Implementation details

- Add a `CompiledRegex` enum to `PropertyFilter` with two variants: `Compiled(regex)` for valid patterns and `InvalidPattern` for patterns that failed to compile. The field is `Option<CompiledRegex>` — `None` means `prepare_regex()` was not called (backward-compatible fallback).
- Add `prepare_regex()` on `PropertyFilter` that compiles the pattern with the same `backtrack_limit(10_000)` used today.
- Add `prepare_regexes()` on `FeatureFlagList` that walks all flags -> conditions -> property filters (including super_groups).
- Call `prepare_regexes()` in `fetch_and_filter()` — the single entry point for all flag evaluation — after constructing the flag list.
- Modify `match_property()` to use a three-state `match` dispatch: pre-compiled regex -> fast path, known-invalid pattern -> immediate `Ok(false)`, not prepared -> on-the-fly compilation fallback.

The fallback path is necessary for cohort property filters (constructed dynamically in `cohort_operations.rs`, not from the flag cache) and for test code that constructs `PropertyFilter` directly.

`fancy_regex::Regex` is `Clone + Send + Sync` with cheap `Arc`-based cloning internally, so this is safe for concurrent evaluation across tokio tasks.

## How did you test this code?

- All existing tests pass
- 7 new unit tests for `prepare_regex()`: valid pattern, invalid pattern, non-regex operator no-op, behavioral equivalence for both `Regex` and `NotRegex` operators, and an explicit test that `InvalidPattern` returns `Ok(false)` for both `Regex` and `NotRegex` (pinning the existing behavior)
- 1 integration test for `prepare_regexes()` verifying it compiles regex filters but leaves non-regex filters untouched

title: feat(visual-review): wire VR CLI into storybook and playwright CI
body:
Wires the Visual Review CLI into both snapshot CI workflows so baseline hashes are tracked automatically.

**Storybook** (`ci-storybook.yml`) — after snapshots are diffed, the VR CLI submits them to the API, auto-approves, and writes the signed baseline back. The baseline update lands in the same commit as snapshot changes.

**Playwright** (`ci-e2e-playwright.yml`) — same pattern, different `--dir` and `--type`.

**Baseline files** — `frontend/snapshots.yml` (storybook) and `playwright/snapshots.yml` colocated next to their respective screenshot directories. Both point at team 2 on us.posthog.com.

**CLI logging** — submit flow now logs baseline count, upload decisions, and run status so the wire-level data flow is visible in CI output.

Requires `VR_API_TOKEN` secret (PAT with `visual_review` scope).

chore(visual-review): wire VR CLI into storybook CI

Add visual review submit as a non-blocking step in the handle-snapshots
job. Uses `npx tsx` to run the CLI directly from source — no build step
or workspace membership needed. Auto-approve during the transition
period so the baseline stays in sync with jest-image-snapshot.

feat(visual-review): add wire-level logging to CLI submit flow

Logs baseline count, manifest size, upload decisions, and run status
so readers can trace the full request/response flow in CI output.

feat(visual-review): add playwright CI wiring and baseline files

Wire VR CLI into playwright e2e CI with same pattern as storybook.
Add baseline files at frontend/snapshots.yml (storybook) and
playwright/snapshots.yml with real repo UUID.
fix(visual-review): trim token to avoid trailing newline from GH secrets

fix(visual-review): pass CI params via env vars instead of interpolation

Fixes semgrep shell-injection warning and likely fixes the 401 auth
failure — direct ${{ }} interpolation in run: can mangle secret values.

fix(visual-review): run VR submit on every PR, gate auto-approve on update mode

The handle-snapshots/handle-screenshots jobs were gated on update mode,
so VR never ran in check mode. Now the jobs always run when there are
frontend/e2e changes, but auto-approve and commit are still conditional
on update mode.

fix(visual-review): parallel uploads, longer presigned URLs, always-run CI

- Upload 10 artifacts concurrently instead of sequentially
- Bump presigned URL expiry from 15min to 1hr (3000+ uploads need time)
- Run VR submit on every PR, gate auto-approve on update mode only

fix(visual-review): commit baseline separately from snapshot PNGs

The commit-snapshots action only detects changes in the snapshot
directory, so baseline YAML changes were staged but never committed.
Commit baselines in a separate step before the PNG commit.
fix(visual-review): set git identity for baseline commits in CI
fix(visual-review): use 4-space indent in baseline YAML to match oxfmt

fix(visual-review): stage baseline into snapshot commit instead of separate push

Avoids race conditions between storybook and playwright workflows
both trying to push baseline commits independently.
test: tweak LemonBadge story to trigger snapshot change

fix(visual-review): always exit 0 during migration phase

VR is observational during migration — errors and drift should not
show red X in CI. When VR becomes the gate, flip exit codes back.
revert: undo LemonBadge test change and reset PNGs to master

title: fix: handle cross-region Vercel SSO when resource_id is missing
body:
## Problem

When clicking "Open in PostHog" from the Vercel dashboard, the SSO request sometimes has no `resource_id`. The region redirect check (`_should_redirect_to_eu`) only looked at `resource_id`, so when it was missing, SSO always ran on US. If the installation is on EU, it fails with "Installation not found" / "Authentication failed".

This was happening for `icfg_mRgl1xRTjYpqw5JHZQNEMFNs` - the installation exists on EU but SSO hits US first with no resource_id.

## Changes

When `resource_id` is missing and we're on US:
1. Exchange the SSO code with Vercel to get the JWT claims (which contain `installation_id`)
2. Check if the `installation_id` exists locally via `OrganizationIntegration`
3. If not found, encrypt the claims with Fernet (same approach as #52114) and redirect to EU with the encrypted token in the URL
4. EU decrypts the token, caches the claims locally, and proceeds with `authenticate_sso` which finds them in cache instead of trying to re-exchange the consumed code

Also added `installation_id` as an alternative lookup in `_should_redirect_to_eu`.

## How did you test this?

- [x] `pytest ee/api/vercel/test/test_vercel_sso.py -v` - all 40 tests pass
- [x] Verified from prod logs that `icfg_mRgl1xRTjYpqw5JHZQNEMFNs` is proxied to EU for all other endpoints

## Changelog

No - bug fix for existing integration behavior.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: MattBro

commits.message:
fix: handle cross-region SSO when resource_id is missing

When Vercel sends SSO requests without a resource_id, the region
redirect check had no way to determine if the installation was on EU.
Now exchanges the SSO code on US first, checks if the installation_id
from the claims exists locally, and if not redirects to EU with an
encrypted claims token (using the same Fernet approach as the connect
flow). EU decrypts the token and caches the claims so authenticate_sso
can proceed without re-exchanging the consumed code.

title: feat(warehouse): add Convex as a data warehouse source
body:
## Problem

Users with Convex databases need to sync their data into PostHog's data warehouse. There was no existing integration for Convex as a source.

## Changes

- Add Convex as a new data warehouse source type using the Convex streaming export API
- **Initial sync**: uses `/api/list_snapshot` to paginate through a full table snapshot
- **Incremental sync**: uses `/api/document_deltas` to fetch only changed documents since the last cursor
- Handle `InvalidWindowToReadDocuments` errors (stale cursor >30 day retention window) as a non-retryable error with a clear message
- Source config with deploy URL and deploy key fields, requires Convex Professional plan
- All tables support incremental sync via the `_ts` field (nanosecond mutation timestamp)
- Datetime partitioning on `_creationTime`

## How did you test this code?

- Tested locally against a real Convex deployment with initial and incremental syncs
- Verified stale cursor handling by reproducing the `InvalidWindowToReadDocuments` error via curl

## Publish to changelog?

yes

commits.message: fix(warehouse): return convex deploy_url in source config response

fix(warehouse): use datetime partitioning for convex source

Partition by _creationTime (monthly) instead of MD5 on _id for more
efficient time-based queries.

fix(warehouse): handle stale convex delta cursor with clear error

Detect Convex's `InvalidWindowToReadDocuments` 400 error when the
document_deltas cursor is older than the ~30 day retention window.
Raises a clear `InvalidWindowError` instead of letting a generic
HTTPError get truncated by Temporal's failure size limit.

Added as a non-retryable error so it fails fast rather than
retrying 9 times with the same invalid cursor.

fix(warehouse): use correct tableName param for convex list_snapshot

The Convex API expects `tableName` (camelCase) but we were sending
`table_name` (snake_case), breaking all initial/full snapshot syncs.

fix(warehouse): filter convex schemas by names parameter

get_schemas was ignoring the names filter, returning all tables
instead of just the requested ones. This could return wrong metadata
when resyncing a specific schema.
chore: discard stale api docs snapshot changes

fix(warehouse): normalize convex _creationTime from ms to seconds

Convex returns _creationTime as milliseconds since epoch (float).
The datetime partitioner expects seconds, so all rows were falling
through to the default "1970-01" partition. Normalize in the source
generator before yielding batches.

fix(warehouse): validate convex deploy URL to prevent SSRF

Enforce that the deploy URL uses https, host matches *.convex.cloud,
and contains no query params or fragments. Validation runs in
validate_credentials, convex_source, and get_schemas.
fix(warehouse): use week partition format for convex source
fix(warehouse): simplify convex document_deltas error handling

title: fix(hogql): null comparisons
body:
## Problem

Postgres printer currently converts `IS [NOT] NULL` into `= NULL / != NULL`. These are not equivalent, `IS [NOT] NULL` must be preserved, while other `= / !=` comparisons should remain unchanged.

## Changes

Preserve null-comparison style in AST from both parsers.

## How did you test this code?

Unit tests

## Publish to changelog?

No
author: georgemunyoro

reviews.body: interesting... in clickhouse we have our own null handling where `a = null` gets transformed to `is null` automatically. I guess we don't want to impose this kind of special sauce on postgres/duck

## Pull request overview

This PR fixes incorrect null-comparison semantics when printing HogQL to Postgres SQL by preserving whether a null comparison originated from `IS [NOT] NULL` vs `= / != NULL` in the AST, and using that metadata in the Postgres printer.

**Changes:**
- Add `null_comparison_style` metadata to `ast.CompareOperation` and propagate it through cloning.
- Set `null_comparison_style="is"` for `IS [NOT] NULL` in both Python and C++ (cpp-json) parsers.
- Update the Postgres printer to render `IS NULL / IS NOT NULL` only when the AST indicates `IS`-style null comparisons; add unit tests for the Postgres printer output.

### Reviewed changes

Copilot reviewed 10 out of 10 changed files in this pull request and generated no comments.

<details>
<summary>Show a summary per file</summary>

| File | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| posthog/hogql/ast.py | Adds `null_comparison_style` to `CompareOperation` nodes. |
| posthog/hogql/parser.py | Marks `IS [NOT] NULL` parses with `null_comparison_style="is"`. |
| common/hogql_parser/parser_json.cpp | Emits `null_comparison_style` for `IS [NOT] NULL` in cpp-json AST output. |
| posthog/hogql/visitor.py | Preserves `null_comparison_style` when cloning `CompareOperation`. |
| posthog/hogql/printer/postgres.py | Prints `IS NULL / IS NOT NULL` when `null_comparison_style=="is"` and RHS is `NULL`. |
| posthog/hogql/test/_test_parser.py | Updates parser expectations to include `null_comparison_style="is"`. |
| posthog/hogql/printer/test/test_printer.py | Adds Postgres printer coverage for `IS NULL` vs `= NULL` behavior. |
| posthog/hogql/grammar/HogQLLexer.cpp.g4 | Adjusts comment scanning termination condition to treat `ch <= 0` as EOL/EOF. |
| common/hogql_parser/HogQLLexer.h | Mirrors the lexer termination condition change in the generated header. |
</details>

title: fix(workflows): Attribute email open and click metrics to workflow email actions
body:
## Problem

We got reports (e.g. [here](https://posthoghelp.zendesk.com/agent/tickets/53916) or [here](https://posthog.slack.com/archives/C0AMDJGKNDD/p1774256197377939)) that email "Opened" and "Clicked" columns show 0 in the workflow metrics overview table, even though SES webhook data confirms opens and clicks are being tracked in ClickHouse.

Email engagement metrics from SES webhooks are stored with per-run invocation IDs as `instance_id`, but the overview table groups by action node IDs (e.g. `action_function_email_65c395f5-...`). Since these don't match, opens and clicks never appear in the correct row.

## Changes

- **Include `actionId` in the email tracking code**: The base64 tracking code embedded in emails now encodes `functionId:invocationId:teamId:actionId` (previously just `functionId:invocationId:teamId`). This is backwards compatible - old 3-field codes still parse correctly with `actionId` as `undefined`.
- **Use `actionId` as `instance_id` for engagement metrics**: `trackMetric` now uses `actionId || invocationId` as the `instance_id`, matching how `email_sent` already stores metrics (via `email.service.ts:112`). This ensures opens/clicks appear in the same row as sent/delivered in the overview table.
- **Fix direct pixel/redirect handlers**: Added `parseTrackingParams` that decodes the `ph_id` query param via `parseEmailTrackingCode`, with fallback to legacy `ph_fn_id`/`ph_inv_id` params. Both `handleEmailTrackingPixel` and `handleEmailTrackingRedirect` now use this method.
- **Pass `actionId` through SES webhook path**: The SES webhook handler now extracts and forwards `actionId` from the tracking code so SES-originated engagement metrics also get correct attribution.

## How did you test this code?

- Ran all email tracking tests (18 tests across `email-tracking.service.test.ts` and `ses.test.ts`) and all email service tests (30 tests in `email.service.test.ts`) - all pass
- Verified the new `ph_id` format tests fail against the old code (confirming they catch the bug)
- Verified the legacy `ph_fn_id`/`ph_inv_id` tests still pass (backwards compatibility)
- Manually tested against local dev environment: hit the pixel and redirect endpoints with a real workflow's tracking code, confirmed metrics appeared in ClickHouse with the correct `instance_id` matching the action node ID
- Queried production ClickHouse (EU) to confirm existing `email_opened` data exists for the reporting customer but was stored with invocation IDs as `instance_id`

## Publish to changelog?

No
author: dmarchuk

commits.message: fix(workflows): Fix email opened and clicked metrics never being recorded

title: fix(subscriptions): add dashboard relation to exported asset query
body:
## Problem

After merging the [subscription flow refactor](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/pull/51649) I noticed that a subset of our subscriptions started to fail. After inspecting the stack trace in temporal ([example](https://cloud.temporal.io/namespaces/posthog-prod-eu.usz2o/workflows/process-subscription-3270/019d2434-5c6e-7da2-8845-cff54b3f8526/timeline)), it turned out that the Slack `deliver_subscription` path has a call chain which invokes the dashboard. E.g.: `_block_for_asset` calls `asset.get_public_content_url()` → `self.filename` → `self.dashboard`, and as a result Django tries a lazy FK load, which is forbidden in an async context.

This PR fixes it by lazy loading the dashboard as well so that it no longer needs to do the lazy FK load.

## Changes

Added "dashboard" to the select_related call so it's eagerly loaded with the initial query, avoiding the lazy load entirely.

## How did you test this code?

:white_check_mark: CI is passing

## Publish to changelog?

No

## Docs update

No

title: refactor(batch-exports): decouple kea logics by extracting batchExportConfigLogic
body:
## Problem

The batch export child logics (`batchExportRunsLogic`, `batchExportBackfillsLogic`, `batchExportBackfillModalLogic`) were tightly coupled to `batchExportConfigurationLogic` via `connect()` — a heavyweight logic that owns form state, validation, `beforeUnload`, and test step execution. This meant child logics couldn't be mounted independently.

When `HogFunctionBackfills` tried to render the backfills tab for hog function destinations, it needed to mount `batchExportBackfillsLogic`, which auto-mounted the full form logic — causing a Kea store path error because no parent `BindLogic` provided it.

Additionally, `hogFunctionBackfillsLogic` had a race condition: its `afterMount` checked `configuration.batch_export_id` before the config had loaded, causing an unnecessary call to `enable_backfills` on every tab open.

## Changes

Most of the changes here are either adding/updating tests or due to renaming the logic.

Therefore when reviewing these changes, it probably makes most sense to review `products/batch_exports/frontend/README.md` first, as it provides the architectural overview.

**Extract `batchExportConfigLogic`** (~35 lines) — a lightweight logic that only loads and caches `batchExportConfig` from the API. Child logics now connect here instead of the form logic, so they can be mounted independently (e.g. from the hog function backfills tab).

**Rename `batchExportConfigurationLogic` → `batchExportConfigFormLogic`** — clarifies that this logic owns the editing experience (form state, validation, dirty-checking, test steps, save/delete). It now connects to `batchExportConfigLogic` for its config data.

**Fix race condition in `hogFunctionBackfillsLogic`** — replaced `afterMount` with a `loadHogFunctionSuccess` listener so `enableHogFunctionBackfills` only fires after the config has loaded and `batch_export_id` is confirmed missing.

**Add architecture docs** to `products/batch_exports/frontend/README.md` with a logic dependency tree, component table, and external consumer notes.

## How did you test this code?

- All existing unit tests pass (`pnpm --filter=@posthog/frontend jest batchExport hogFunctionBackfills --no-coverage`)
- Playwright E2E tests pass
- New tests were added for hog function backfills (unit tests for Kea logic, E2E test and Storybook story (visual regression))
- Ran stack locally and tested all the tabs in both batch exports scene and hog function backfills

## Publish to changelog?

No

## Docs update

No docs update needed.

## 🤖 LLM context

This PR was co-authored with Claude Code. The refactor was planned using a dependency graph analysis of the batch export Kea logics, with alternatives (passing config as props, wrapping with BindLogic for the full form logic) considered and rejected. See `products/batch_exports/frontend/README.md` for the architecture overview.
author: rossgray

reviews.body: I have some questions about the README.
LGTM! Thank you

Add Storybook story for CDP backfills

batchExportConfigLogic that only loads/caches the config, and the existing

to the config logic instead, allowing them to mount independently — fixing

title: fix: bundle Monaco Editor workers and configure worker URLs
body:
## Problem

Monaco Editor requires web workers (editor, JSON language, TypeScript language) to function properly. Without proper worker configuration, Monaco may attempt to load workers from incorrect origins, causing cross-origin errors and breaking language features like syntax highlighting and code completion.

## Changes

- Added build configuration in `frontend/build.mjs` to bundle three Monaco Editor workers as separate ESM modules:
  - `monacoEditorWorker.js` - Core editor worker
  - `monacoJsonWorker.js` - JSON language support worker
  - `monacoTsWorker.js` - TypeScript/JavaScript language support worker

- Created `frontend/src/lib/monaco/monacoEnvironment.ts` to configure Monaco's worker URL resolution, ensuring workers are loaded from `/static/` instead of attempting to load from CDN or other origins

- Created three worker entry point files that import the corresponding Monaco worker modules:
  - `frontend/src/lib/monaco/workers/editor.worker.ts`
  - `frontend/src/lib/monaco/workers/json.worker.ts`
  - `frontend/src/lib/monaco/workers/ts.worker.ts`

- Updated `MonacoDiffEditor.tsx` and `CodeEditor.tsx` to import the `monacoEnvironment` configuration on component load

## How did you test this code?

This change is a build and configuration update. The bundled workers will be validated by:
- Build system successfully compiling the new worker bundles
- Monaco Editor components loading without cross-origin errors
- Language features (JSON validation, TypeScript intellisense) functioning correctly when the workers are available at the configured paths

## Publish to changelog?

no

title: fix(replay): scope ClickHouse TLS skip to client only, revert CA cert approach
body:
## Summary

- Replace process-wide `NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0` with a scoped `https.Agent({ rejectUnauthorized: false })` passed via `@clickhouse/client`'s `http_agent` option
- This skips TLS verification only for ClickHouse, leaving S3, Kafka, and other connections verified normally
- Revert the `CLICKHOUSE_CA` config and `Dockerfile.node` cert copy from #51222 — `ee/certs/ch.crt` is a dev-only cert (`CN=localhost`, expired 2021), and prod ClickHouse has a hostname mismatch (`*.prod.posthog.dev` cert vs `*.prod-us.posthog.dev` host) that prevents proper cert validation without infra changes

## Follow-up

After this merges and deploys, a charts PR is needed to remove `NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED: '0'` and `CLICKHOUSE_CA` from `apps/recording-api/values.yaml`.

## Test plan

- [ ] Verify recording-api connects to ClickHouse and block listings return data
- [ ] Verify other TLS connections (S3, Kafka) are unaffected
- [ ] Remove `NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0` from charts after deploy
author: TueHaulund

commits.message:
fix(replay): scope ClickHouse TLS skip to client only, revert CA cert approach

The ee/certs/ch.crt is a dev-only cert (CN=localhost) that doesn't work
in production. The prod ClickHouse uses a different CA (PostHog PROD CA)
and has a hostname mismatch (cert covers *.prod.posthog.dev but host is
*.prod-us.posthog.dev), so cert validation can't work without infra changes.

Instead of the process-wide NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0, use a scoped
https.Agent with rejectUnauthorized:false passed via @clickhouse/client's
http_agent option. This skips TLS verification only for ClickHouse, leaving
S3, Kafka, and other connections verified normally.

Reverts the CLICKHOUSE_CA config and Dockerfile cert copy from #51222.
fix: add keepAlive and maxSockets to custom https.Agent
fix: add nosemgrep for ClickHouse TLS skip
fix: move nosemgrep to matching line

title: fix(insights): set insights saved property to true by default
body:
## Problem

Creating insights via MCP or dashboard templates (probably more places?) does not explicitly pass the `saved=true`, so the insights default to `saved=false`.

This results in insights that aren't searchable, since search filters only include `saved=true`.


## Changes

The `saved` property is [legacy](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/pull/1379). Updating it to be `true` by default so we can prevent this bug in other areas where we miss passing `saved=true`.


## How did you test this code?

Manually + Added tests

title: fix(billing): refactor customer archetype pipeline to graph_asset
body: Replace the monolithic archetype_llm_classification + archetype_to_salesforce assets with a single graph_asset (archetype_classify_and_sync) that uses DynamicOutput to process accounts in batches of 1000. Each accounts batch classifies via LLM and pushes to Salesforce immediately, so partial progress survives failures. A Salesforce timestamp field (customer_archetype_classified_at__c) enables incremental processing on re-runs — accounts already pushed this week are skipped. This fixes the 413 Request Entity Too Large error caused by storing ~163K account classifications in Dagster metadata. ## Problem LLM classification asset completed but couldn't upload to SalesForce due to the payload size. I'm refactoring this to upload to SFDC in increments and persist a `customer_archetype_classified_at__c` to incrementally run the asset instead of relying on dagster metadata store ## Changes - graph_asset with 3 ops: prepare_and_fan_out, classify_and_push_accounts_batch, collect_results - Global semaphore for LLM gateway concurrency control (5 concurrent requests) - SF timestamp-based incremental skip instead of metadata hashes - ARCHETYPE_CSV_PATH env var for local development with exported data - SF failures logged as warnings (not raised) to preserve materialization ## How did you test this code? ~~TBC: I'm currently running the full pipeline locally, to make sure no trouble once deployed to cloud~~ I ran the full account set locally via llm-gateway - it succeeded in 2h41mins: 👉 _Stay up-to-date with [PostHog coding conventions](https://posthog.com/docs/contribute/coding-conventions) for a smoother review._ ## Publish to changelog? no ## Docs update
author: abhischekt

commits.message:
fix(billing): refactor customer archetype pipeline to graph_asset with incremental SF push

Replace the monolithic archetype_llm_classification + archetype_to_salesforce
assets with a single graph_asset (archetype_classify_and_sync) that uses
DynamicOutput to process accounts in batches of 1000.

Each accounts batch classifies via LLM and pushes to Salesforce immediately,
so partial progress survives failures. A Salesforce timestamp field
(customer_archetype_classified_at__c) enables incremental processing on
re-runs — accounts already pushed this week are skipped.

This fixes the 413 Request Entity Too Large error caused by storing ~163K
account classifications in Dagster metadata.

Key changes:
- graph_asset with 3 ops: prepare_and_fan_out, classify_and_push_accounts_batch,
  collect_results
- Global semaphore for LLM gateway concurrency control (5 concurrent requests)
- SF timestamp-based incremental skip instead of metadata hashes
- ARCHETYPE_CSV_PATH env var for local development with exported data
- SF failures logged as warnings (not raised) to preserve materialization

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: fix(storybook): disable source maps in CI to fix Cloudflare Pages OOM
body: ## Problem The Cloudflare Pages Storybook build sometimes fails at ~90% during webpack's `SourceMapDevToolPlugin` phase with `Resource temporarily unavailable (os error 11)` ([example](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/runs/68415816212)). The build generates source maps for hundreds of story bundles and exhausts the container's fixed resources. Do we want these sourcemaps in the production storybook deploy? 😓 Our CloudFlare build config does not use `--test` and by default will include sourcemaps: CF Pages has no option to increase build resources ([docs](https://developers.cloudflare.com/pages/platform/limits/)). ## Changes Disable source map generation in CI using Storybook's native [`build.test.disableSourcemaps`](https://storybook.js.org/docs/api/main-config/main-config-build) config, gated on `process.env.CI` (set automatically by CF Pages). Follows the same pattern already used for the webpack cache setting in `main.ts` line 32.
author: rnegron

commits.message: fix(storybook): disable source maps in CI to fix Cloudflare Pages OOM

title: feat: wire subscription workflows to new split-activity flow and remove tenacity retry
body:
## Problem

Follow up from: https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/pull/51648 which wires up the new subscription flow in favour of the old subscription flow.

## Changes

Please refer to the PR linked above for the hierarchical overview of the old v new architecture. This PR solely wires up the new flow and cleans up the code that is now no longer needed.

The key changes include:

- Replaced the monolithic `deliver_subscription_report_async` function with separate activities for asset creation and delivery
- Migrated from `subscription_scheduling_workflow.py` to separate `activities.py` and `workflows.py` files
- Updated import paths to use the new `types.py` module for shared data structures
- Removed retry logic from the Celery exporter since retries are now handled at the Temporal activity level
    - This will temporaly cause a regression for the "one off" exports, but since those are very visible to the user, I think this is acceptable for now. In the follow up PR in this stack, we will readd resiliency for those exports as well.
- Updated test expectations to reflect the new architecture where retries happen in Temporal rather than in-process

## How did you test this code?

✅ CI is passing (including the new tests)

✅ Manual subscriptions still work (both on new creation and on a schedule)

**New creation:**

![CleanShot 2026-03-23 at 11.20.08.png](https://app.graphite.com/user-attachments/assets/0d7e1081-df52-4645-b5bd-3865aab6aafb.png)

```

```

reviews.body: ## Pull request overview Adjusts `LemonTable`’s header loader rendering to prevent header/content layout shift during loading states (notably in Error Tracking issues list layouts), by ensuring the loader doesn’t introduce an extra header column. **Changes:** - Render the loader in its own `` row instead of as an additional `` in the main header row. - Compute a `colSpan` intended to match the visible header column count. - Add header CSS to make the loader row take up no space while still allowing the loader bar to be positioned/visible. ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 2 out of 2 changed files in this pull request and generated 1 comment. | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | frontend/src/lib/lemon-ui/LemonTable/LemonTable.tsx | Moves loader into a dedicated header row and adds `colSpan` calculation to avoid column-count changes while loading. | | frontend/src/lib/lemon-ui/LemonTable/LemonTable.scss | Styles the loader host header cell to have zero height/padding and no borders so it doesn’t affect layout. | --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. title: fix(llma): fix trace-not-found when clicking view trace from sentiment tab
body:
## Problem

Clicking "View trace" from the sentiment tab frequently shows "Trace not found." Removing the `timestamp` URL parameter makes the trace load correctly.

Two issues in how the sentiment tab constructs the trace deep-link:

1. **Timezone mismatch**: The raw ClickHouse timestamp (UTC, no timezone indicator) was passed directly. `dayjs()` treated it as local time and the backend's `relative_date_parse` treated it as project timezone, shifting the lookup window by the timezone offset.
2. **Wrong timestamp reference**: The sentiment query used `max(ts)` (latest event in trace) for the link, but the trace query's `_map_results` filter checks `first_timestamp >= date_from`. Since `first_timestamp` (earliest event) is always before `max(ts)`, the trace was filtered out.

## Changes

- Added `min(ts) as created_at` to the sentiment generations SQL query to capture the trace's first event timestamp
- Changed the "View trace" link to use `getTraceTimestamp(createdAt)` instead of the raw `timestamp`
- This matches the pattern used by the traces table, sessions scene, and all other trace link callers in LLM analytics

## How did you test this code?

Verified the fix aligns with the existing `getTraceTimestamp` pattern used across all other trace link callers (traces table, sessions scene, trace scene tree navigation). The function properly converts to UTC with "Z" suffix and subtracts a 5-minute buffer.

## Publish to changelog?

No
author: andrewm4894

reviews.body:
### 💡 Codex Review Here are some automated review suggestions for this pull request. **Reviewed commit:** `2813df34cc`
ℹ️ About Codex in GitHub
[Your team has set up Codex to review pull requests in this repo](http://chatgpt.com/codex/settings/general). Reviews are triggered when you - Open a pull request for review - Mark a draft as ready - Comment "@codex review". If Codex has suggestions, it will comment; otherwise it will react with 👍. Codex can also answer questions or update the PR. Try commenting "@codex address that feedback".

commits.message:
fix(llma): fix trace-not-found when clicking view trace from sentiment tab

The sentiment tab was passing the raw ClickHouse timestamp (UTC without
timezone indicator) and using max(ts) (latest event) instead of min(ts)
(first event). This caused two issues:

1. Timezone: dayjs and the backend treated the bare timestamp as local/
   project time, shifting the lookup window by the timezone offset.
2. Window position: max(ts) is always after the trace's first_timestamp,
   so the TraceQueryRunner's date range filter rejected the trace.

Fix: add min(ts) as created_at to the sentiment SQL query and use
getTraceTimestamp(createdAt) for the trace link — matching the pattern
used by the traces table, sessions, and all other trace link callers.

title: feat(feature-flags): gate realtime cohort membership on backfill timestamp
body:
## Problem

Realtime and behavioral cohorts were being resolved via the `cohort_membership` table even when no backfill had populated it yet. This could lead to incorrect membership lookups — a cohort that hasn't been backfilled would return empty results from the membership table instead of falling through to dynamic filter evaluation.

## Changes

- Add `last_backfill_person_properties_at: Option<DateTime<Utc>>` to the `Cohort` struct
- Update `uses_realtime_membership()` to require both a realtime/behavioral cohort type AND a populated backfill timestamp
- Update the SQL query to select the new column
- Expand tests to cover both with-timestamp and without-timestamp cases

## How did you test this code?

- Updated and expanded unit tests for `uses_realtime_membership` covering all cohort types with and without backfill timestamps
- All test fixture construction sites updated for the new field
- `cargo fmt`, `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings` pass clean

## Publish to changelog?

No

## Docs update

No docs changes needed.
author: dmarticus

reviews.body:
Nice! I guess adding an integration test would would make sense here, something that answers the question: "when a Realtime cohort has no backfill timestamp, does it actually fall through to dynamic filter evaluation and produce the correct flag result e2e?"

Something like:
1. Insert a Realtime cohort (with person-property filters) and `last_backfill_person_properties_at = None`
2. Insert a person whose properties match those filters
3. Create a flag targeting that cohort
4. Evaluate the flag and assert it matches (proving the dynamic eval fallback works)

What do you think?

commits.message:
feat(feature-flags): gate realtime cohort membership on backfill timestamp

Realtime and behavioral cohorts now require a populated
last_backfill_person_properties_at timestamp before using the
cohort_membership table. Without it, the cohort falls through to
dynamic filter evaluation. This prevents incorrect membership
lookups for cohorts that haven't been backfilled yet.
refactor(feature-flags): consolidate realtime membership test into single parameterized loop

chore(feature-flags): add test for realtime cohort ID filtering logic

Verify that filtering a list of cohorts by uses_realtime_membership()
selects only Realtime/Behavioral cohorts with a populated backfill
timestamp, matching the filter applied in prepare_flag_evaluation_data.

feat(feature-flags): add integration test for realtime cohort dynamic eval fallback

Insert a Realtime cohort with person-property filters but no backfill
timestamp, a person matching those filters, and a flag targeting the
cohort. Verify the flag evaluates to true, proving that cohorts without

title: fix(rbac): enforce object-level permissions on detail actions
body:
## Problem

Several `@action(detail=True)` endpoints on viewsets that use `AccessControlViewSetMixin` bypass object-level permission checks. They fetch objects via manual queryset lookups (`Model.objects.filter(...)`, `get_object_or_404(...)`) instead of calling `self.get_object()`, which means `AccessControlPermission.has_object_permission()` is never invoked. A user with RBAC restrictions (e.g., `access_level="none"`) on a specific resource can still access these detail actions.

Affected endpoints:
- `DashboardsViewSet.move_tile`
- `FeatureFlagViewSet.activity`
- `InsightViewSet.activity`
- `SurveyViewSet.activity`
- `SessionRecordingViewSet.summarize` (cache check before permission check)

## Changes

- Call `self.get_object()` (which triggers `check_object_permissions`) instead of manual queryset lookups
- Move `self.get_object()` before the cache check in `SessionRecordingViewSet.summarize` so permissions are enforced on cached responses
- Add tests for each fixed endpoint

## How did you test this code?

- New integration tests for each endpoint verify that a user with `access_level="none"` gets 403 on both the standard retrieve and the previously-broken detail action

## Publish to changelog?

No
author: feliperalmeida

reviews.body:
## Pull request overview

This PR closes RBAC permission bypasses on several `@action(detail=True)` endpoints by ensuring object-level permission checks run for detail actions (and cached responses), and adds regression tests to prevent recurrence.

**Changes:**
- Replace manual object lookups with `self.get_object()` in multiple detail actions so `check_object_permissions`/`has_object_permission` is enforced.
- Ensure `SessionRecordingViewSet.summarize` runs `self.get_object()` before cache reads to prevent permission bypass via cached responses.
- Add integration tests asserting users with `access_level="none"` get 403 on both `retrieve` and the previously-bypassing detail action endpoints.

title: feat(flags): preload referenced cohorts in flags hypercache
body:
## Problem

The Rust feature-flags service queries PostgreSQL via `CohortCacheManager` for every team's cohorts on each `/flags` request, even though the cohort definitions rarely change. This adds unnecessary latency and PG load, especially for teams with many cohort-based flags.

## Changes

Preloads cohort definitions (including transitive dependencies) into the flags hypercache at cache-write time so the Rust service can skip the separate PG query.

**Python (cache write path):**
- Extracts cohort IDs from active flag filters (`groups` only — `super_groups` and `holdout_groups` cannot contain cohort properties)
- BFS-loads referenced cohorts with transitive cohort-on-cohort dependency resolution (depth limit 20, cycle-safe)
- Serializes cohorts into the hypercache payload alongside flags and evaluation metadata
- Batch path collects all cohort IDs across teams and loads in a single BFS pass
- Adds `post_save`/`post_delete` signal on `Cohort` to invalidate the flags cache on definition changes (skips recalculation-only saves via `_COHORT_RECALCULATION_FIELDS` subset check)

**Rust (cache read path):**
- `HypercacheFlagsWrapper` gains optional `cohorts` field with `#[serde(default)]` for backwards compatibility
- `FeatureFlagMatcher` consumes preloaded cohorts via `Option::take()`, falls back to `CohortCacheManager` when absent
- `EvaluationMetadata` gains `Default` and `PartialEq` derives

**Rolling deploy safety:**
- Old Rust ignores the unknown `cohorts` key (no `deny_unknown_fields`)
- New Rust reading old cache entries defaults `cohorts` to `None` and falls back to PG

## How did you test this code?

- 20 new Python tests across 4 test classes: cohort extraction, BFS loading (transitive deps, circular deps, depth limit), serialization (all 16 fields), signal handler filtering
- 2 new Rust tests for hypercache cohort deserialization
- Rust test helper updated to propagate cohorts through round-trip

### Test plan

The automated unit tests cover extraction, BFS loading, serialization, and signal handler logic. This plan focuses on end-to-end scenarios that exercise the full Python → Redis → Rust pipeline.

#### Prerequisites

- [x] PostHog dev environment running (`hogli start`)
- [x] Rust feature-flags service running and connected to the same Redis and Postgres
- [x] Test team created with cohorts (direct, transitive, circular, unreferenced) and flags referencing them

#### 1. Python: cache write path

- [x] `_get_feature_flags_for_service` returns `flags`, `evaluation_metadata`, and `cohorts`
- [x] Only referenced cohorts included (unreferenced cohort excluded)
- [x] Transitive deps loaded (flag → cohort B → cohort A loads both)
- [x] Circular cohort dependencies (D ↔ E) terminate without infinite loop
- [x] BFS depth limit enforced (chain of 25 cohorts, loader returned only 20)
- [x] Batch path isolates cohorts per team (no cross-team leakage)
- [x] Log messages include `flag_count` and `cohort_count`

#### 2. Python: signal handler (cache invalidation)

- [x] Recalculation-only saves (`is_calculating`) do NOT trigger cache invalidation
- [x] Definition changes (`name`) DO trigger cache invalidation
- [x] Mixed fields (`count` + `name`) DO trigger invalidation
- [x] Cohort deletion triggers invalidation via `post_delete`
- [x] Signal uses `transaction.on_commit`, not direct task call
- [x] No-op when `FLAGS_REDIS_URL` is not configured

#### 3. Rust: flag evaluation with preloaded cohorts

- [x] Preloaded cohort path: `/flags` with cohorts in cache evaluates correctly (cohort flags: `no_condition_match`, circular: `dependency_cycle_cohort`, plain: `condition_match`)
- [x] Fallback path: cache without `cohorts` key falls back to `CohortCacheManager`, identical results

#### 4. Rolling deploy safety

- [x] Old Rust (master) reads new cache (with `cohorts`): silently ignores unknown key, correct results
- [x] New Rust (branch) reads old cache (without `cohorts`): falls back to PG, correct results

## Publish to changelog?

no
author: haacked

reviews.body:
## Pull request overview

This PR reduces `/flags` request latency and Postgres load in the Rust feature-flags service by preloading only the cohort definitions actually referenced by a team’s active feature flags (including transitive cohort-on-cohort dependencies) into the flags HyperCache payload written by Django.

**Changes:**
- **Python:** extracts referenced cohort IDs from active flag `filters.groups`, BFS-loads cohorts + transitive dependencies (depth-limited), serializes them into the flags hypercache payload, and adds cohort-change signals to invalidate the flags cache.
- **Rust:** extends the hypercache wrapper to optionally deserialize `cohorts` (backwards compatible), threads cohorts through fetch/filter flows, and consumes preloaded cohorts in `FeatureFlagMatcher` (falling back to `CohortCacheManager` when absent).
- **Tests:** adds/updates Python unit tests for extraction/loading/serialization + signal filtering, and Rust tests for cohort deserialization and round-trip helpers.

### Reviewed changes

Copilot reviewed 12 out of 12 changed files in this pull request and generated 3 comments.

<details>
<summary>Show a summary per file</summary>

| File | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| rust/feature-flags/src/utils/test_graph_utils.rs | Updates test builders to include the new `cohorts` field in the wrapper/list structures. |
| rust/feature-flags/src/handler/tests.rs | Updates handler tests to include `cohorts: None` in constructed wrapper/list values. |
| rust/feature-flags/src/handler/flags.rs | Plumbs `cohorts` through `fetch_and_filter` so downstream evaluation can use it. |
| rust/feature-flags/src/handler/billing.rs | Updates billing tests for the expanded `FeatureFlagList` shape. |
| rust/feature-flags/src/flags/test_helpers.rs | Ensures redis/hypercache test helpers round-trip `cohorts` alongside flags + metadata. |
| rust/feature-flags/src/flags/test_flag_matching.rs | Updates flag matching tests to include `cohorts: None`. |
| r

commits.message:
feat(flags): preload referenced cohorts in flags hypercache

Adds cohort definitions to the feature flags hypercache so the Rust
feature-flags service can skip a separate CohortCacheManager PostgreSQL
query per request. Python computes referenced cohorts (including
transitive dependencies via BFS) at cache-write time and includes them in
the Redis payload. Rust deserializes the preloaded cohorts and uses them
with a graceful fallback to the existing PG path when absent.

Key changes:
- Extract cohort IDs from flag filters (groups + super_groups)
- BFS load cohorts with transitive deps (depth limit 20, cycle safe)
- Serialize cohorts into hypercache payload alongside flags
- Batch path collects all cohort IDs across teams, loads once with team_id__in
- Rust: optional cohorts field on HypercacheFlagsWrapper (#[serde(default)])
- Rust: FeatureFlagMatcher consumes preloaded cohorts via take(), falls back to CohortCacheManager
- Signal handler invalidates flags cache on cohort definition changes (skips recalculation-only saves)

fix(flags): improve cohort preloading observability and test coverage

Fix holdout key name in docstring and tighten BFS depth-limit test
assertion to match actual implementation bound of 20 cohorts.

title: feat(experiments): respect team flags_persistence_default when creating experiments
body:
## Problem

When a team has "ensure continuity" (`flags_persistence_default`) enabled in project settings, new feature flags automatically default to persisting across authentication steps. However, experiments ignored this setting — their feature flags always defaulted `ensure_experience_continuity` to `false`.

## Changes

- **Backend**: `ExperimentService._ensure_feature_flag()` now falls back to `self.team.flags_persistence_default` when `ensure_experience_continuity` is not explicitly provided in experiment parameters.
- **Frontend**: `VariantsPanelCreateFeatureFlag` reads `currentTeam?.flags_persistence_default` from `teamLogic` so the persistence checkbox starts checked when the team setting is enabled.

## Testing

- Extended `test_creating_experiment_with_ensure_experience_continuity` to verify:
  - Team default `True` is applied when not explicitly provided
  - Explicit `False` overrides the team default
 - Also tested locally that on/off setting behaved as expected

standalone feature flags.

title: chore(flags): migrate super_groups flags key (phase 1)
body:
Part of #47929 

## Summary

Phase 1 of the `super_groups` → `feature_enrollment` migration. This PR adds dual-writing so that every place that writes `super_groups` into feature flag filters now also writes `feature_enrollment: true` (or clears it alongside `super_groups`).

This is a backwards-compatible change, since no code reads `feature_enrollment` yet. The new field is purely additive, preparing for Phase 2 where the flag service will start reading it.

### Changes by file

**`posthog/api/documentation.py`**
- Added `feature_enrollment` boolean field to `FeatureFlagFiltersSchemaDef` so it appears in the OpenAPI spec.

**`posthog/models/feature_flag/feature_flag.py`**
- Added `has_feature_enrollment` property that reads `filters.get("feature_enrollment", False)`. Existing `super_conditions` property kept for now (legacy readers still active).

**`products/early_access_features/backend/api.py`**
- All 5 write sites in `EarlyAccessFeatureSerializer` now dual-write:
  - Promote to active stage (update) → sets `feature_enrollment: True`
  - GA rollout_to_all → clears `feature_enrollment`
  - Demote/archive → clears `feature_enrollment`
  - Create with active stage (existing flag + new flag) → sets `feature_enrollment: True`
  - Destroy → clears `feature_enrollment`

**`products/early_access_features/backend/apps.py`**
- `_pre_delete` hook now also clears `feature_enrollment` when clearing `super_groups`.

**`posthog/api/feature_flag.py`**
- Added comment to `_update_super_groups_for_key_change` noting that `feature_enrollment` is key-independent and doesn't need updating on flag key rename.

**`products/early_access_features/backend/test/test_early_access_feature.py`**
- All relevant tests now assert `feature_enrollment` presence/absence alongside `super_groups` assertions.

## Test plan

- [x] All 28 early access feature tests pass
- [x] `test_updating_feature_flag_key_updates_super_groups` passes
author: patricio-posthog

reviews.body: LGTM!

Some logic used the status calculation which in the future might become more complex or get cached, i.e. it's not a reliable source of truth. This PR flips it around and use the helpers as the source of truth.

- Let lifecycle helpers check for field properties directly (i.e. act as source of truth)
- Replace static `compute_status` with instance based `computed_status`

- Cleanup unreachable code

- Test suite

No

Makes sense to improve this! Needed some extra logic in the bootstrap phase.

An alternative I had in mind here which might be cleaner is to only rely on the `status` field for all these helpers, and only on the write path we need to validate that status and dates are in sync. So f.ex `is_running` is simply checking that status is running and doesn't have care about any dates (status is source of truth. That said, haven't thought it fully through, so might be missing something with that).

title: fix: add admin permission checks to billing-modifying endpoints
body:
## Problem

Member-level Vercel SSO users can call billing-modifying endpoints (activate, deactivate, patch limits, etc.). PostHog's `BillingViewset` had no role checks, so these requests pass through to the billing service which rejects them with 403 "permission_denied". This surfaces as a confusing 500 to the user. 132 occurrences in the last 7 days.

## Changes

Added `IsOrganizationAdmin` permission to all billing-modifying actions:
- `activate`, `deactivate`, `patch`, `subscription_switch_plan`, `portal`
- `purchase_credits`, `activate_trial`, `cancel_trial`
- `claim_coupon`, `apply_startup_program`

Read-only endpoints (`list`, `get_invoices`, `credits_overview`, `coupons_overview`) remain accessible to all users.

Also removed redundant inline admin checks from `apply_startup_program` and `claim_coupon` since the permission class now handles this.

## How did you test this?

- [x] 14 new tests in `TestBillingPermissionDeniedForMembers` - 10 verifying 403 for members on protected endpoints, 4 confirming read-only access is preserved
- [x] All existing billing tests pass

## Changelog

No - bug fix, not a new feature.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: MattBro

Member-level Vercel SSO users could call billing-modifying endpoints

these endpoints check for admin/owner role upfront and return a proper
403.

Read-only endpoints (list, get_invoices, credits_overview) remain
accessible to all authenticated users.

title: fix: add cross-region proxy support to VercelProxyViewSet
body:
## Problem

The billing service calls `POST /api/vercel/proxy/` to forward usage/invoice data to Vercel. It routes based on `customer.license_id` (EU customers go to `eu.posthog.com`, US to `us.posthog.com`). But the proxy looks up `OrganizationIntegration` only in the local region's DB. If a customer's integration is in the other region, the proxy returns 404, causing "Vercel API call failed" errors (44+37+4 occurrences in the last week).

Other Vercel viewsets use `VercelRegionProxyMixin` for cross-region routing, but `VercelProxyViewSet` doesn't because it uses `BillingServiceAuthentication` (organization_id in JWT) rather than Vercel auth (installation_id in headers).

## Changes

- When integration not found locally and current region is US, proxy the full request (with auth headers and body) to `eu.posthog.com/api/vercel/proxy/`
- If EU also returns 404 or the proxy fails, fall through to the original 404
- 6 new tests covering proxy success, EU 404 fallthrough, network errors, region guards, and header forwarding

## How did you test this?

- [x] `pytest ee/api/vercel/test/test_vercel_proxy.py -v` - all 26 tests pass (14 existing + 6 new)

## Changelog

No - bug fix for existing integration behavior.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: MattBro

commits.message:
fix: add cross-region proxy support to VercelProxyViewSet

When the billing service calls POST /api/vercel/proxy/ and the
OrganizationIntegration isn't found locally, proxy the request to EU
(if current region is US). This handles the case where a customer's
integration lives in a different region than their license_id routes to.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

- Read US_DOMAIN/EU_DOMAIN from settings at runtime instead of

parameterized.expand + class-level @patch have arg ordering issues.

title: fix(test): increase jest.setTimeout to 45s in postgres-parity tests
body:
<!-- mendral:session:action:01KK9DQ1GXJ4KJCMZRB6QY6F26 -->
## Summary

- Increases `jest.setTimeout` from 30s to 45s in `postgres-parity.test.ts` to give 15s of headroom above the `delayUntilEventIngested` polling budget (300 × 100ms = 30s).

## Context

Fixes a race condition introduced when PR #50006 reduced `maxDelayCount` from 1000→300 (capping the polling budget at 30s) without increasing `jest.setTimeout` to match. When Kafka/ClickHouse ingestion is slow, the polling exhausts its full 30s budget at the exact moment Jest's timeout fires — Jest wins the race and throws the opaque `Exceeded timeout of 30000 ms` error instead of `delayUntilEventIngested`'s actionable `Failed to get data in time` error.

26 occurrences confirmed, most recent on [run 23496622232](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/actions/runs/23496622232/job/68379502748) (Mar 24 15:23 UTC). Passing runs also show suite durations of 37–39s, meaning they only pass on the retry attempt — the race condition is structural.

With 45s:
- `delayUntilEventIngested` can exhaust its 30s budget and throw a meaningful error before Jest fires
- Worst case per test: 2 × 45s = 90s (with `jest.retryTimes(1)`)
- Worst case total: 4 tests × 90s = 360s — well within CI job timeout

Relates to insight: *postgres-parity tests timeout at 30s due to Jest timeout == delayUntilEventIngested budget*


---

> [!NOTE]
> Created by [Mendral](https://mendral.com). Tag @mendral-app with feedback or questions.
>
> - [View implementation session](https://app.mendral.com/sessions/action:01KK9DQ1GXJ4KJCMZRB6QY6F26)
> - Addresses [this insight](https://app.mendral.com/insights/01KK9DQ1GQK9BN92JKZ0HKH9YS)
author: mendral-app

commits.message: fix(test): increase jest.setTimeout to 45s in postgres-parity tests
fix(test): add inline comment explaining jest.setTimeout(45000) rationale

title: fix(flags): fix cohort version update
body:
## Problem

A customer reported their cohort showed stale data ("no new people since a day ago") despite `last_calculation` being recent. Removing and re-adding a filter fixed it by forcing a version bump.

The probable root cause: `calculate_people_ch` updates the Postgres `Cohort.version` **after** the `try/finally` block. If `self.save()` or `_safe_reset_calculating_state()` in the `finally` block raises, the version update is silently skipped. ClickHouse has the new version's data, but Postgres still points to the old version, so all queries return stale results.

## Changes

Moved the `Cohort.objects.filter(...).update(version=..., count=...)` call from after the `try/finally` block into the `try` block, right after `recalculate_cohortpeople` succeeds. This ensures the version is persisted before the `finally` block runs, so exceptions there can no longer silently skip it.

- Renamed `update_fields` to `version_update_fields` to avoid confusion with the `self.save(update_fields=...)` call in `finally`
- Added a test that mocks `_safe_reset_calculating_state` to raise and verifies version is still updated

The concurrency guard (`version__lt=pending_version | version__isnull=True`) is preserved, lower versions still can't overwrite higher ones.

**Tradeoff:** If the version `.update()` DB call itself raises, `errors_calculating` is incremented even though ClickHouse data was written. This is visible and self-healing (next retry succeeds), far better than the current silent split-brain.

## How did you test this code?

- New unit test `test_calculate_people_ch_updates_version_even_when_finally_raises`
- Existing cohort tests verified for regressions
- `ruff check` and `ruff format` pass
author: matheus-vb

reviews.body: ## Pull request overview This PR fixes a cohort “split-brain” failure mode where ClickHouse cohortpeople can be recalculated for a new version, but Postgres `Cohort.version` stays stale if an exception is raised in the `finally` cleanup path—causing downstream queries to keep reading the old version. **Changes:** - Move the Postgres `version`/`count` update into the `try` block immediately after a successful `recalculate_cohortpeople` call. - Rename the local dict used for the `.update(...)` call to avoid confusion with `self.save(update_fields=...)`. - Add a regression test ensuring `version` and `count` are persisted even when `finally` raises (via mocking `save` / `_safe_reset_calculating_state`). ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 2 out of 2 changed files in this pull request and generated no comments. | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | `posthog/models/cohort/cohort.py` | Ensures `Cohort.version`/`count` are persisted before `finally` cleanup runs, preventing silent stale-version reads. | | `posthog/test/test_cohort_model.py` | Adds coverage for the failure mode where `finally` raises but version/count must still update. | --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.
Nice fix! One non-blocking suggestion inline.
