title: fix(billing): Turn usage signals for SFDC into an incremental pipeline
body: 1. StartToClose timeout: The fetch_salesforce_org_ids_activity had a 2-minute timeout that was too short for ~280k org mappings as data volume grew. 2. Event loop blocking: Gzip decompression and JSON parsing of large Redis payloads (~280k entries) ran on the async event loop, blocking heartbeats and causing Temporal to consider the activity dead. 3. Unbounded gRPC payloads: The entire org-mappings dataset was passed through Temporal's gRPC layer as a single activity result, risking the 4 MB limit. Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) ## Problem Fix an error with the [usage_enrichment data pipeline](https://cloud.temporal.io/namespaces/posthog-prod-us.usz2o/workflows/salesforce-usage-enrichment-schedule-2026-03-29T06%3A00%3A00Z/019d382d-98f9-71ff-88ec-f24bab75ab9c/timeline) ## Changes - Paginated workflow with Continue-As-New: Replace the monolithic fetch-then-process loop with a page-at-a-time approach. Each workflow execution processes one page of org mappings via enrich_org_page_activity, then calls continue_as_new to keep event history bounded. - Redis List for org mappings: Switch from a single gzip-compressed blob to a Redis List (RPUSH/LRANGE) so pages can be read via LRANGE without decompressing the entire dataset. Uses pipelined EXISTS + LRANGE to avoid TOCTOU races. - Consolidated activity: Merge the old fetch_salesforce_org_ids_activity and update_salesforce_usage_activity into a single enrich_org_page_activity that reads from Redis, aggregates signals, and updates Salesforce — keeping all heavy data inside the activity and off the gRPC layer. - Thread-offloaded decompression: Move gzip.decompress + json.loads into asyncio.to_thread to avoid blocking the event loop (still used for the accounts cache path). - Timing instrumentation: Add time.monotonic measurements for Redis reads to aid future diagnosis. - Bump timeouts: Cache activity → 10 min, enrich page activity → 30 min with 5-min heartbeat. ## How did you test this code? - added unit tests - ran pipeline locally (not fully completed) 👉 _Stay up-to-date with [PostHog coding conventions](https://posthog.com/docs/contribute/coding-conventions) for a smoother review._ ## Publish to changelog? no ## Docs update
author: abhischekt

reviews.body: Couple of comments

commits.message:
fix(billing): resolve fetch_salesforce_org_ids_activity StartToClose timeout

Offload gzip decompression and JSON parsing to a thread to avoid
blocking the event loop on large Redis payloads. Increase the activity
timeout from 2 to 5 minutes to accommodate growing data volume. Add
timing instrumentation for diagnosing future slowdowns and test coverage
for the fetch activity including malformed cache entries.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

chore(billing): address code review feedback for usage enrichment fix

Rename parse_duration_ms to mapping_duration_ms to accurately reflect
that it times dataclass instantiation, not JSON parsing. Consolidate
duplicate malformed cache entry tests into a single parameterized loop.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix(billing): paginate usage enrichment with Continue-As-New and Redis List

Root cause: fetch_salesforce_org_ids_activity returned all 280K org
mappings (~38 MB serialized) which exceeded Temporal's 4 MB gRPC message
limit, causing the activity to silently fail on result delivery and
eventually timeout.

Key changes:
- Replace single fetch activity with enrich_org_page_activity that reads
  from Redis internally — no mapping data passes through Temporal
- Use Continue-As-New to process one page (10K orgs) per workflow
  execution, keeping event history bounded
- Switch org mappings Redis storage from compressed blob to Redis List
  (RPUSH/LRANGE) for O(N) page reads without full decompression
- Add heartbeat progress reporting (processed/total/updated)
- Cap error list at 10 across Continue-As-New executions

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: feat(hogql): support table column aliases across all dialects
body:
## Problem

HogQL blocked table column aliases (e.g. `FROM events AS e(a, b, c)`) in non-postgres dialects with "table column aliases are not allowed in {dialect}". This prevented using a standard SQL feature for renaming columns at the table binding level.

## Changes

Introduces `ColumnAliasedTableType` — a new AST type that changes a table binding's visible schema when column aliases are provided. This properly implements SQL table column alias semantics:

- `e.a` resolves correctly through the aliased binding (maps to `uuid` internally)
- `e.uuid` fails — original names are hidden when aliased
- `SELECT *` expands to aliased names
- `WHERE e.c IS NOT NULL` works
- Fewer aliases than columns: remaining columns keep their original names

**Dialect-specific printing:**
- **Postgres:** uses aliased names (`e.a`) since postgres handles `(a, b, c)` natively
- **ClickHouse/HogQL:** uses real DB column names (`e.uuid`) since these dialects don't support the syntax
- **Subqueries:** column aliases are baked into the inner SELECT as `AS` aliases for non-postgres

**Additional fixes:**
- Alias-name collision: `FROM events AS e(event)` no longer corrupts the mapping when an alias name matches an existing column
- Silent alias drop: `FROM events(a, b, c)` without explicit `AS` now works
- `ExpressionField`s accessed through column aliases get `isolate_scope=True` to avoid broken references
- Added `ColumnAliasedTableType` to all `isinstance(x, TableAliasType)` checks across the codebase (resolver, resolver_utils, lazy_tables, clickhouse printer, timestamp_visitor, where_clause_extractor)

## How did you test this code?

- 20 new printer tests across ClickHouse and postgres dialects covering: explicit refs, WHERE clauses, unqualified refs, `SELECT *`, original name rejection, subquery alias baking, arg validation errors
- 6 new resolver tests covering: type creation, remaining column behavior, original name rejection, star expansion, unqualified resolution, excess aliases
- Manually verified queries like `SELECT e.a FROM events AS e(a, b, c) WHERE e.c IS NOT NULL`

## Publish to changelog?

no

## 🤖 LLM context

Agent-authored PR. Co-authored with Claude Code (Opus 4.6).
author: georgemunyoro

reviews.body:
## Pull request overview

Adds first-class support for SQL table column alias lists (`FROM t AS x(a,b,...)`) across HogQL dialects by introducing a new table-type wrapper that remaps visible column names while preserving correct resolution and dialect-specific printing.

**Changes:**
- Introduced `ColumnAliasedTableType` to model renamed table schemas during type resolution.
- Updated resolver/printers/visitors/utilities to treat `ColumnAliasedTableType` as alias-like and print correctly per dialect (bake aliases into subqueries for non-Postgres).
- Added resolver + printer tests covering alias resolution, `SELECT *`, original-name hiding, and error cases.

### Reviewed changes

Copilot reviewed 11 out of 11 changed files in this pull request and generated 3 comments.

<details>
<summary>Show a summary per file</summary>

| File | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| posthog/hogql/ast.py | Adds `ColumnAliasedTableType` and maps aliased field names back to original DB column names during resolution. |
| posthog/hogql/resolver.py | Constructs `ColumnAliasedTableType`, adjusts `*` expansion, and bakes subquery column aliases for non-Postgres dialects. |
| posthog/hogql/printer/base.py | Updates join/field printing to understand `ColumnAliasedTableType` and only emit `(a,b,c)` syntax for Postgres. |
| posthog/hogql/printer/clickhouse.py | Treats `ColumnAliasedTableType` as alias-like when walking through underlying tables. |
| posthog/hogql/visitor.py | Adds visitor traversal support for `ColumnAliasedTableType`. |
| posthog/hogql/resolver_utils.py | Treats `ColumnAliasedTableType` as alias-like for table discovery. |
| posthog/hogql/transforms/lazy_tables.py | Extends alias-like checks to include `ColumnAliasedTableType` in lazy join/table logic. |
| posthog/hogql/helpers/timestamp_visitor.py | Treats `ColumnAliasedTableType` as alias-like when inspecting timestamp fields. |
| posthog/hogql/database/schema/util/where_clause_extractor.py | Treats `ColumnAlia

Cool stuff! 🙌 

And as always, check what the robots had to say...

commits.message:
feat(hogql): convert table column aliases to basic aliases instead of blocking

Add ColumnAliasedTableType that changes the table binding's visible
schema when column aliases are provided (e.g. FROM events AS e(a, b, c)).
Aliased names become the only visible columns through that binding,
with remaining unaliased columns keeping their original names.

For ClickHouse/HogQL dialects, the printer uses the real DB column names
since these dialects don't support the (a, b, c) syntax. For Postgres,
the aliased names are used since postgres handles renaming natively.

For subqueries, column aliases are baked into the inner SELECT as AS
aliases for non-postgres dialects.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix(hogql): fix column alias edge cases and add missing isinstance checks

- Fix alias-name collision: when an alias name matches an existing
  column name (e.g. FROM events AS e(event)), the identity mapping
  no longer overwrites the alias entry
- Fix silent alias drop: FROM events(a, b, c) without explicit AS
  now correctly creates ColumnAliasedTableType
- Add ColumnAliasedTableType to all isinstance checks for
  TableAliasType across the codebase: resolver, resolver_utils,
  lazy_tables, clickhouse printer, timestamp_visitor, and
  where_clause_extractor

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: feat(sql): copy as markdown
body:
## Problem

Building this for _**me**_! And hopefully some others too.. Typically when dealing with support requests it's really helpful to share the results of a SQL query at the time of writing. The best way to share small snippets of data tends to be in the format of a markdown table.

This is an export feature of TablePlus that I always really valued, so would be nice to bring this to PostHog

## Changes

Adds a new method to copy data as markdown, which flattens and escapes requisite characters to make a markdown table. This is based on the same table extraction as we use for CSV so that the data is flattened in the right way for a simple table view.

The value copied to the clipboard would look like this, noting the header `a|b` has the pipe escaped:

```table
| a\|b                                 | created_at                  | event                |
| ------------------------------------ | --------------------------- | -------------------- |
| 019ca6b5-3074-0000-2158-87da4bda20bc | 2026-03-01T00:03:38.228896Z | $autocapture         |
| 019ca774-cc5e-0000-5d43-b5e07887068c | 2026-03-01T03:32:55.518299Z | $autocapture         |
| 019ca76b-ed43-0000-ad25-730b45f9d19a | 2026-03-01T03:23:14.115933Z | $feature_flag_called |
| 019ca725-12c3-0000-e995-732da5dbad96 | 2026-03-01T02:05:50.659183Z | $feature_flag_called |
| 019ca6c3-5569-0000-9d71-81000f41c845 | 2026-03-01T00:19:05.193179Z | $feature_flag_called |
```

Whilst the columns don't need to be padded, it make reading it much easier in places where markdown is not supported, but monospaced values are.

## How did you test this code?

Tested locally and in unit testing

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/9b6766f4-aafd-4f9b-ad77-0a65aad36038

## Publish to changelog?

title: feat(llma): add ID-based summarization and enable MCP tool
body:
## Problem

The summarization endpoint requires callers to pass the full trace/event data blob. This makes it unusable from MCP tools, where agents only have trace or generation IDs from previous exploration (clusters, traces list, etc).

## Changes

**Backend** (`products/llm_analytics/backend/api/summarization.py`):
- Extend `SummarizeRequestSerializer` with optional `trace_id`, `generation_id`, `date_from`, `date_to` fields
- Add cross-field validation: provide either `data` or one of the IDs, not both
- `summarize_type` is now optional — inferred from which ID field is provided
- Add `_fetch_trace_data()` — uses `TraceQueryRunner` to fetch trace, then `llm_trace_to_formatter_format` to convert to formatter input
- Add `_fetch_generation_data()` — HogQL query to fetch a single `$ai_generation` event by UUID
- Update `create()` to use fetch methods when IDs are provided, falling back to existing `data` path

**MCP** (`products/llm_analytics/mcp/tools.yaml`):
- Enable `llm-analytics-summarization-create` with description focused on the ID-based usage

Fully backward compatible — existing callers using the `data` field are unaffected.

## How did you test this code?

Agent-authored PR. Backend logic changes need test coverage — the existing tests in `test_summarization.py` test the data-blob path. New tests should cover:
- [ ] `trace_id` path: fetches trace and returns summary
- [ ] `generation_id` path: fetches generation and returns summary
- [ ] Validation: rejects when both `data` and `trace_id` provided
- [ ] Validation: rejects when neither `data` nor ID provided
- [ ] 404 when trace/generation not found in date range

## Publish to changelog?

No

## 🤖 LLM context

Agent-authored. This is a companion to #53107 (clustering tools) and #53113 (sentiment tool). The ID-based summarization completes the MCP workflow: explore clusters → find traces → summarize them — all without the agent needing to pass large data blobs.

Key design decision: extend the existing endpoint rather than creating a new one, so the same MCP tool operation ID works for both usage patterns.

title: feat: add LLM traces list query example and improve trace discovery docs
body:
## Problem

The LLM traces documentation lacked a concrete example for querying multiple traces with aggregated metrics, and the guidance on discovering trace properties was incomplete. Users needed clearer instructions on how to find and filter traces using the available tools and schema discovery.

## Changes

- **Added `example-llm-traces-list.md`**: New reference document with a two-phase SQL query pattern for efficiently listing multiple LLM traces with aggregated latency, token usage, costs, and error counts. Includes clear guidance on omitting large content fields and directing users to the single trace query for detailed data.

- **Converted `example-llm-trace.md` to template**: Migrated the single trace query to a Jinja2 template (`example-llm-trace.md.j2`) that uses the `render_hogql_example()` helper to generate HogQL dynamically based on trace ID and date range parameters.

- **Enhanced `exploring-llm-traces/SKILL.md`**: 
  - Added critical guidance on schema discovery before constructing filters (call `posthog:read-data-schema` first to confirm available properties and values)
  - Expanded "Finding traces" section with step-by-step schema discovery workflow
  - Added examples of multiple AND-ed filters and person property filtering
  - Clarified the distinction between `$ai_*` properties (which should not be confirmed) and custom properties (which must be discovered)
  - Added references to the new query examples in the documentation
  - Improved formatting and clarity of filter examples

- **Updated `query-examples/SKILL.md`**: Minor formatting adjustment to description string.

## How did you test this code?

This is a documentation and reference material change. The SQL queries follow PostHog's HogQL syntax and the Jinja2 template uses the existing `render_hogql_example()` helper pattern. No automated tests are required for documentation updates.

## Docs update

No additional docs update needed—these changes are themselves documentation improvements to the PostHog AI skills system.

title: feat(mcp): allow listing tools by name
body:
## Problem

MCP server consumers (e.g. IDE extensions, automation scripts) sometimes need to expose only a specific set of individual tools rather than an entire feature category. The existing `features` param filters by category, but there was no way to allowlist by exact tool name.

## Changes

- Add a `tools` query parameter to the MCP server URL that acts as a tool-name allowlist (e.g. `?tools=dashboard-get,execute-sql`).
- When `features` and `tools` are both provided they compose as a **union (OR)** — a tool is exposed if it matches a feature category **or** its name is in the tools list. This lets callers pick a feature group and surgically add individual tools from other categories on top.

## How did you test this code?

- unit tests
- manual validation in CC

## Publish to changelog?

No.

## Docs update

No
author: VojtechBartos

reviews.body: Great, we should also update the docs [here](https://posthog.com/docs/model-context-protocol) to include the tool filtering :)

title: fix(insights): truncate long event and math names in editor panel
body:
## Problem

In the insight editor panel, long event names and math selector labels get hard-clipped without ellipsis, making them look broken. Short event names also get squeezed unnecessarily when the math label is long.

## Changes

- Fix `EntityFilterInfo` to use block-level truncation so `text-overflow: ellipsis` works through nested spans
- Add `max-w-full` to icon+text wrapper in `EntityFilterInfo` to constrain inline-flex content
- Add `min-width: 0` to `.ActionFilterRow__center` so flex items can shrink
- Make `MathSelector` use `fullWidth`, `truncate`, and `truncateText` for proper ellipsis
- Wrap math selector in a panel-scoped shrinkable container (`@min-[0px]/editor-panel` queries)
- Event name gets `min-w-36` in panel mode so it always shows a readable amount
- All panel-specific changes scoped via `editor-panel` container queries to avoid affecting the control view

## How did you test this code?

I'm an agent. No automated tests were run for this change — it's purely CSS/layout. The author verified visually in the browser during development.

👉 _Stay up-to-date with [PostHog coding conventions](https://posthog.com/docs/contribute/coding-conventions) for a smoother review._

## Publish to changelog?

All bot-raised inline comments are resolved with solid author explanations, snapshots are updated to match the new layout, and there are no showstoppers.

fix(insights): truncate long event and math names in editor panel

Event names and math selector labels were hard-clipped without ellipsis
in the panel editor. Fix EntityFilterInfo to use block truncation, add
max-w-full constraints, and make MathSelector truncate in narrow panels.

fullWidth was hardcoded in MathSelector, causing the inline events
math select to expand and overlap action buttons. Make fullWidth and
truncateText opt-in props, only passed from ActionFilterRow.

The previous change flipped the default from min-w-36 to min-w-0,

title: fix(sql-editor): back/forward with insights
body:
## Problem

https://posthog.slack.com/archives/C0ACRAMJUAG/p1774533598372539

Root cause was in the SQL editor URL rehydration  logic. The editor only reopened `#insight=...`,  `#view=...`, and `#draft=...` targets on first mount,  because those branches were gated on  `values.queryInput === null`. After switching to a  plain SQL query in the same mounted editor, browser  back/forward could restore a URL that still  contained `#insight=...` while the in-memory tab stayed as an unsaved query, which is why users saw "Save as insight" instead of "Update insight"

## Changes

Fix this. Compare the object the URL is pointing us towards at all point, not just on first mount.

## How did you test this code?

Added tests

## Publish to changelog?

no, just a bugfix

## Docs update

no, just a bugfix
author: mariusandra

reviews.body: Since I was around, LGTM 🚢

title: refactor(notifications): remove polling, move FF gate to view logic
body:
## Problem

The notifications frontend used polling as a fallback for SSE failures, generating constant `/notifications/unread_count` requests that hit Postgres. The backend used `PostHogFeatureFlagPermission` which returned 403 when the FF was off, causing silent failures and noise in error monitoring.

## Changes

- Remove all polling infrastructure from the frontend (`fallbackToPoll`, `pollTimer`, `UNREAD_POLL_TIMEOUT`, `notificationReceived` unread_count re-fetch, `toggleRead` unread_count re-fetch)
- Remove `PostHogFeatureFlagPermission` from the viewset — `list` returns empty results and `unread_count` returns 0 when the FF is off (200 instead of 403)
- Guard SSE connection with FF check in `startSSE` — if the flag is off, no API calls or SSE connections are made
- SSE uses a 3-strike abort strategy on error instead of falling back to polling
- Add `TestNotificationsAPIFeatureFlagDisabled` test class for FF-off behavior (2 tests)

## How did you test this code?

- 21 backend tests passing (13 existing + 6 cache + 2 new FF-disabled)

## Publish to changelog?

no
author: yasen-posthog

reviews.body: The PR removes the class-level `PostHogFeatureFlagPermission` and only adds per-method FF checks to `list` and `unread_count` — the write endpoints (`mark_all_read`, `mark_read`, `mark_unread`) are left completely unguarded by the feature flag after this change. This is an API permission model change with zero reviews.

All five API endpoints have per-method FF checks in the final diff — the bot comment claiming write endpoints are unguarded is factually incorrect. The behavioral change from 403 to 200-with-empty-payload is intentional and consistent across all methods, with proper test coverage added.

commits.message:
refactor(notifications): remove polling, move FF gate to view logic

- Remove all polling infrastructure (fallbackToPoll, pollTimer,
  UNREAD_POLL_TIMEOUT, notificationReceived unread_count re-fetch)
- Remove PostHogFeatureFlagPermission from the viewset — no more 403s
  when the FF is off. Instead, list returns empty results and
  unread_count returns 0
- Guard SSE connection with FF check on the frontend — if the flag is
  off, no API calls or SSE connections are made
- SSE uses 3-strike abort strategy on error instead of polling fallback
- Add tests for FF-disabled behavior

title: chore(llma): improve MCP traces list tool prompt, schema, and taxonomy
body:
## Problem

The MCP `query-traces-list` tool had an incomplete prompt, missing response fields, and several AI properties were absent from the taxonomy. This made the tool less useful for LLM consumers trying to understand trace data.

## Changes

- Renamed MCP tool from `query-traces-list` to `query-llm-traces-list` for consistency
- Moved tool description from inline YAML to a dedicated prompt file with comprehensive documentation
- Added missing response fields to the prompt: `aiSessionId`, `inputState`/`outputState`, `isSupportTrace`
- Added "Event types and their properties" section documenting which `$ai_` properties live on which event type (`$ai_generation`, `$ai_embedding`, `$ai_span`, `$ai_trace`, `$ai_metric`, `$ai_feedback`)
- Added `$ai_session_id`, `$ai_http_status`, `$ai_span_name` to the common filter properties list
- Documented that `$ai_is_error`/`$ai_error` may not appear via `read-data-schema`
- Added missing taxonomy entries: `$ai_is_error`, `$ai_error`, `$ai_error_type`, `$ai_error_normalized`, `$ai_trace_name`
- Switched `schema_ref` from `TracesQuery` to `AssistantTracesQuery`

## How did you test this code?

Reviewed prompt against query runner behavior and schema definitions to verify accuracy.

## Publish to changelog?

No

## 🤖 LLM context

Co-authored with Claude Code. Systematic review of prompt vs query runner (`traces_query_runner.py`) vs schema (`LLMTrace` in both Python and TypeScript) to identify discrepancies.

title: chore(modeling): fix possible race for failed row counts
body:
## Problem

<!-- Who are we building for, what are their needs, why is this important? -->

<!-- Does this fix an issue? Uncomment the line below with the issue ID to automatically close it when merged -->

<!-- Closes #ISSUE_ID -->

we still have some (very few) failed jobs that slip through with a non-zero materialized row count

## Changes

i think this is a race between preempting / cancelling a job and the row count update in the batch. if the job is retrieved (RUNNING) and then preempted / cancelled, and then the row count is updated by the batch, then we end up with a failed job with a row count until the next batch iteration catches that the job is no longer RUNNING.

title: feat(flags): paired schedule presets and edit UI for scheduled changes
body:
## Problem

Users scheduling recurring feature flag changes (e.g. "enable at 9am weekdays") often need a complementary schedule (e.g. "disable at 5pm weekdays") but must create each one manually. Additionally, there is no way to edit an existing scheduled change — users must delete and recreate.

## Changes

![2026-03-31 13 28 43](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/08f8353b-719a-477f-8586-d3733dad9115)


**Paired schedule presets** — the Repeats dropdown now includes a "Paired schedules" option group (Business hours, Weekdays only, Custom pair) when the operation is "Change status". Selecting a preset creates two complementary enable/disable cron schedules in one action. This is purely frontend — the backend sees two standard create calls.

**Edit scheduled changes** — each active schedule card now has an edit (pencil) button that opens a `LemonModal` pre-populated with the schedule's current values. Users can change the date/time, repeat interval or cron, end date, and enable/disable status. The edit logic lives in a separate `featureFlagScheduleEditLogic.ts` to isolate state from the creation form. Only changed fields are PATCHed.

**Shared `describeCron` helper** — extracted the duplicated cron-to-human-string logic into a single exported function used across all three files.

Key details:
- Paired presets: `business_hours` (enable 9am / disable 5pm Mon-Fri), `weekdays_only` (enable midnight Mon / disable end-of-day Fri), `custom_pair` (user-provided crons)
- Partial failure handling: if the enable schedule succeeds but the disable fails, a warning toast explains the situation
- Edit modal validates: future date for one-time schedules, 5-field cron syntax, end date after start
- Editing a paused schedule does not silently unpause it (uses derived is_recurring comparison)

## How did you test this code?

All 80 existing `featureFlagLogic` tests pass. TypeScript compiles without new errors. Frontend formatter passes. This is a frontend-only change — no backend modifications. I'm an agent and have not performed manual browser testing.

👉 _Stay up-to-date with [PostHog coding conventions](https://posthog.com/docs/contribute/coding-conventions) for a smoother review._

## Publish to changelog?

no

## Docs update

No docs update needed — this builds on the existing scheduled changes feature.

## 🤖 LLM context

This PR was co-authored with Claude Code. The implementation follows a 5-stage plan (paired presets logic → paired presets UI → edit logic → edit UI → polish). Code review feedback was incorporated before the PR: fixed a bug where editing a paused schedule would silently unpause it, and extracted a shared `describeCron` helper to eliminate 5x duplication.

title: feat(ingestion): add event filter evaluation step to ingestion pipeline
body:
## Problem

We need the Node.js ingestion pipeline to evaluate customer-configured event filters and drop (or count in dry run) matching events before they reach transformations.

Stacked on #52649 (migration PR) and `pl/pipelines/batching` (batching framework).

## Changes

Adds event filter evaluation to the Node.js ingestion pipeline:

- **`common/event-filters/`** module:
  - `schema.ts` — zod schemas for filter tree nodes and DB rows, with `EventFilterMode` (disabled/dry_run/live)
  - `evaluate.ts` — recursive tree evaluation with conservative empty-group handling (empty AND = false, not vacuous true), exhaustive operator check
  - `manager.ts` — `EventFilterManager` using `BackgroundRefresher` (60s) to load active filters from Postgres
  - `batch-app-metrics.ts` — `EventFiltersBatchAppMetrics` aggregates metrics per batch instead of producing one Kafka message per matched event, flushed via `queueMessages` at batch end
  - `test-helpers.ts` — shared tree-building helpers (`cond`, `and`, `or`, `not`) mirroring the Python test suite

- **`common/steps/event-filters-steps.ts`** — three pipeline steps:
  - `createEventFiltersBatchAppMetricsBeforeBatchStep` — creates batch metrics in beforeBatch hook, attaches to each element
  - `createApplyEventFiltersStep` — evaluates filters using event headers (runs before message parsing), increments batch metrics
  - `createFlushEventFiltersBatchAppMetricsStep` — afterBatch step that flushes aggregated metrics as a side effect

- **Wiring**: `EventFilterManager` created in `ingestion-consumer.ts`, batch metrics flow through `joined-ingestion-pipeline` (beforeBatch → batch context → afterBatch)

## How did you test this code?

95 automated tests across 4 test suites:

- **`event-filters-steps.test.ts`** (43 tests): step behavior (no filter, live drop, dry_run pass-through, metrics increment) plus all filter evaluation scenarios via `it.each` — condition matching, empty groups, boolean logic, complex trees including the Python `test_complex_tree_with_many_test_cases`
- **`batch-app-metrics.test.ts`** (7 tests): aggregation, flush via `queueMessages`, key separation, clearing after flush
- **`schema.test.ts`** (18 tests): zod validation for conditions, groups, nodes, rows — mirrors Python `TestValidateFilterTree`/`TestValidateCondition`/`TestValidateNodeStructure`
- **`manager.integration.test.ts`** (36 tests): end-to-end with real Postgres — loading, disabled/null/empty filters, multi-team, `treeHasConditions` guard, complex evaluation, plus 14 resilience tests for corrupt data (wrong types, unknown fields/operators, partial trees)

Tested on the PoC branch with live ingestion.

## Publish to changelog?

No

title: fix(phai): add query type reference to read_taxonomy and wrap validation errors
body:
## Problem

LLMs calling the `read_taxonomy` tool produce ~22 preventable validation errors per month (out of ~154K calls). Analysis of traces from the last 30 days shows three categories:

1. **Wrong field names (13 hits):** LLM sends `event` instead of `event_name`
2. **Wrong discriminator values (8 hits):** LLM sends class names like `ReadEvents` instead of `events`, or invented kinds like `person_properties`
3. **Other wrong fields (1 hit):** Missing `property_name`

These errors are recoverable (the LLM retries successfully), but waste tokens and add latency.

## Changes

- Added a "Query types" reference section to the tool description listing exact `kind` values and required field names, giving the LLM an unambiguous quick reference
- Wrapped `ValidationError` in `MaxToolRetryableError` in `_run_impl` so any remaining validation errors follow the established retry error pattern instead of propagating as raw exceptions

## How did you test this code?

- Added a test confirming `_run_impl` raises `MaxToolRetryableError` (not raw `ValidationError`) when given invalid input (e.g., `event` instead of `event_name`)
- All existing tests pass: `pytest ee/hogai/tools/read_taxonomy/test/ -x` (10 passed)

I'm an agent — I have not tested this manually beyond the automated test suite.

## Publish to changelog?

no

## 🤖 LLM context

Investigation started from PostHog trace `cd342414-5f98-4240-81b7-b0b553384413` which showed a `ValidationError` on the `read_taxonomy` tool. Used PostHog MCP SQL queries to analyze error frequency and categories across the last 30 days before designing the fix.
author: cvolzer3

fix(llma): add query type reference to read_taxonomy and wrap validation errors

LLMs calling the read_taxonomy tool sometimes use wrong field names
(e.g. `event` instead of `event_name`) or wrong discriminator values
(e.g. `ReadEvents` instead of `events`), causing ~22 preventable
validation errors per month. This adds an explicit query type reference
to the tool description and wraps ValidationError in MaxToolRetryableError.

title: fix(phai): prevent LLM from hallucinating `element` as top-level filter type
body: ## Problem The PostHog AI assistant was hallucinating `type: "element"` as a top-level filter in `filter_session_recordings`, causing a Pydantic `ValidationError` on first attempt. The `element` type is only valid as a nested property filter inside `events`-type filters for autocaptured interactions (`$autocapture`, `$rageclick`), not as a top-level entry in `filter_group.values`. Observed in trace `0e83611d-ec93-4f3a-9568-e94d56cf8d7c` — the LLM self-corrected on retry, but we want it to get it right on the first try. ## Changes - **`ee/hogai/tools/replay/filter_session_recordings.py`**: Added prompt guidance in the tool args description clarifying that `element` is not a top-level filter type, with the correct nested usage pattern and valid element property keys (`tag_name`, `text`, `href`, `selector`). - **`products/replay/backend/prompts.py`**: Added a concrete example under "Special Patterns" showing correct element/CSS text filtering via `$autocapture` events with nested `element`-type property filters. Includes guidance on when to explain limitations (text merely visible vs. interacted with). ## How did you test this code? This is a prompt-only change. Verified the f-string renders correctly (double-brace escaping). Pre-commit hooks (ruff lint + format) passed. Existing test suite has a pre-existing environment issue (`transformers` library conflict) unrelated to these changes. ## 🤖 LLM context Co-authored with Claude Opus 4.6. Investigated the failing trace via PostHog MCP tools (`query-traces-list`, `execute-sql`), identified the root cause in the schema (`MaxInnerUniversalFiltersGroup` vs `MaxRecordingEventFilter.properties`), and refined the prompt wording iteratively.
author: cvolzer3

commits.message:
fix(replay): prevent LLM from hallucinating `element` as top-level filter type

The AI assistant was using `type: "element"` as a top-level filter in
`filter_session_recordings`, causing a ValidationError. The `element`
type is only valid as a nested property filter inside `events`-type
filters for autocaptured interactions. Added prompt guidance and an
example to both the tool args description and the filter examples.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: feat(web-analytics): wrap bot preview functions as virtual event properties
body:
## Problem

Using those bot traffic functions is already possible: you can write custom HogQL expressions against user agent strings, or use the traffic type functions available in function previews. But that requires knowing HogQL syntax and where to look, which puts it out of reach for most Web analytics users who just want to filter or break down by "is this a bot?"

We want bot detection to be as easy as selecting any other event property with no HogQL knowledge required.

## Changes

Adds four virtual event properties that surface bot detection as first-class, selectable properties in the UI:

- `$virt_is_bot` (Boolean) — is this event from a bot/crawler/automation?
- `$virt_traffic_type` (String) — "Regular", "Bot", "AI Agent", or "Automation"
- `$virt_traffic_category` (String) — granular category (llm_crawler, search_crawler, seo_crawler, social_crawler, monitoring, http_client, headless_browser)
- `$virt_bot_name` (String) — display name ("Googlebot", "ChatGPT", "curl", etc.)

These are computed at query time using `multiMatchAnyIndex` against 96 bot patterns, registered as `ExpressionField` on the events table, and exposed in the taxonomic filter behind the `traffic-type-virtual-properties` feature flag (backend always computes).

## How did you test this code?

- Parameterized tests against 65 real-world user agent strings across 8 categories
- End-to-end ClickHouse integration tests (coalesce fallback, null/empty UA, bot filtering, traffic type grouping, cross-event-type verification)
- Snapshot tests for query compilation

## Publish to changelog?

No — behind feature flag, not yet GA.

title: fix(insights): fix tooltip positioning on bold number previous period link
body:
## Problem

The tooltip on the "previous period" link in BoldNumber insights wasn't showing. The wrapper `<span style={{ display: 'contents' }}>` had no bounding box, causing `getBoundingClientRect()` to return zeros and tooltip positioning to fail.

## Changes

- Changed the ref type from `React.RefObject<HTMLDivElement>` to `React.RefObject<HTMLElement | null>` so it can be used on both `div` and `Link` elements
- Removed the wrapper `<span>` with `display: contents` and applied the ref directly to the `<Link>` component
- This gives the ref a proper bounding box for tooltip positioning

## How did you test this code?

Manually verified that hovering the "previous period" link now shows the InsightTooltip with the previous period value.

Slack thread: https://posthog.slack.com/archives/C0ACRAMJUAG/p1775050764968779?thread_ts=1774977653.814709&cid=C0ACRAMJUAG

## LLM context

Follow-up fix to #53033. The `display: contents` CSS property makes an element have no box model, which broke the tooltip positioning that relies on `getBoundingClientRect()`.

https://claude.ai/code/session_015LBYzb8bfxVCZkG2XwkEmL
author: pauldambra

reviews.body: ## Pull request overview Fixes BoldNumber “previous period” tooltip positioning by ensuring the tooltip anchor element has a real bounding box for `getBoundingClientRect()`. **Changes:** - Generalized the tooltip anchor ref type to support non-`div` elements. - Removed the `display: contents` wrapper and attached the ref/hover handlers directly to the clickable “previous period” link. - Updated tooltip positioning math to use the new anchor ref. --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.

No showstoppers. This is a focused UI fix that moves a `ref` from a `display: contents` span (which yields empty `getBoundingClientRect()`) directly onto the `Link` element, and broadens the ref type accordingly — a clean, low-risk correction.

title: fix(test): mock scrollIntoView in VariableCalendar test
body:
<!-- mendral:session:action:01KN4GKWBHW4BPW8HMY2W4GG7C -->
## Summary

- Add `window.HTMLElement.prototype.scrollIntoView = jest.fn()` to `beforeEach` in `VariableCalendar.test.tsx`, fixing the "keeps include time on when reopened with a saved datetime value" test that times out at 5000ms.

## Problem

`LemonCalendarSelect` calls `element.scrollIntoView()` inside `useOnMountEffect` when `granularity='minute'` and a value is pre-selected. jsdom does not implement `scrollIntoView`, causing `userEvent.click` to hang indefinitely. This only affects the datetime test (not the date-only test) because `scrollToTime()` only targets time elements.

## Fix

Added the same `scrollIntoView` mock already used in `LemonCalendarSelect.test.tsx:73` to the `VariableCalendar.test.tsx` `beforeEach` block.

---

title: feat(revenue-analytics): Join persons on distinct id
body:
## Problem

Linking revenue analytics data to persons can be tricky. We're currently not enforcing a way to do the join, so users try do do their own way and end up having trouble.

Let's be more prescriptive by telling them how we should join the data. We'll standardize it by joining on `metadata.posthog_person_distinct_id` field, which should be passed over to Stripe.

Related to https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/issues/52270

title: fix(replay): route summarize-session child workflow to SESSION_REPLAY_TASK_QUEUE
body:
## Problem

After #52790 moved `summarize-session` from `VIDEO_EXPORT_TASK_QUEUE` to `SESSION_REPLAY_TASK_QUEUE`, the `video-segment-clustering` workflow fails with "Workflow class summarize-session is not registered on this worker" when starting `summarize-session` as a child workflow.

The clustering workflow calls `start_child_workflow("summarize-session", ...)` without specifying `task_queue`, so the child inherits the parent's queue (`VIDEO_EXPORT_TASK_QUEUE`) — but `summarize-session` is no longer registered there.

## Changes

Add explicit `task_queue=settings.SESSION_REPLAY_TASK_QUEUE` to the `start_child_workflow` call in `clustering_workflow.py`.

## How did you test this code?

Verified all three call sites for `"summarize-session"` now specify `SESSION_REPLAY_TASK_QUEUE`:
- `clustering_workflow.py` (this fix)
- `summarize_session.py:739` (already correct from #52790)
- `summarize_session.py:401` (workflow definition, not a caller)

## 🤖 LLM context

Hotfix for production error discovered immediately after deploying #52790. The clustering workflow was the only caller that didn't specify `task_queue` explicitly.

title: feat: Add OAuth Client ID Metadata Document (CIMD) support
body:
## Problem

OAuth clients like Claude Code and other MCP (Model Context Protocol) applications need a way to register dynamically without pre-registration or complex Dynamic Client Registration flows. The Client ID Metadata Document (CIMD) specification allows clients to use HTTPS URLs as their client_id, with the authorization server fetching client metadata (name, redirect URIs, logo) from that URL.

Some interesting resources around this are:

 1. https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/modelcontextprotocol/issues/991 — MCP GitHub issue discussing CIMD vs DCR trade-offs, implementation guidance,
  and concerns
 2. https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/modelcontextprotocol/pull/1296 — MCP PR implementing SEP-991 for CIMD support, including spec changes and
  security considerations
 3. https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-ietf-oauth-client-id-metadata-document-00 — The IETF draft spec itself (metadata document format, required
  fields, validation rules, caching, SSRF, size limits)
 4. https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/modelcontextprotocol/blob/main/docs/specification/draft/basic/authorization.mdx — Current MCP authorization spec
  showing CIMD as recommended approach, example metadata document, server-side validation steps
 5. https://claude.ai/oauth/claude-code-client-metadata — Claude Code's live CIMD metadata document (used to debug the localhost port and URL-in-path issues)

## Changes

- **Implemented CIMD support**: Added OAuth Client ID Metadata Document support per draft-ietf-oauth-client-id-metadata-document-00
- **Added CIMD database fields**: New fields on OAuthApplication model for `is_cimd_client`, `cimd_metadata_url`, `cimd_metadata_last_fetched`, and `logo_uri`
- **Enhanced OAuth validator**: Extended OAuthValidator to resolve CIMD URLs, fetch metadata documents, and create applications automatically
- **Improved localhost redirect handling**: Extended RFC 8252 loopback redirect URI handling to support `localhost` with ephemeral ports (not just 127.0.0.1 and ::1)
- **Added logo display**: OAuth authorization page now displays client logos when available
- **Removed deprecated API endpoint**: Removed `OAuthApplicationPublicMetadataViewSet` and related API since metadata is now injected into page context
- **Enhanced admin interface**: Added CIMD fields to Django admin with proper readonly handling
- **Added comprehensive caching**: CIMD metadata documents are cached with TTL based on HTTP Cache-Control headers
- **Added security validation**: SSRF protection and size limits for CIMD metadata fetching
- **Updated authorization server metadata**: Advertises CIMD support via `client_id_metadata_document_supported`

## How did you test this code?

You can easily test this works with CLaude Code pointing to your local server. You'll see it'll properly include the Claude Code name. It does NOT specify their logo, however, which is not amazing, but we can always add that manually in production to make it pretty!

## Publish to changelog?

No - this is infrastructure for OAuth client registration that doesn't directly impact end users.

## Docs update

This adds support for a draft OAuth specification that will primarily be used by MCP clients. We can specify in a possibly new API docs where we explain how our OAuth system works, including that we support CIMD, DCR and private client ids.
author: rafaeelaudibert

reviews.body:
### Review

**Critical**

1. **`posthog/api/oauth/cimd.py:141-162`** - `stream=True` + `response.content` defeats the streaming. The full response body gets pulled into memory before the size check runs, so a malicious CIMD server returning a multi-GB response will OOM the worker. The 5s timeout is per socket recv, not total transfer time, so a slow drip server can keep it going way longer. Use `response.iter_content(chunk_size=1024)` with a byte counter and `json.loads(body)` instead of `response.json()`.

2. **`posthog/api/oauth/cimd.py:265-330`** - No rate limiting on CIMD app creation. Every unique CIMD URL creates a new OAuthApplication row. An authenticated attacker can enumerate thousands of URLs and flood the DB. DCR has explicit throttling (`DCRBurstThrottle`: 5/min, `DCRSustainedThrottle`: 30/hour). CIMD should have something similar for the new-client path.

**Important**

3. **`posthog/api/oauth/cimd.py:88-100,141`** - Classic DNS rebinding SSRF. `validate_cimd_url` resolves DNS and checks the IP, then `requests.get()` does a second DNS resolution. Attacker DNS returns a public IP first (passes check), then a private IP (for the real request). Same pattern exists in `mcp_store/proxy.py` and others so this could be a follow-up to `is_url_allowed` itself, but worth flagging since CIMD lets any authenticated user trigger fetches to attacker-controlled URLs.

4. **`posthog/api/oauth/cimd.py:158-159`** - `int(content_length)` blows up with an unhandled `ValueError` if a server returns non-numeric `Content-Length` (e.g. `"abc"`). Either wrap in try/except or just drop the Content-Length check since the body-size check on line 162 is the actual enforcement.

5. **`posthog/models/oauth.py:152-155`** - Adding `verbose_name` to `is_dcr_client` is a migration-detectable change but migration 1068 doesn't include an `AlterField` for it. `makemigrations --check` will fail (Graphite bot already flagged this). Either regenerate the migration or revert the verbose_name.
Nice work, left some notes mostly on validation and security. Would be nice to have some feedback from the security folks too.
CIMD logic and image hardening LGTM

commits.message: feat: Add OAuth Client ID Metadata Document (CIMD) support
fix: move logo_uri to generic section
refactor: Simplify storybook by always including in context
refactor: bunch of fixes from agent review

fix: Fix throttling mechanism

Move it to the right place + add global throttling

fix: Remove useless `db_index`

Already specific as `null` so, not needed

fix: Avoid following redirects when evaluating CIMD

This is dangerous since someone could just redirect to an internal address which is no bueno.

We're also now validating the image URIs
fix: Guarantee we always refresh the cache when expired

refactor: Simplify caching/request coalescing behavior

We could previously get a bunch of conflicts and fire a lot of requests at the same time. Let's make that slightly harder by syncing the requests via celery
fix: Give more proper information in UI in case of errors
fix: Block `query` params and reduce code duplication

title: fix: set remote config flags to 100% rollout by default
body:
## Problem

Remote config flags were defaulting to 0% rollout instead of 100%, which doesn't make sense for configuration flags since they should typically be enabled for all users.

Closes #53073

## Changes

- Update FeatureFlagSerializer.create() to set remote config flags to 100% rollout when they have 0% or no rollout configured
- Add comprehensive tests for both scenarios:
  - Remote config flags created without filters
  - Remote config flags created with explicit 0% rollout

## How did you test this code?

Added two new test cases:
- `test_create_remote_config_flag_defaults_to_100_percent_rollout`: Tests flags created without filters
- `test_create_remote_config_flag_with_zero_rollout_gets_updated_to_100`: Tests flags created with explicit 0% rollout

Both tests verify that remote config flags end up with 100% rollout in the response and database.

title: fix(hogql): add range() and generate_series() table functions
body:
## Problem

HogQL lacks `range()` and `generate_series()` table functions which are commonly used in DuckDB/Postgres for generating integer sequences. Users writing HogQL queries that target these dialects have no way to generate series data, e.g. `SELECT * FROM range(100)`.

## Changes

- Add `RangeTable` and `GenerateSeriesTable` as `FunctionCallTable` subclasses with proper arg bounds (1-3 args)
- Register both in the database root so they're available in HogQL
- `to_printed_clickhouse` raises `QueryError` since these functions don't exist in ClickHouse
- `to_printed_postgres` added for the postgres dialect path
- Updated postgres printer to check for `to_printed_postgres` before falling back to `to_printed_clickhouse`

## How did you test this code?

Added 10 printer tests covering:
- ClickHouse dialect correctly rejects both functions
- Postgres dialect prints `range(...)` and `generate_series(...)` with 1, 2, and 3 args
- Arg validation: missing args, empty args, too many args

Manually verified queries like `SELECT * FROM range(100)` and `SELECT * FROM generate_series(1, 10)`.

## Publish to changelog?

no

## 🤖 LLM context

Agent-authored PR. Co-authored with Claude Code (Opus 4.6).
author: georgemunyoro

reviews.body: ## Pull request overview Adds HogQL support for `range()` and `generate_series()` table functions (common in DuckDB/Postgres) and updates the Postgres printer to prefer dialect-specific table printing, while ensuring ClickHouse rejects these functions. **Changes:** - Introduce `RangeTable` and `GenerateSeriesTable` as `FunctionCallTable` subclasses with arg bounds and dialect-specific printing behavior. - Register `range` and `generate_series` as root tables so they can be used in `FROM range(...)`/`FROM generate_series(...)`. - Update the Postgres printer to call `to_printed_postgres` when available and add printer tests for printing/errors. ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 4 out of 4 changed files in this pull request and generated 3 comments. | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | posthog/hogql/printer/test/test_printer.py | Adds printer tests for ClickHouse rejection and Postgres printing/arg-validation for the new table functions. | | posthog/hogql/printer/postgres.py | Prefers `to_printed_postgres()` for table printing in the Postgres dialect before falling back to ClickHouse printing. | | posthog/hogql/database/schema/duckdb_table_functions.py | Implements `RangeTable` and `GenerateSeriesTable` with dialect-specific printing and arg bounds. | | posthog/hogql/database/database.py | Registers `range` and `generate_series` in root tables. | --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.

commits.message:
fix(hogql): block range/generate_series in ClickHouse and add tests

range() and generate_series() are DuckDB/Postgres-only table functions.
to_printed_clickhouse now raises QueryError instead of emitting invalid
SQL. Added to_printed_postgres for the postgres printer path, and tests
for arg validation, ClickHouse rejection, and postgres output.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

chore: consolidate range/generate_series tests into parameterized groups

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: feat(workflows): allow mapping templates to hide event filter UX
body: ## Problem For mapping templates we always show an event filter for each mapping to allow it to apply to a subset of the overall hog function's event filter (which is always checked first [here](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/blob/bd347dc1513ea6f43af4ec7a60084f27a64112cd/nodejs/src/cdp/services/hog-executor.service.ts#L275)). This makes sense for most mappings use cases, as you'll want certain inputs to apply to certain event types ($identify will have different fields to send to the destination compared to $pageview for example) But for use cases like the new native push action, this is not really relevant as you will always want to match all top-level filtered events, and I believe this will confuse users settings this up, especially if they've never used destinations before: ## Changes Add an optional mapping flag to the template `use_all_events_by_default`. If set to true, we will just not show the event filters in the individual mapping sections (defaulting it to all events matching the top-level filters) ## How did you test this code? Ensured the UX hiding works as expected depending on flag value ## Publish to changelog? No

title: chore(replay): move session_summary to session_replay temporal module
body:
## Summary

- Move `session_summary/` from `posthog/temporal/ai/` to `posthog/temporal/session_replay/`, where it belongs alongside other session replay temporal code
- Swap the summarization child workflow from `VideoExportWorkflow` (Python/Playwright) to `RasterizeRecordingWorkflow` (Node.js rasterizer)
- Register session summary workflows on `SESSION_REPLAY_TASK_QUEUE` instead of `VIDEO_EXPORT_TASK_QUEUE`
- Move session summary tests to `posthog/temporal/tests/session_replay/session_summary/`

## Detail

The session summary workflows render session replay videos for Gemini analysis. They previously lived in `posthog/temporal/ai/` and ran on the video export task queue using `VideoExportWorkflow`. This PR:

1. **Moves code** — `session_summary/` → `posthog/temporal/session_replay/session_summary/` with `SESSION_SUMMARY_WORKFLOWS` and `SESSION_SUMMARY_ACTIVITIES` defined in `__init__.py`
2. **Swaps renderer** — replaces `export-video` child workflow with `rasterize-recording` (faster Node.js rasterizer, 30min timeout vs 3h)
3. **Strips export_context** — removes fields the rasterizer doesn't need (`timestamp`, `filename`, `duration`, `mode`)
4. **Updates cross-references** — `video_segment_clustering` (stays in `temporal/ai/`) has two imports from `session_summary` that are updated to the new path

`video_segment_clustering/` intentionally stays in `posthog/temporal/ai/` — it's not session replay code.

## Test plan

- [x] `pytest posthog/temporal/tests/session_replay/session_summary/ -x` — 48 passed
- [x] `pytest ee/hogai/session_summaries/tests/ -x` — 143 passed
- [x] Django import check passes (no circular imports)
- [ ] Verify rasterizer renders correctly on staging
author: TueHaulund

title: fix(llma): add LangChain message format support to normalizeMessage
body:
## Problem

The LLM analytics traces page floods the browser console with warnings and fires ~900k `llma message normalization failed` events per month. This happens because LangChain/LangGraph messages use `type` (human, ai, tool, system, context) instead of `role` as the discriminator, so every message falls through to the unsupported handler which fires `console.warn` + `posthog.capture` per message.

## Changes

- Add `isLangChainMessage` type guard recognizing messages with `type` in `[human, ai, tool, system, context]` and no `role` field
- Add LangChain handler that normalizes content, tool_calls, and tool_call_id
- Add `context` to `roleMap` (maps to `system`)
- Extract role from `type` field when `role` is absent
- Downgrade remaining fallback log from `console.warn` to `console.debug`
- Keep `posthog.capture` on fallback so we can track remaining unknown formats
- 11 new tests covering the type guard and all LangChain message types

## How did you test this code?

- All 100 tests pass in `products/llm_analytics/frontend/utils.test.ts` (89 existing + 11 new)
- Track error counts post-merge via this insight: https://us.posthog.com/project/2/insights/G3ahmI7j

## Publish to changelog?

No
author: andrewm4894

reviews.body: Medium-complexity change to a shared message normalization function with zero reviews and no CODEOWNERS match. The `tool`-type LangChain messages silently inherit `defaultRole` rather than a deterministic mapped role, which may misattribute roles depending on call site — warrants a quick human check.

title: fix(notifications): add Redis cache for unread_count endpoint
body:
## Problem

The `/notifications/unread_count/` endpoint hits Postgres on every call. It's polled every 30s as SSE fallback, and also re-fetched on every incoming SSE notification. With 100% rollout, this creates significant unnecessary database load since the count only changes when a notification is created or read state changes.

## Changes

- Add a `cache.py` module in `products/notifications/backend/` with per-user Redis caching for unread count (60s TTL, keyed by `user_id:organization_id`)
- Cache the computed count on miss in the `unread_count` endpoint
- Invalidate on `mark_read`, `mark_unread`, and `mark_all_read`
- Bulk-invalidate for all resolved recipients on notification creation (in `on_commit` alongside Kafka publish)

## How did you test this code?

Existing test suite passes (`products/notifications/backend/tests/` — 13 tests). 

## Publish to changelog?

no
author: yasen-posthog

reviews.body: Just a couple of minor comments

commits.message:
fix(notifications): add Redis cache for unread_count endpoint

The unread_count endpoint is polled frequently (every 30s as SSE
fallback, plus on every notification received) with no caching,
hitting Postgres with an annotated subquery each time. Add a 60s
Redis cache keyed by user+org, invalidated on notification creation,
mark_read, and mark_unread. mark_all_read sets the cache to 0
directly since the count is definitively zero after that operation.

title: fix(dagster): route DROP PART to leader replica in part breaker
body:
## Problem

The part breaker Dagster job fails at the final `DROP PART` step when running on non-leader replicas. `ALTER TABLE ... DROP PART` requires the shard leader, so it fails with `Code: 529. DROP PART cannot be done on this replica because it is not a leader`.

The INSERT SELECT, ATTACH, and replication all succeed, only the DROP fails. This leaves the old oversized part active alongside the new broken parts, wasting disk space until manually cleaned up.

## Changes

- Before executing `DROP PART`, check `is_leader` on `system.replicas`
- If the current replica is a leader, drop directly (existing behavior)
- If not a leader, route the DROP to an online (leader-eligible) replica on the same shard via `cluster.map_any_host_in_shards_by_role` with `Workload.ONLINE`

## How did you test this code?

- Confirmed leadership configurations across replicas
- Drops on leaders succeed

## Publish to changelog?

no

title: chore(flags): refactor mock setup
body:
## Problem

The feature-flags Rust crate has ~500 test data construction sites using positional factory functions like `create_test_flag(None, None, None, None, None, None, None, None)` and verbose 15-field struct literals.
These are fragile: adding a field to a struct breaks every manual construction site, and the positional `Option` parameters are unreadable.

## Changes

Introduces a `Mock` / `MockFrom` / `MockInto` trait system and a `mock!` macro that provides ergonomic, maintainable test data construction:

```rust
// Before: 8 positional Option params, 20+ line struct literal
let flag = create_test_flag(Some(1), Some(team.id), None, Some("key".to_string()),
    Some(FlagFilters { groups: vec![FlagPropertyGroup { properties: Some(vec![PropertyFilter {
        key: "email".to_string(), value: Some(json!("test@example.com")),
        operator: Some(OperatorType::Exact), prop_type: PropertyType::Person,
        group_type_index: None, negation: None,
    }]), rollout_percentage: Some(100.0), variant: None, ..Default::default() }],
    multivariate: None, aggregation_group_type_index: None, payloads: None,
    super_groups: None, holdout: None }), None, None, None);

// After: only specify what matters
let flag = mock!(FeatureFlag,
    id: 1, team_id: team.id, key: "key".mock_into(),
    filters: mock!(PropertyFilter, key: "email".mock_into(), value: Some(json!("test@example.com"))).mock_into()
);
```

### Trait system (`src/utils/mock.rs`)

- **`Mock`** — like `Default`, but for test contexts (non-zero IDs, 100% rollout, descriptive names)
- **`MockFrom<T>`** — by-value type conversion for test data (mirrors `std::convert::From`)
- **`MockInto<T>`** — blanket reciprocal (mirrors `std::convert::Into`)
- **`mock!` macro** — struct construction with field overrides via struct update syntax (`..base`)
- Identity impl, `&str` → `String`, `Option<String>` lifting, `PropertyFilter` → `FlagFilters`, `Vec<FeatureFlag>` → `FeatureFlagList`

### Mock impls (colocated with type definitions)

- `flag_models.rs`: FeatureFlag, FeatureFlagRow, FlagFilters, FlagPropertyGroup, Holdout, MultivariateFlagVariant, FeatureFlagList, MultivariateFlagOptions, EvaluationMetadata
- `property_models.rs`: PropertyFilter
- `cohort_models.rs`: Cohort

### Migration (net -1400 lines)

Migrated ~340 call sites across 10 test files, deleted 8 factory functions.

Deleted from `test_utils.rs`: `create_test_flag`, `create_test_flag_with_properties`, `create_test_flag_with_property`, `create_test_flag_that_depends_on_flag`

Deleted from `test_helpers.rs`: `create_simple_flag`, `create_simple_property_filter`, `create_simple_flag_filters`, `create_simple_flag_property_group`

## How did you test this code?



## Publish to changelog?

No

reviews.body: Great idea and nice cleanup! One non-blocking question/suggestion.

title: fix(revenue-analytics): Re-sync materialized views after Stripe source sync
body:
## Problem

When setting up Revenue analytics with Stripe source, we create the external data source, trigger the source sync in the background (temporal), and sync the materialized viewset. The materialized viewset syncs when the external data source tables are still not created, resulting in "empty" saved queries. This is fine, works as a protection layer. 

Revenue analytics materialized views will then be re-materialized on a schedule (default to 12h). The problem is that this re-materialization job only runs the saved queries and stores the results, it does not update the query definition.
This is bad, as we'll keep running the empty query and materializing no results.

The fix triggers an update to the saved queries definitions when the relevant external data sources finish syncing, making sure the saved queries are updated and we show date in Revenue analytics.

<!-- Who are we building for, what are their needs, why is this important? -->

title: chore(phai): analytics for successful/failed conversation turns
body:
## Problem

We need better visibility into the outcomes of PostHog AI conversation turns to understand success rates, failure modes, and cancellations. Currently, we're not capturing structured data about how conversation turns complete.

## Changes

Added PostHog analytics tracking at three key completion points in the Max thread logic:

1. **Cancellation tracking**: When a conversation turn is aborted (DOMException with AbortError), we now capture a 'cancelled' status event
2. **Error tracking**: When an exception occurs and is released, we capture a 'failure' status event with error details including:
   - HTTP status code (for API errors)
   - Error type classification (network_error, api_error, or unknown_error)
3. **Success/generation error tracking**: When the turn completes normally, we capture either 'success' or 'generation_error' status depending on whether any messages have error status

All events include consistent context: conversation_id, trace_id, agent_mode, and generation_attempt for correlation and debugging.

## How did you test this code?

Code review of the analytics instrumentation logic. The changes are additive analytics calls that don't affect the existing error handling or completion flow. Existing error handling and message finalization logic remains unchanged.

## Publish to changelog?

no

title: fix(replay): use blocklist for negative property filters in session recordings
body:
## Problem

High-traffic teams with >1M event-sessions have recordings incorrectly hidden by the internal users filter. 

The negative guard query (introduced in #43399) scans ALL event-sessions for the team and returns an allowlist of sessions passing negative conditions (e.g. "host is not internal IP"). With `LIMIT 1000000`, teams exceeding 1M event-sessions have sessions silently dropped from the allowlist, causing the `GlobalIn` check in the main query to exclude valid recordings.

Reported via support#48896 — affected team has 15.3M event-sessions in a 30-day window, with 23 of 24 recordings for one user incorrectly hidden.

## Changes

Flip from an **allowlist** to a **blocklist** approach for negative property filters:

**Before**: scan all events → `GROUP BY $session_id HAVING countIf(inverted) = 0` → return sessions that pass (huge result set, hits LIMIT)

**After**: `WHERE inverted_1 OR inverted_2 OR ...` → `GROUP BY $session_id` → return sessions that fail → main query uses `NOT GlobalIn` to exclude them (tiny result set, LIMIT is a genuine backstop)

The blocklist has selective WHERE conditions (specific IPs, test emails), so it touches far fewer rows and returns far fewer sessions. Semantically equivalent — a session is blocked if any event matches any positive (inverted) condition.

## 🤖 LLM context

Investigated a customer bug report where the internal users filter was hiding recordings for a non-internal user. Root cause: the negative guard query introduced in #43399 removed WHERE conditions (correctly, to fix per-event vs per-session semantics) but this made it scan all event-sessions, causing the 1M LIMIT to silently truncate results on high-traffic teams.
author: TueHaulund

reviews.body:
this is super clever!

it's easy to reverse if it causes a problem and should be much better

commits.message:
fix(replay): use blocklist for negative property filters in session recordings

The negative guard query scanned ALL event-sessions for the team and
returned an allowlist of sessions passing negative conditions (e.g.
"host is not internal IP"). With LIMIT 1000000, high-traffic teams
with >1M event-sessions had sessions silently dropped, causing the
internal users filter to incorrectly hide recordings.

Flip to a blocklist approach: find the small set of sessions that MATCH
the positive (inverted) form of negative filters, then exclude them
with NOT GlobalIn. The blocklist has selective WHERE conditions so the
result set is small and the LIMIT is a genuine backstop again.

Fixes support#48896 (team 224124, 15.3M event-sessions vs 1M LIMIT).
fix(replay): add type ignore for union-attr on prop.operator

title: fix(notifications): stop SSE infinite retry loop on connection failure
body:
## Problem

When the livestream SSE connection fails (service down, auth issue, or local dev without livestream running), `fetchEventSource` retries automatically because the `onerror` callback doesn't throw. This creates an infinite rapid-fire loop of failed requests that overwhelms the livestream service, floods the browser network tab, and contributes to the 504 spike seen after the 100% notifications rollout.

## Changes

- Throw from the `onError` callback after calling `fallbackToPoll()` so `fetchEventSource` stops retrying and the polling fallback takes over

## How did you test this code?

- Verified locally that SSE failure no longer produces a flood of requests in the network tab

## Publish to changelog?

no
author: yasen-posthog

reviews.body: Trivial one-line fix adding a thrown error in an SSE onError callback to stop the browser's built-in retry loop after falling back to polling. No production risk.

commits.message:
fix(notifications): stop SSE infinite retry loop on connection failure

fetchEventSource retries automatically unless onerror throws. Our
onError callback was just calling fallbackToPoll() without throwing,
so the library immediately retried the connection on failure, creating
an infinite rapid-fire loop of failed SSE requests that overwhelms
the livestream service and floods the browser network tab.

Throw after fallbackToPoll() to tell fetchEventSource to stop retrying
and let the polling fallback take over.

body:
## Problem

The new feature flags release conditions UI (`FeatureFlagReleaseConditionsCollapsible`) allows users to select behavioral cohorts from the cohort dropdown even when the `REALTIME_COHORT_FLAG_TARGETING` feature flag is disabled. The old UI correctly hides these cohorts via the `hideBehavioralCohorts` prop on `PropertyFilters`, but the new UI was missing this guard.

## Changes

Added the same `REALTIME_COHORT_FLAG_TARGETING` feature flag check to the new UI's `ConditionContent` component, passing `hideBehavioralCohorts={!realtimeCohortFlagTargeting}` to `PropertyFilters` — matching the old UI behavior exactly.

## How did you test this code?

- Verified TypeScript compiles without errors
- This is an agent-authored PR; manual testing has not been performed. The change mirrors the existing pattern in `FeatureFlagReleaseConditions.tsx` (line 387) identically.

## Publish to changelog?

No

Agent-authored fix. The behavioral cohort filtering flow is: `useFeatureFlag('REALTIME_COHORT_FLAG_TARGETING')` → `hideBehavioralCohorts` prop → `PropertyFilters` → `TaxonomicFilter` → `infiniteListLogic` adds `?hide_behavioral_cohorts=true` query param → backend excludes behavioral cohorts from the response.

title: feat: make sandbox not clobber worktrees (and a few other improvements)
body:
## Problem

When sandbox creation fails (e.g. migration errors), `sandbox destroy` is called which nukes the worktree, which makes it impossible to debug what went wrong. Additionally, the entrypoint runs migrations in the critical path, so a single migration failure crashes the container before you can get in.

## Changes

**Preserve worktrees by default**
- `sandbox destroy` no longer deletes the git worktree. Pass `--clean` to opt into deletion.
- `sandbox create` failure no longer calls `destroy` — leaves containers, volumes, worktree, and registry intact for debugging.
- `sandbox destroy` uses `-t 0` for instant container teardown instead of waiting for graceful shutdown.

**Move migrations to phrocs**
- Entrypoint no longer runs migrations — phrocs handles them like the normal dev stack.
- If migrations fail, you're already in mprocs looking at the error instead of staring at a crashed container.

**Attach to mprocs immediately on create/start**
- `sandbox create` and `sandbox start` drop you into the mprocs TUI as soon as the container boots, instead of blocking on a health check.
- Browser opens in the background when the health check passes.

Add "rm" alias for "delete". Make "rm" and "ls" default. 

## How did you test this code?

Tested `sandbox create`, `sandbox destroy`, `sandbox start`, and `sandbox shell` on a branch with a known migration failure. Verified worktree preservation, mprocs attachment, and background browser open.
author: aspicer

commits.message: fix(sandbox): preserve worktrees by default, add --clean to destroy

title: feat(personhog): add graceful drain protocol for pod shutdown
body:
## Problem

When a personhog pod receives a SIGTERM (e.g. during a rollout or downscale), it revokes its lease and exits immediately. This causes the coordinator to treat it as a crash, and partitions sit unassigned until the next rebalance cycle detects the missing pod.

## Changes

- Add a `drain_timeout` field to `PodConfig` (default 30s) that controls how long a pod waits for partitions to drain before force-exiting
- Restructure `PodHandle::run()` into two phases: normal operation (watch handoffs/assignments) and drain (on cancellation, transition to `Draining` status and keep processing handoff events)
- Keep the heartbeat alive during drain so the coordinator sees a `Draining` pod rather than a crashed one with an expired lease
- Add a `graceful_drain_transfers_partitions` integration test that simulates SIGTERM on a pod and verifies all partitions are handed off to the surviving pod before exit

title: fix(auth): preserve session during social auth re-authentication
body:
## Problem

When a user triggers re-authentication via the re-auth modal and clicks Google (social auth), `sso_login()` unconditionally calls `request.session.flush()`. This destroys the user's authenticated session before the OAuth redirect, causing 401 errors on in-flight requests (e.g. pinned tabs, PostHog recorder) and breaking the OAuth return redirect — the user ends up on the login screen instead of being re-authenticated in place.

## Changes

- Skip `request.session.flush()` in `sso_login()` when `reauth=true` query param is present — during re-authentication the user is already authenticated and session fixation prevention is unnecessary
- Add tests verifying session is flushed for regular login but preserved for re-auth

## How did you test this code?

- 2 unit tests added (`TestSSOLoginSessionHandling`)

## Publish to changelog?

no
author: yasen-posthog

commits.message:
fix(auth): preserve session during social auth re-authentication

When the re-auth modal triggers a social auth flow (e.g. Google),
`sso_login()` was unconditionally calling `request.session.flush()`.
This destroyed the user's authenticated session before the OAuth
redirect, causing 401 errors on in-flight requests and breaking the
return redirect after the OAuth callback.

Skip the session flush when `reauth=true` is present — during
re-authentication the user is already authenticated and we only need
to confirm their identity, not prevent session fixation.

fix(auth): require authenticated user for reauth session preservation

Add `request.user.is_authenticated` guard so unauthenticated requests
with `?reauth=true` still get the session flushed (defense-in-depth).
fix(auth): fix test to capture anonymous session key after logout

title: feat(actions): preview matching events for unsaved actions
body:
## Problem

When creating a new action, users can't see which events match their action definition until after saving. The "Matching events" section is gated by the action having an `id`, because the `EventsQuery` requires a saved `actionId` to look up step definitions from the database. 

The backend doesn't actually need a saved action; it just reads the step definitions and converts them to HogQL WHERE clauses.

Additionally, when editing an existing action's steps, the matching events preview still shows results for the saved version, not the in-progress edits.

## Changes

- Add `EventsQueryActionStep` schema type and `actionSteps` field to `EventsQuery`. This allows the frontend to pass step definitions inline.
- Handle `actionSteps` in `EventsQueryRunner` converts schema step objects to `ActionStepJSON` and calls `steps_to_expr`. Ignored if `actionId` is also set.

## How did you test this code?

- new backend tests
- verified frontend manually

No

No docs update needed — this is an internal schema addition.

## 🤖 LLM context

This PR was authored by Claude Code (claude-opus-4-6). The implementation follows a plan developed in plan mode to add live event preview for unsaved action definitions.

title: fix(cdp): reduce memory pressure in cyclotron worker kafka job queue
body:
### Problem
                                                                                                                 
When the worker queues jobs to Kafka, it fires off all 1000 produces in parallel. Each produce waits for WarpStream to confirm it received the message. While waiting, the code holds onto the full job data (event payloads, person properties, VM state) because the function closures reference it. When WarpStream is slow, the next batch arrives before the previous one is done. Now you have two batches of heavy objects in memory. Then three. Then four. This keeps going until the pod runs out of memory.

### Fix                                                                                                            
  
1. We serialize the jobs into JSON strings first, then throw away the original heavy objects before starting the produces. The parallel produces still happen at the same speed, but the closures now only hold flat strings instead of deeply nested objects. Once the JSON strings are produced, they get cleaned up normally.            
                                                            
2. Also fixed a smaller issue where filtering jobs was copying the entire array on every iteration instead of just appending to it.
author: meikelmosby

commits.message:
fix(cdp): reduce memory pressure in cyclotron worker kafka job queue

The cdp-cyclotron-worker-hog pods show linear memory growth to OOM (~8GB)
over ~3 hours. Pyroscope profiles show 48% of in-use objects come from
`queueInvocations` — the produce closures capture full invocation objects
(globals, vmState, person properties) and hold them until the broker acks.
Under WarpStream backpressure, multiple batches of these heavy objects
stack up faster than GC can reclaim them.

Fix by serializing invocations to lightweight JSON strings upfront, then
releasing the original objects before producing. The produce closures now
only reference flat strings instead of deep nested objects. Produces still
run in parallel via Promise.all so throughput is unchanged.

Also replaces an O(n²) array spread in queueInvocationResults with a
simple push loop.

fix(cdp): remove invocations.length = 0 mutation

The array clearing was both unnecessary (no closure in Promise.all
captures the original invocations after the synchronous .map()) and
a contract violation for callers holding a reference to the array.
